Section 1

Practice Philosophy, Core Standards & Professional Expectations

Applies to every employee of Mir Neurology & Spine Center regardless of role or department. Understanding and following Section 1 is required before beginning independent work.

🎯
1.1–1.3 — Mission, Purpose & Core Values
▼
1.1 Purpose of This Manual

This manual establishes the standards, workflows, and expectations for all employees of Mir Neurology & Spine Center. It serves as the official training guide for:

  • Front Desk
  • Medical Assistants (Onsite & Virtual)
  • Scheduling
  • Back Office
  • Prior Authorization
  • Referral Coordinators
  • Cognitive Assessment Team
  • CCM Team
  • Billing Staff
  • Managers and Supervisors
Every employee is expected to understand and follow the policies in this manual.
1.2 Mission
"To provide outstanding neurological care by combining clinical excellence, compassionate service, efficient operations, and continuous quality improvement."
1.3 Core Values

Every decision should support one or more of the following values:

🛡️
Patient Safety
✅
Accuracy
💙
Compassion
👔
Professionalism
🤝
Accountability
👥
Teamwork
⚡
Efficiency
📈
Continuous Improvement
🌟
Integrity
🏥
1.4–1.5 — Patient-First Philosophy & Accuracy Standards Non-Negotiable
▼
1.4 Patient First Philosophy

Every employee should ask before making a decision:

Ask These Questions First
  • Is this best for the patient?
  • Will this improve patient care?
  • Will this reduce delays?
  • Will this improve the patient's experience?
  • Will this reduce unnecessary cost?
  • Will this improve safety?
If the Answer is No

Reconsider the process. Every workflow, every shortcut, every communication method should be evaluated through the lens of patient benefit. If a process doesn't serve the patient, it needs to change.

1.5 Accuracy is Non-Negotiable
Neurology is a specialty where small mistakes can have serious consequences.

Examples of accuracy failures that cause patient harm:

  • Missing laboratory tests
  • Incorrect medication doses
  • Wrong ICD-10 codes
  • Missing diagnoses
  • Wrong referrals
  • Missing prior authorizations
  • Incorrect MRI orders
  • Missing consultation notes
  • Incorrect medication quantities
Policy: Accuracy is always more important than speed. Every employee is expected to double-check their work before considering it complete.
✅
1.6 — Double-Check Standard by Role
▼

Every employee must review their work before completing any task. The checklist below defines what each role must verify.

🩺 Medical Assistants — Must Verify
  • Orders
  • Diagnoses
  • Medications
  • Referrals
  • Imaging
  • Laboratory requests
  • Documentation
🗂 Front Desk — Must Verify
  • Demographics
  • Insurance
  • Prescription insurance
  • Next of Kin
  • Care Team
  • Appointment information
📅 Scheduling — Must Verify
  • Appointment type
  • Provider
  • Location
  • Testing
  • Follow-up interval
📋 Prior Authorization — Must Verify
  • Medication
  • Diagnosis
  • Documentation
  • Expiration dates
⏱️
1.7–1.9 — Provider Efficiency, Patient Safety & Customer Service
▼
1.7 Provider Time is Valuable

Provider time is the most valuable clinical resource in the practice. Every employee should work to maximize provider efficiency.

✅ DO
  • Prepare charts before clinic
  • Have the next patient ready
  • Verify orders
  • Complete paperwork beforehand
  • Obtain outside records
  • Organize referrals
  • Complete routine forms before provider review
❌ DO NOT
  • Interrupt unnecessarily
  • Wait until clinic to clarify routine issues
  • Ask providers to complete forms that staff should prepare
  • Make providers search for missing information
1.8 Patient Safety Comes Before Convenience
Every employee is responsible for protecting patient safety. The following are patient safety issues — not simply workflow issues:
  • Missing abnormal laboratory results
  • Missing MRI findings
  • Incorrect medication dose
  • Wrong referral
  • Missing follow-up
  • Incorrect diagnosis
  • Delayed prior authorization
1.9 Customer Service Standards

Patients judge the practice by every interaction.

Every employee should:
  • Be courteous
  • Be respectful
  • Listen carefully
  • Resolve problems
  • Follow through
  • Keep patients informed
Language Standards
❌ Avoid:
"I don't know."  |  "I have to ask someone."  |  "Someone will call you."
✅ Instead say:
"I'll look into that now."  |  "I'll follow up and update you."
🤝
1.10–1.12 — Communication, Teamwork & Ownership Mentality
▼
1.10 Communication Standards

Communication should be:

Timely
Accurate
Professional
Complete
Documented
Important clinical information should never rely on memory alone.
1.11 Teamwork

Every department supports every other department. Problems should be solved together rather than transferred between departments.

  • Providers
  • Medical Assistants
  • Front Desk
  • Scheduling
  • Back Office
  • Prior Authorization
  • Billing
  • Cognitive Assessment
  • CCM
  • Management
1.12 Ownership Mentality
Every employee should think like an owner. Do not assume "someone else will take care of it."
Instead, ask:
  • Has this actually been completed?
  • Is the patient taken care of?
  • Does someone need to follow up?
🔁
1.13–1.16 — Close the Loop, Accountability & Standardization
▼
1.13 Close the Loop — Every Task Must Reach Completion
Never leave loose ends. Every task should be followed until completion.
Referral Loop
Ordered → Sent → Received → Appointment Scheduled → Patient Seen → Consultation Note Received → Provider Reviewed
Laboratory Loop
Ordered → Completed → Reviewed → Patient Notified → Treatment Started → Follow-up Completed
1.14 Continuous Improvement

Every mistake should improve the system. When an error occurs, ask:

  • Why did this happen?
  • How do we prevent it?
  • Can we improve the workflow?
  • Does the manual need updating?
  • Should staff receive additional training?
The goal is to improve the process — not simply correct one mistake.
1.15 Accountability
"No one knew" is not an acceptable answer.

Every task must have an owner. For every activity we should know:

  • Who is responsible?
  • When is it due?
  • What is the current status?
  • Has it been completed?
1.16 Standardization

Every employee should perform the same task the same way. Standardized processes reduce errors and improve efficiency.

  • Patient check-in
  • Prior authorization
  • Referrals
  • Laboratory orders
  • Medication refills
  • Forms
  • Imaging
  • Documentation
📝
1.17–1.19 — Documentation, Financial Stewardship & Quality
▼
1.17 Documentation Standard
If it is not documented, it is considered not done.

Always document:

  • Provider communication
  • Patient communication
  • Laboratory review
  • Referral status
  • Prior authorization status
  • Medication discussions
  • Abnormal results
  • Follow-up plans
1.18 Patient Financial Stewardship

Our responsibility extends beyond medical care. We should help patients avoid unnecessary financial hardship by:

  • Verifying insurance participation before referrals
  • Using covered ICD-10 diagnoses when appropriate and supported by the clinical record
  • Confirming laboratory participation with the patient's insurance when possible
  • Checking medication formularies before prescribing high-cost medications
  • Initiating prior authorizations promptly
  • Assisting patients with manufacturer assistance programs when appropriate
Good clinical care includes helping patients receive affordable care whenever possible.
1.19 Quality Philosophy

We measure quality by:

  • Patient outcomes
  • Patient safety
  • Accuracy
  • Timeliness
  • Customer satisfaction
  • Communication
  • Teamwork
  • Continuous improvement
Every employee contributes to quality.
🌟
1.20 — Professional Expectations
▼

Every employee is expected to:

  • Arrive prepared
  • Continue learning
  • Accept feedback professionally
  • Follow established workflows
  • Ask questions when unsure
  • Report problems early
  • Respect coworkers
  • Maintain confidentiality
  • Take pride in their work
  • Strive for excellence every day
📋

  • How your role affects patient safety
  • Why every task requires follow-up until completion

  • How to contribute to continuous quality improvement

📖
How to Use This Manual
▼
🆕 New Hire Onboarding

Complete the Section 1 checklist first. Then read your department tab fully before starting. Work through each section with your supervisor.

📅 Morning Meetings

Use the Daily Checklists tab each morning. Open your department tab to review any protocols relevant to the day's schedule.

🔍 Quality Reviews

Reference the Quality Improvement tab for audit checklists. Use the search bar to quickly find any specific topic or rule.

About Mir Neurology & Spine Center

Learn who we are, what drives our practice, and the providers and locations that serve our patients. This is the foundation every staff member should know — our story, our mission, and the community we serve.

All StaffPractice OverviewProvidersLocations
🏛️
Who We Are
▼

Mir Neurology & Spine Center

Board-Certified Neurological Care · Hagerstown & Cumberland, MD

Mir Neurology Center was established by Dr. Sarim Mir, a board-certified neurologist with more than three decades of experience in neurological care. After graduating from King Edward Medical University in 1987 and completing advanced training in clinical neurophysiology, Dr. Mir built a practice designed to deliver high-level neurology care locally — instead of forcing patients to travel long distances for specialized evaluation and treatment.

Today, Mir Neurology serves patients from Washington County, Allegany County, and surrounding communities with a growing team of dedicated providers. Our practice combines neurological expertise with a patient-first philosophy that emphasizes listening carefully, explaining clearly, and building treatment plans that fit each person's real-life needs.

🎯
Our Mission
▼

"Our mission is to provide neurological care that is clinically excellent, compassionate, and accessible. Every patient deserves a thorough evaluation, a clear diagnosis, and a treatment plan that supports both symptom relief and long-term quality of life."

— Mir Neurology Mission Statement

We believe neurology care should feel personal, not rushed, and that families deserve guidance they can trust through every stage of treatment. That mission shapes the way we practice every day — from first appointments to diagnostic testing to follow-up visits — our focus is on making complex neurological care easier to navigate for patients and caregivers alike.

🔬
Clinically Excellent

Thorough evaluations and evidence-based treatment

🤝
Compassionate

Patient-first care with clear communication

📍
Accessible

Local specialty care, most major insurances accepted

⭐
What Makes Mir Neurology Different
▼

Patients choose Mir Neurology because they want more than quick visits and disconnected referrals. Our board-certified neurologists bring decades of experience to conditions such as epilepsy, stroke, Parkinson's disease, tremor, memory disorders, migraines, neuropathy, and other neurological concerns. We combine the depth of specialty care with the reassurance of a local practice that knows its community.

Advanced Diagnostic Services
  • EEG testing (epilepsy, seizure evaluation)
  • EMG & nerve conduction studies (neuropathy, nerve injury)
  • Cognitive assessment (memory disorders, dementia screening)
  • Medical Botox for qualifying neurological conditions
Accessible & Community-Focused
  • Accepts Medicare, Medicaid, and most major insurance plans
  • Two office locations serving western Maryland
  • Key testing and treatment services integrated into the practice
  • Patients stay closer to home for specialized neurological care
👨‍⚕️

🗂 Front Desk Training

Section 2 — Official training guide for all Front Desk staff. The Front Desk is the patient's first and last point of contact with the practice. Responsible for accurate registration, insurance verification, payment collection, forms management, patient flow, and excellent customer service at every visit.

RegistrationInsurance VerificationPatient Check-InPayment CollectionForms TrackingCustomer ServiceBreeze Check-InMVA Intake
✅
Patient Visit Checklist — Front Desk Responsibilities (Every Visit)
▼
Complete all applicable items for every patient at check-in and check-out. New patient items are required at first visit; follow-up items apply at every subsequent visit. Reset the checklist for each new patient.
🆕 New Patient — Required at First Visit
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    HIPAA Form — signed and scanned into chart
  • ✓
    New Patient Intake Form — completed in full
  • ✓
    Insurance Card — scanned (front and back)
  • ✓
    Photo ID — scanned and attached to chart
  • ✓
    Picture of the Patient — taken and uploaded to chart
  • ✓
    Next of Kin — name, relationship, and contact number recorded
  • ✓
    Guarantor Info — confirmed and entered in EMR
  • ✓
    Controlled Medication Agreement Form — completed and signed if applicable
🔄 Every Visit — New & Follow-Up
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Balance — pull and review patient's outstanding balance
  • ✓
    Balance Collected — collect applicable copay, deductible, or balance; document payment
  • ✓
    Notes — review any account notes or flags before the patient is seen
  • ✓
    Portal Activation — confirm patient is enrolled; enroll at check-in if not yet activated
  • ✓
    Adding Care Team — confirm all current providers (PCP + specialists) are listed; update as needed
  • ✓
    F/U Appointment Made & Notified — follow-up scheduled before patient leaves; patient confirmed of date/time
  • ✓
    LAB / Imaging Orders Printed & Patient Educated — orders printed, handed to patient, and patient verbally educated on where to go and what to expect
  • ✓
    NCS / EMG Table — if NCS/EMG is scheduled, confirm patient demographics have been entered into the EMG computer before the appointment
Reset before each new patient. All items must be completed or marked N/A with a reason documented. If you are unable to complete an item, notify your supervisor — do not skip without documentation.
📋
Core Responsibilities
▼
The Front Desk is the coordination center of the practice — accurate registration, excellent communication, and efficient patient flow set the foundation for safe, high-quality care.
Registration & Intake
  • Patient check-in and check-out
  • Demographic verification and updates
  • Insurance and prescription insurance verification
  • Scan insurance and prescription insurance cards
  • Care Team updates at every visit
  • Next of Kin documentation
  • Guarantor information verification
Patient Flow & Coordination
  • Appointment scheduling per provider instructions
  • Payment collection (copays, deductibles, balances)
  • Forms intake, tracking, and routing
  • Patient education material distribution
  • PT and brace order distribution at check-out
  • Breeze Online Check-In review and processing
Communication Responsibilities
  • Clear, professional communication with patients at all stages
  • Notify MAs of patient arrival, insurance issues, or missing items
  • Coordinate workflow between Front Desk, MAs, scheduling, and providers
  • Answer phones professionally and minimize unnecessary transfers
  • Relay urgent messages to clinical staff immediately
Scheduling Focus Areas
  • Controlled substance follow-ups
  • Parkinson disease follow-ups
  • Cognitive Assessment appointments
  • EMG appointments
  • Botox appointments
  • Small fiber biopsy appointments
🪪
Patient Check-In Workflow — 6-Step Process
▼
Follow all 6 steps for every patient at check-in. Steps apply to new and returning patients unless otherwise noted.
Step 1 — Verify Breeze Online Check-In
If Completed via Breeze
  • Review the submitted information
  • Update only changed information
  • Do not ask the patient to complete duplicate paperwork
If Not Completed
  • Provide appropriate registration forms
  • Encourage the patient to complete Breeze before their next visit
Step 2 — Verify Demographics

Confirm and update immediately:

  • Full name
  • Date of birth
  • Address
  • Home phone
  • Mobile phone
  • Email address
  • Preferred pharmacy
Step 3 — Verify Insurance
Confirm
  • Primary insurance
  • Secondary insurance
  • Medicare vs Medicare Advantage distinction
  • Effective dates
Scan
  • Insurance cards (front and back)
  • Prescription insurance cards
Step 4 — Verify Next of Kin

Every patient should have: emergency contact name, relationship, and phone number. If missing, obtain before completing check-in whenever possible.

Step 5 — Verify Care Team

Update the Care Team with all regularly treating providers — especially important for CCM patients. Examples: PCP, Cardiology, Nephrology, Rheumatology, Pain Management, Psychiatry, Neurosurgery, Orthopedics, Endocrinology, Pulmonology.

Step 6 — Verify Prescription Coverage
Always ask: "Do you have a prescription insurance card?" Scan it into the chart. Do not assume Medicare Part B covers medications. Different Medicare Part D plans have different formularies.
📲
Breeze Online Check-In
▼

Encourage eligible patients to complete Breeze before their appointment.

Benefits for Staff
  • Less paperwork to process at check-in
  • More accurate demographics already in the system
  • Reduced staff workload during busy clinic hours
Benefits for Patients
  • Shorter wait times at check-in
  • Better satisfaction — less time filling out forms
  • Faster intake process
When a patient completes Breeze, review the submitted information and update only what has changed. Never ask the patient to redo paperwork they already completed online.
💳
Payment Collection
▼
Collect at Check-In
  • Copays
  • Deductibles
  • Outstanding balances (per office policy)
If Patient Cannot Pay
  • Offer payment plan options when applicable
  • Document refusal or inability to pay
  • Do not embarrass the patient — handle with discretion
All payments must be documented in the system. Never skip payment collection without documenting the reason. End-of-day reconciliation depends on accurate collection records.
📄
Forms Workflow, Tracking & Patient Assistance Paperwork
▼
Common Form Types
  • FMLA
  • Disability
  • DMV
  • Medication Assistance
  • Work Notes
  • School Forms
When a Form is Received — 8-Step Process
  1. Record the date received
  2. Collect the fee (if applicable) — do not charge Medical Assistance patients if exempt under office policy
  3. Scan the form into the chart
  4. Enter into the Forms Tracking Sheet
  5. Route to the assigned MA
  6. Inform the patient of the standard turnaround time: approximately two weeks unless otherwise directed
  7. Update the tracking sheet at each status change until completed
  8. Notify the patient when the form is ready for pickup or fax
Forms Tracking Sheet — Required Fields
  • Patient name
  • Type of form
  • Date received
  • Fee collected
  • MA assigned
  • Provider
  • Date completed
  • Date faxed
  • Date picked up
  • Current status
The Front Desk must always be able to answer patient questions about form status using this tracking sheet. "I'm not sure" is not an acceptable response.
Medication Assistance / Patient Assistance Paperwork
  • Record date received
  • Scan into the chart immediately
  • Enter into the tracking sheet
  • Route to the assigned MA
  • Document who received it
  • Track until fully resolved
Never leave paperwork on a desk without documentation. Every piece of paper that enters the office must be logged.
🪑
Waiting Room Communication Important
▼
2.3 Patient Greeting Standards — Every Interaction
Always
  • Smile and acknowledge the patient immediately
  • Make eye contact
  • Introduce yourself if appropriate
  • Be courteous and respectful
  • Listen carefully and fully
  • Resolve issues whenever possible
Never
  • Ignore a patient at the desk or in the waiting area
  • Speak negatively about coworkers
  • Say "I don't know" — say "I'll find out for you"
  • Transfer calls unnecessarily
  • Ask patients to repeat information already in the chart
Waiting Room Updates — When Running Behind
Problem IdentifiedPatients reported waiting long periods without any updates when providers were running behind. This created frustration and complaints, and patients felt ignored.
🚫Never allow patients to sit in the waiting room without updates for more than 20–30 minutes.
Expectations
  • Proactively update every waiting patient every 20–30 minutes
  • Do not wait for patients to ask — approach them first
  • If the delay is unexpected or long, offer water or other comfort
  • If a patient chooses to leave, document it and notify the provider
Approved Communication Phrases
Running Behind"The provider is running a little behind today. We appreciate your patience and will get you in as soon as possible."
Time Estimate Available"We expect it will be approximately ___ more minutes. Thank you for waiting with us."
General Check-In"I just wanted to check in — we haven't forgotten you. We'll have you in shortly."
Training Points
  • Communication dramatically reduces patient frustration even when waits are long
  • Patients who feel acknowledged are far less likely to complain or leave
  • Silence sends the message that no one cares — always communicate
2.14 Customer Service Recovery — When Something Goes Wrong
  1. Listen completely — do not interrupt or become defensive
  2. Apologize for the inconvenience — even if it was not your fault
  3. Take ownership — do not redirect blame to another department
  4. Find a solution — escalate to a supervisor if needed
  5. Update the patient — follow through and confirm resolution
Many patients never complain — they simply do not return. Every interaction matters. A single recovery moment can rebuild trust completely.
🚗
Motor Vehicle Accident (MVA) Intake Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedMVA visits were missing critical billing and legal documentation at intake, causing delays in payment and gaps in records for legal proceedings.
Required Information — Collect Every Item for Every MVA Visit
Insurance & Claim
  • Claim number (auto insurance claim)
  • Insurance carrier name
  • Policy number
  • Effective date of coverage
Legal & Adjusters
  • Adjuster name and contact information
  • Attorney name and firm (if applicable)
  • Attorney phone and fax numbers
  • Date of accident
⚠️If any of this information is missing, do not skip it. Follow up with the patient before they leave or call back the same day. Incomplete MVA intake causes billing failures.
Training Points
  • MVA cases are billed differently from routine insurance — missing info can result in zero reimbursement
  • Legal cases may require documentation months later — collect everything now
  • Always separate MVA visits from routine neurology visits during scheduling when possible
📋
Workers' Compensation Intake Form Print & Complete
▼
⚠️Complete this form for every Workers' Compensation visit. All fields must be filled before the patient leaves. Missing information causes billing failures and legal documentation gaps.

WORKERS' COMPENSATION INTAKE FORM
Mir Neurology & Spine Center · (301) 797-7600
Patient Information
Workers' Compensation Insurance Information
Insurance Adjuster Information
★ If Applicable — Attorney Information
🧲
MRI & Imaging Order Tracking Important
▼
Problem IdentifiedImaging centers reported not receiving orders. Patients arrived for MRIs that were never received, and providers did not have results available at follow-up visits.
Expectations — Track Every MRI Order Through 4 Stages
  1. Order sent to imaging center — confirm transmission
  2. Imaging center confirmed receipt — call if not confirmed within 24 hours
  3. Patient appointment scheduled — document date and location
  4. Results received and available in chart — follow up after appointment date
Training Points
  • Never assume an order went through — always confirm
  • If the imaging center does not confirm receipt in 24 hours, call them directly
  • Document every step in the patient's chart as it happens
  • Providers need imaging results before follow-up visits — timing matters
📁
Document Scanning Standards Important
▼
Problem IdentifiedDocuments were scanned under incorrect chart tabs, using scan dates instead of the Date of Service, and using inconsistent or duplicated category names. This delayed clinical review and caused provider frustration.
Standard Document Categories — Use These Exactly
  • Consultations
  • Hospital Records
  • Labs
  • Imaging
  • Procedures
  • Administrative
  • Patient Education
Correct Document Placement Examples
  • Discharge summaries → "Discharge Summary" tab
  • Emergency department notes → "Emergency Department" tab
  • Specialist consult notes → "Consultation" tab
  • Lab results → "Labs" tab with correct DOS
  • MRI / CT reports → "Imaging" tab
🚫Always use the original Date of Service — not today's date, not the scan date. Using the wrong date makes records appear out of order and delays clinical care.
⚠️Do not create new or duplicate tab categories. If you are unsure which category to use, ask your supervisor before scanning.
✅
Daily Front Desk Checklist & Check-Out Responsibilities
▼
Before Clinic
  • Computers, scanner, and printer working
  • Supplies available at desk
  • Breeze Check-In responses reviewed
  • Appointment schedule reviewed for the day
During Clinic — Every Patient
  • Demographics verified and updated
  • Insurance verified; cards scanned
  • Prescription insurance card scanned
  • Next of Kin confirmed or obtained
  • Care Team updated
  • Copay / balance collected and documented
  • Forms entered into tracking sheet and routed to MA
  • Phones answered professionally; transfers minimized
  • MMSE / MIPS initiated in the encounter (not on paper)
  • EMG demographics entered into EMG computer before appointment
Check-Out — Before Patient Leaves
Patients should not leave without receiving everything discussed during the visit.
  • Follow-up appointment scheduled and confirmed with patient
  • Laboratory orders printed and handed to patient
  • Imaging orders printed and handed to patient
  • PT orders printed and provided
  • Brace prescriptions provided
  • Medication education materials provided if applicable
  • Visit summary provided if applicable
  • Any completed forms given to patient
  • Patient educated verbally on where to go for labs/imaging
End of Day
  • All forms routed; tracking sheet updated
  • Payments reconciled
  • Outstanding patient issues communicated to supervisor
  • EMG tables scanned after each patient checkout
  • Correct scheduling types used for every appointment booked
  • Referral documentation faxed as requested
  • All scanned documents indexed under correct category and DOS
  • Urgent messages communicated to appropriate provider or clinical staff

⚠ Critical Operational Standards — Front Desk

The following standards were identified from real patient encounter errors. All front desk staff must follow these procedures without exception.

🚪
Patient Rooming — Promptness, Room Type & Tracking Required — Every Visit
▼
Real Example: Sofia was roomed in Room 4 but this was never marked on the schedule. Clinical staff had no visibility into her status, causing delays and confusion. Additionally, EMG patients were brought to standard exam rooms without the EMG room being prepared — causing provider downtime and workflow disruption.
🚫Do not room a patient without immediately updating the schedule to reflect their room assignment. Patients must be roomed promptly — delays in rooming cascade through the entire provider schedule.
Required Steps When Rooming a Patient
  1. Escort the patient to the room promptly after check-in — do not leave them waiting at the desk
  2. Immediately update the schedule/appointment to indicate the patient is roomed
  3. Enter the room number (e.g., Room 4) in the designated field
  4. This must be done in real time — not after the fact
Correct Room Assignment by Appointment Type
⚠️Rooming a patient in the wrong room wastes provider time and disrupts clinic flow. Always match the patient to the correct room before escorting them.
⚡ EMG Patients — EMG Room Only
  • EMG patients must be roomed in the EMG room — never in a standard exam room
  • The EMG room must be fully prepared before the patient arrives: equipment powered on correctly (base power first), supplies stocked, EMG computer ready
  • Patient demographics must be entered in the EMG computer before the patient is brought in
🧠 CA Room — Patient Must Never Be Left Unattended After Testing
  • After Cognitive Assessment testing is complete, the patient must not be left alone in the CA room
  • Staff must actively track the patient's location and either room them for the provider or return them to the waiting area if there is a delay
  • Leaving a post-testing CA patient unattended is a patient safety and supervision concern
ℹ️Marking patients as roomed allows providers and clinical staff to know which patients are ready, prevents missed patients, and maintains accurate flow tracking throughout the day. Prompt rooming directly protects provider efficiency — a roomed patient means the provider can move immediately to the next encounter.
🧠
Scheduling Testing Appointments Correctly Medicolegal & Revenue Risk
▼
Real Example: A patient was due for a sleep-deprived EEG at his visit. The front desk did not schedule it. This omission creates medicolegal risk (ordered test not performed) and causes direct loss of revenue. This appointment was created without proper review of the provider's orders. Appointments created by front desk staff are subject to supervisor review — see quality control process below.
Front Desk Testing Scheduling Rules
❌ What Goes Wrong
  • Testing ordered by the provider is not added to the schedule
  • Wrong appointment type or duration is selected
  • EEG ordered at follow-up is not placed on the EEG technician's schedule
  • Sleep-deprived EEG scheduled without the correct protocol time slot
✅ What Must Be Done
  • Review the provider's documented plan before the patient leaves or when scheduling follow-ups — confirm the correct visit type (e.g., EMG vs. routine follow-up; new patient vs. established)
  • If testing is ordered (EEG, EMG, etc.), schedule it at the same time as the follow-up visit or as directed
  • For EEG at a follow-up: place the appointment on both the provider's schedule and the EEG technician's schedule
  • Select the correct appointment type and time slot for the specific test
  • Sleep-deprived EEG requires early morning scheduling per protocol
  • When in doubt about the correct appointment type, confirm with the MA or provider before scheduling — do not guess
Quality Control — Supervisor Review Process
Policy (Confirmed by Fatima): The front desk supervisor and scheduling team will review all appointments scheduled by front desk staff to verify they have been properly created — correct type, correct time slot, all required tests included, and placed on the correct provider/technician schedules. Any errors must be corrected before the appointment date.
💻
EMG Computer — Patient Demographics Entry
▼
Problem Identified: Patient demographics were not entered into the EMG computer prior to the procedure. It is the responsibility of front desk staff to enter demographics for all EMG patients so the provider can begin the study without delay.
Responsibilities & Startup Procedure
Front Desk Responsibilities
  • Enter all EMG patient demographics into the EMG computer before the appointment
  • This includes: patient name, date of birth, gender, referring provider, insurance, and diagnosis codes
  • If you are unsure how to enter demographics, ask the provider for guidance — do not skip it
  • Confirm entry is complete before the patient is roomed for EMG
⚠ Most Common Startup Mistake
The EMG computer will not function properly if the base power is off.
  1. Turn on the power in the base unit FIRST
  2. Wait for the base to initialize
  3. Then turn on the EMG computer

Turning on only the EMG computer without the base power being on is the most common error. Always start with the base.

📋
MMSE & MIPS Testing — Must Be Done in the Encounter
▼
Policy: All front desk staff must know how to perform MMSE and other required MIPS testing directly within the patient encounter in the EMR. MMSE must NOT be done on paper. Paper-based MMSE results cannot be properly tracked, reported, or attributed to MIPS quality measures.
Requirements
MMSE — Mini-Mental State Examination
  • Must be administered and documented directly in the patient's encounter in the EMR
  • Paper MMSE is not acceptable — results are not trackable for quality reporting
  • All front desk staff must be trained on how to open the MMSE in the encounter
  • If unsure, ask your supervisor or a trained colleague before administering on paper
MIPS Quality Measures
  • All required MIPS testing must be completed and documented within the encounter
  • Front desk staff are responsible for initiating MIPS tools at check-in or during rooming
  • Incomplete MIPS documentation affects the practice's quality reporting scores and reimbursement
  • Training on all applicable MIPS measures is mandatory for all front desk staff
ℹ️If you have not been trained on MMSE or MIPS entry in the EMR, notify your supervisor immediately. Do not administer these tests until you have received proper training.
👥
Care Team Completeness — All Specialists Must Be Listed
▼
Real Example: A patient is actively seeing a rheumatologist, nephrologist, and orthopedist — none of whom are listed in his care team in the EMR. An incomplete care team prevents the provider from having a full picture of the patient's medical management and creates coordination-of-care gaps.
🚫Do not leave the care team section incomplete. All actively involved providers must be listed.
Care Team Requirements
Who Must Be Listed
  • Primary Care Physician (PCP)
  • All active specialists the patient is currently seeing (e.g., rheumatologist, nephrologist, orthopedist, cardiologist, etc.)
  • Any co-managing provider involved in the patient's ongoing care
  • Referring provider if different from PCP
When to Update
  • At every new patient intake — ask about all current treating providers
  • At follow-up check-in — ask if they have seen any new specialists since last visit
  • Whenever a patient mentions a new provider during the visit — document it
  • When the PCP or any specialist changes — update immediately (see PCP change rule below)
ℹ️When collecting care team information, ask the patient: "Are you currently seeing any other doctors or specialists for any of your conditions?" Get the provider's full name and practice if possible.
🔄
PCP Changes — Remove Previous PCP Immediately
▼
Policy: When a patient's PCP changes, the previous PCP must be removed from the chart. Leaving an old PCP in the system causes referrals, records, and communications to be sent to the wrong provider — and creates a misleading care team record.
Process
  1. At check-in, confirm the patient's current PCP name and practice
  2. If it has changed, update the PCP field in the EMR immediately
  3. Remove or inactivate the previous PCP — do not leave both listed as active
  4. If a referral or record is in process for the old PCP, notify the appropriate staff to update the destination
  5. Document the date of the PCP change in the chart notes if needed
⚠️A simple question at check-in prevents this: "Is your primary care doctor still Dr. [Name]?" — ask it every visit.
📱
Patient Portal Enrollment & Guest WiFi — Check-In Requirement (Both Offices)
▼
Problem: The provider is being asked to enter the WiFi password for patients multiple times a day. Patient portal enrollment is being skipped at check-in. Both of these tasks are the responsibility of front desk staff and must be completed as part of the standard check-in process at both the Hagerstown and Cumberland offices.
🚫Front desk staff must not allow patients to bypass portal enrollment. The provider should never need to enter the WiFi password for a patient.
Check-In Requirements — Both Offices
Patient Portal Enrollment
  • At every check-in, confirm whether the patient has been enrolled in the patient portal
  • If not enrolled, enroll them during check-in — do not defer it
  • Walk the patient through downloading the app or accessing the portal on their device
  • Explain what the portal is used for: appointment info, messages, records, results
  • If the patient cannot manage it themselves, assist them in calling a family member who can help
  • Document portal enrollment status in the chart
Guest WiFi
  • Inform every patient of the guest WiFi at check-in
  • Provide the WiFi name and password — do not wait to be asked
  • This allows patients to access their portal, contact family, or look up their records while in the office
  • The provider should never be entering the WiFi password for patients — this is a front desk responsibility
Check-In Script — Portal & WiFi
At check-in, say:
"While you wait, feel free to connect to our guest WiFi — the network is [WiFi Name] and the password is [password]. Also, are you enrolled in our patient portal? It lets you view your appointments, send us messages, and access your medical records. Let me get you set up if you haven't already."
Portal Benefits — Why We Promote It
  • Appointment reminders automatically sent
  • Secure messaging reduces phone volume
  • Patients can review results and visit summaries
  • Faster communication with clinical staff
  • Patients enrolled in portal experience better overall care communication
⚠️
Common Front Desk Errors — Avoid These
▼
These are the most frequently observed Front Desk errors. Every staff member should review and actively avoid all items on this list.
  • Asking patients to repeat Breeze paperwork they already completed online
  • Failing to scan prescription insurance cards
  • Missing or incomplete Next of Kin information
  • Failing to update Care Team at check-in
  • Not entering forms into the tracking sheet
  • Giving vague or inaccurate answers about form status
  • Forgetting to distribute PT or brace prescriptions at check-out
  • Not informing patients of the standard two-week turnaround time for forms
  • Transferring calls unnecessarily instead of handling them at the Front Desk
  • Delaying provider flow by not preparing patients before being called
  • Rooming a patient without immediately updating the schedule
  • Not scheduling ordered testing (EEG, EMG) at the same time as the follow-up
  • Allowing a patient to leave without a scheduled follow-up appointment
  • Not enrolling patients in the patient portal at check-in
  • Using today's date instead of the Date of Service when scanning documents
📊
Quality Standards (KPIs) & Competency Assessment
▼
2.18 Key Performance Indicators — Front Desk Performance Is Monitored By:
  • Registration accuracy
  • Demographic completeness
  • Next of Kin completion rate
  • Prescription insurance scan rate
  • Breeze Online Check-In utilization rate
  • Average patient check-in time
  • Average patient wait time
  • Forms tracking completion rate
  • Customer service complaints
  • Payment collection accuracy
  • Portal enrollment rate
2.19 Competency Assessment — Before Working Independently, Demonstrate Ability to:
Progress:
0 / 12
  • ✓
    Register a new patient correctly including all demographics and insurance
  • ✓
    Verify demographics and insurance — identify Medicare vs Medicare Advantage
  • ✓
    Scan prescription insurance cards and know why Part D matters
  • ✓
    Update Care Team information with all active specialists
  • ✓
    Document Next of Kin with name, relationship, and contact number
  • ✓
    Process Breeze Online Check-In without duplicating paperwork
  • ✓
    Enter and track patient forms on the Forms Tracking Sheet
  • ✓
    Collect payments and document according to office policy
  • ✓
    Complete patient check-out — verify all orders, scripts, and follow-up were provided
  • ✓
    Answer common patient questions using tracking sheets confidently
  • ✓
    Enroll patients in the patient portal and demonstrate how to use it
  • ✓
    Communicate effectively with MAs, providers, and scheduling — know when to escalate
🧪
Receiving Calls About External Lab Results — Front Desk Action Required Required
▼
When a patient calls to say their blood work is done at an outside lab (not Meritus or UPMC), the Front Desk must act immediately — not take a message and move on. A prior delay in receiving external lab results caused a one-month treatment delay for a patient. This is a medicolegal risk.
When a Patient Calls — What to Do
  • When a patient calls to report that they have completed lab work at an external facility: immediately notify the patient's assigned MA — do not defer or create a task to handle later
  • Provide the MA with: patient name, date of birth, name of the external facility, and approximate date the tests were done
  • If you are unsure which MA is assigned to the patient, look it up in the EMR or ask a supervisor
  • Document the call in the chart: time received, patient name, facility, and that the MA was notified
Key Context — Why This Matters
  • Results from external facilities do not automatically appear in our EMR
  • At many outside facilities, lab results are filed under the PCP's name or order, not the specialist — our team must specifically request them
  • If results are not obtained and reviewed before the patient's next appointment, critical findings may be missed
  • Front Desk staff are often the first to receive this type of call — your response determines whether the results are captured in time
💊
Medication PA Monitoring, Formulary Verification & Manufacturer Samples Critical
▼
Issue IdentifiedMedication prior authorizations are not being monitored closely enough, leading to prolonged treatment delays. Formulary restrictions are being discovered late — after the prescribing decision — when alternatives or appeals could have been initiated much earlier. Manufacturer sample programs are underutilized as a bridge while PAs are pending.
PA Monitoring — Preventing Treatment Delays
  • Monitor all pending medication prior authorizations actively — do not wait for the patient to call about a delay
  • When a PA is pending, identify an expected decision date and follow up with the payer if it has passed
  • Notify both the provider and the patient promptly when a PA is approved, denied, or requires additional information
  • For denied PAs, immediately escalate to the PA team and provider — do not leave the patient without a plan
  • Document all PA status updates in the tracking sheet and patient chart
Formulary Verification — Do It Early
🚫Formulary verification must happen early in the prescribing process — before the medication is prescribed if possible, not after a pharmacy denial.
  • When a high-cost or specialty medication is under consideration, verify the patient's formulary status before or at the time of prescribing
  • Use the Formulary & PA Tool tab (🔍) to check plan-specific coverage quickly
  • If a medication is not covered or requires PA, notify the provider immediately so alternatives, step therapy, or an appeal can be initiated without delay
  • Identify formulary restrictions proactively — do not wait for the pharmacy to reject a claim
Manufacturer Sample Programs — Bridge Coverage
  • Pharmaceutical manufacturer sample programs should be fully utilized to bridge treatment while a prior authorization is pending
  • When a PA is submitted and approval is pending, immediately check whether the manufacturer offers samples or a bridge program
  • Samples prevent treatment gaps — patients should not go without medication while waiting for insurance approval when samples are available
Designated Sample Manager — Required
  • A designated staff member must be assigned responsibility for the sample program
  • Responsibilities include: ordering samples, maintaining inventory, tracking expiration dates, distributing to providers, and logging all samples dispensed to patients
  • This role must have a backup — sample management cannot depend on a single person
  • All sample dispensing must be documented in the patient's chart
🎓
Front Desk Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Front Desk Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

🩺 Medical Assistant (MA) / VMA Training

Section 3 — Official training guide for all Medical Assistants and Virtual Medical Assistants (VMAs). Covers pre-visit chart preparation, medication and allergy reconciliation, EMG workflow, neurological exam documentation, diagnosis entry, lab and imaging orders, referral management, prior authorizations, patient assistance programs, DME, home PT, provider communication, and chart completion.

Section 3 RequiredChart PrepMedication ReconciliationAllergy DocumentationEMG WorkflowNeuro ExamLab OrdersImaging OrdersReferral ManagementPrior AuthDMEHome PTOpenEvidence
📋
Core Responsibilities
▼
Clinical Prep
  • Pre-visit chart preparation (labs, imaging, consult notes)
  • Medication reconciliation — all classes including infusion medications
  • Allergy reconciliation and documentation
  • EMG workflow management
  • Cognitive assessment status review
Documentation & Coding
  • Neurological exam documentation in TalkEHR
  • Diagnosis entry — all diagnoses established this encounter
  • Upload patient education materials
  • Patient summaries and handouts
  • Correct chart labeling and date of service
  • Use OpenEvidence for clinical decision support
Follow-Through
  • Lab and imaging orders with correct ICD-10 codes
  • Follow up on results — pending labs, abnormals, no-shows
  • Referral management — initiate, track, confirm receipt
  • Proactive prior authorization initiation
  • DME and brace order processing
  • Home PT coordination
  • Controlled medication tracking and follow-up scheduling
  • Patient assistance program enrollment
  • Responsive provider communication — urgent interrupts only
📂
Pre-Visit Chart Preparation Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedProviders were entering exam rooms to find that labs, radiology reports, consult notes, or pending communications were missing from charts. This caused delays, repeat visits, and incomplete clinical decisions.
🚫Providers should never discover missing information during a patient encounter. All chart review must be completed before the provider enters the room.
Complete Pre-Visit Chart Review Checklist
  • Labs — Are the most recent results available and reviewed?
  • Radiology / Imaging — Are MRI, CT, X-ray, and ultrasound reports present?
  • Consult Notes — Are notes from all referred specialists present?
  • Hospital Records — Are any recent ED visits, hospitalizations, or discharge summaries documented?
  • Pending Communications — Are there unresolved messages, portal messages, or callback requests?
  • Referral Status — Are all outstanding referrals tracked and documented?
  • EMG Tables — Are EMG tables printed, scanned, and verified?
  • Medication List — Has the full medication list been verified (name, dose, frequency, formulation)? For Vitamin B: always specify which type — B Complex, B1 (Thiamine), or B12 (Cobalamin). "Vitamin B" alone is not acceptable documentation.
  • Allergies — Is the allergy list complete, including EpiPen inquiries?
  • Infusion Medications — For patients on Ocrevus, Tysabri, Briumvi, IVIG, or Vyepti: confirm infusion date, dose, next scheduled infusion, and any adverse reactions since last visit
  • Cognitive Assessment Status — If CA is ordered or pending: confirm insurance authorization, test battery assignment, and that the encounter is set up correctly
ℹ️Accuracy First: If information is missing or unclear, it must be obtained before the provider enters the room — not discovered during the visit.
Training Points
  • Missing information discovered during a visit slows down every subsequent patient
  • Providers who must search for records mid-visit lose trust in the support team
  • Chart prep is one of the highest-value tasks an MA performs — do it thoroughly
💊
Medication Reconciliation Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedMedication lists contained incorrect doses, wrong frequencies, duplicate entries, and missing medications. Parkinson's medications in particular had repeated dosing errors — which can have serious clinical consequences.
Most Common Medication Errors to Watch For
  • Incorrect dose listed (e.g., 25mg entered instead of 250mg)
  • Wrong frequency (e.g., "daily" instead of "three times daily")
  • Duplicate medication entries under different names
  • Medications the patient stopped taking still listed as active
  • New medications started by another provider not added
  • Incorrect formulation (e.g., regular vs. extended release)
  • Incorrect Parkinson's medication doses — extremely high-risk
⚠️
High-Risk Medications — Extra Verification Required:
Sinemet  ·  Requip  ·  Amantadine  ·  Anticoagulants (Warfarin, Eliquis, Xarelto)  ·  Seizure medications (Keppra, Lamictal, Dilantin)  ·  Controlled substances
Infusion Medications — Separate Verification Required
⚠️Patients on infusion therapies require additional reconciliation. Confirm infusion date, dose administered, next scheduled infusion, and any reactions or side effects since last infusion.
MS Infusion Therapies
  • Ocrevus (ocrelizumab) — every 6 months
  • Tysabri (natalizumab) — every 4 weeks; JC virus titer required
  • Briumvi (ublituximab-xiiy) — every 6 months after loading doses
Other Infusion Therapies
  • IVIG (various indications) — confirm product, dose, frequency
  • Vyepti (eptinezumab) — every 3 months for migraine prevention
⚠️ Mavenclad (Cladribine) — Required Documentation Every Encounter
  • Treatment start date (when the first course began)
  • Current treatment course and cycle (e.g., Course 1, Cycle 2)
  • Dates of completed cycles
  • Next treatment due date
⚠️All four items must be documented at every encounter for patients on Mavenclad. Missing any item is an incomplete chart.
Medication Verification Workflow — Complete All 6 Steps
  1. Verify medication name — exact spelling, generic vs. brand
  2. Verify dose — confirm with patient and compare to outside records
  3. Verify frequency — daily, twice daily, three times daily, as needed
  4. Verify formulation — regular, extended release, patch, liquid
  5. Compare with outside records — pharmacy printout, previous notes, hospital discharge list
  6. Clarify all discrepancies before provider enters room — do not leave it for the provider to discover
Training Points
  • When in doubt about any medication detail, ask the patient directly and compare with their pharmacy list
  • If a patient is unsure, call the pharmacy before the provider enters
  • Do not assume previously entered data is correct — verify every visit
⚠ Vitamin B — Always Specify the Type
Documenting "Vitamin B" is not acceptable. You must always clarify and document which specific form the patient is taking:
  • B Complex — a combination supplement containing multiple B vitamins
  • B1 (Thiamine) — used for neuropathy, alcohol-related deficiency, Wernicke's encephalopathy
  • B12 (Cobalamin) — used for neuropathy, deficiency, pernicious anemia; may be oral, sublingual, or injectable
Ask the patient directly: "Is it a B Complex, B1, or B12?" If they are unsure, check the bottle or call the pharmacy. Document the exact type, dose, and frequency.
Medication Safety — Special Clinical Scenarios
Lab Values That Don't Match the Medication Regimen
  • If lab values are inconsistent with the documented medication regimen, verify medication reconciliation before flagging to the provider
  • Common example: TSH elevated but patient is listed as taking levothyroxine — confirm dose, compliance, and current regimen
Hypothyroidism — Off Therapy
  • Patients with severe hypothyroidism who have been off therapy should generally be restarted on a low dose of levothyroxine per provider direction — do not assume previous high-dose therapy should resume
  • If thyroid management is deferred or outside neurology scope, notify the PCP and forward laboratory results
💡MRI Anxiety / Claustrophobia: Patients requesting anxiolytic medication to tolerate an MRI should be scheduled for an Open MRI whenever clinically appropriate, rather than initiating anxiolytic therapy.
Staff Education — Generic/Brand Names, Duplicates & Look-Alike Medications
⚠️Staff must be familiar with common neurological medications — including generic and brand name equivalents — to avoid duplicate documentation, medication errors, and EHR entry mistakes.
⚠️ Duplicate Therapy — Stimulants
  • Concerta is methylphenidate — if a patient lists both, this is a duplicate stimulant entry, not two separate medications
  • Do not document duplicate stimulant therapy without first clarifying with the provider
  • Common generic/brand pairs staff must know: Concerta/Ritalin = methylphenidate · Adderall = amphetamine salts · Vyvanse = lisdexamfetamine
🔴 Look-Alike / Sound-Alike Medications
  • Nortriptyline (tricyclic antidepressant / nerve pain) vs. Naratriptan (triptan / migraine) — these are entirely different medications; confusing them is a medication error
  • When in doubt about a medication name, look it up or ask before documenting
  • Never guess at a medication spelling — confirm with the patient, medication bottle, or pharmacy record
🚫Custom Medication Entries in EHR — Senior MAs Only. Only Senior MAs may create a custom medication entry in TalkEHR, and only after verifying the medication and discussing it with the provider or designated clinical supervisor. Never create a custom medication entry for an unverified or nonexistent medication. Incorrect custom entries corrupt the medication record and create patient safety risks.
🚨
Allergy Documentation Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedA patient was carrying an EpiPen for bee sting anaphylaxis, but this severe allergy was not documented in the chart at all. If this patient received a medication or procedure triggering a reaction, the chart would have shown no allergies.
🚫Undocumented severe allergies are a patient safety risk. Every allergy must be identified, verified, and documented at every visit.
EpiPen Rule
  • If a patient carries an EpiPen — always ask WHY
  • Verify the specific allergen and the type of reaction (anaphylaxis, hives, swelling, etc.)
  • Document the allergy with full details: allergen, reaction type, severity
  • Flag as "Severe" or "Life-Threatening" as appropriate
Required Question During Reconciliation"I see you carry an EpiPen — can you tell me what you're allergic to and what happens when you're exposed?"
General Allergy Review — Every Visit
  • Ask the patient to confirm all listed allergies at every visit
  • Ask if any new allergies or reactions have occurred since the last visit
  • Document reactions with specificity (e.g., "anaphylaxis" not just "allergic reaction")
  • Remove allergies only when the patient and provider both confirm it is safe to do so
⚡
EMG Workflow & Quality Control Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedEMG tables were missing from charts, charts were being marked ready for signature before EMG tables were verified, and dictation contained inaccuracies that were not caught before signing.
🚫A chart must NOT be marked ready for signature until the EMG table is confirmed printed, scanned, uploaded, and verified against the dictation.
EMG Table Workflow — All Steps Required Same Day
  1. EMG procedure is performed by provider
  2. EMG table is printed immediately after the procedure
  3. Table is scanned and uploaded to the patient's chart same day
  4. MA reviews the table against the dictation for accuracy
  5. Chart is marked ready for signature only after all items are verified
MA Verification — Check Each Item Against Dictation
  • Number of extremities tested — does the table match what was dictated?
  • Number of nerves tested — verify count matches dictation
  • Severity grading — confirm Mild / Moderate / Severe is correctly documented
  • Dictation accuracy — does the overall summary match the table findings?
👤Shahzaib is the designated EMG documentation trainer and will assist with retraining other MAs on EMG verification standards.

⚡ EMG / EEG Program Standards

Scheduling, coding, documentation, and follow-through requirements for all EMG and EEG studies.

Key Principle

Every EMG and EEG study should be clinically appropriate, accurately documented, correctly coded, and fully completed before the patient's visit is considered closed. Proper scheduling, documentation, and follow-up ensure high-quality patient care and accurate billing.

📌
EMG / EEG Scheduling & Coding Policies Required
▼
Scheduling Rules
  • Muscular Dystrophy patients: Schedule a consultation first. EMG should only be performed if the provider determines it is clinically necessary after that consultation. Do not schedule EMG as the initial encounter for suspected or known muscular dystrophy.
  • Acute injury timing: EMG should generally be performed 11–14 days after an acute injury for optimal diagnostic accuracy. Performing it too soon can produce false-negative results. If scheduling earlier, flag this for provider awareness.
  • EEG indications: Ensure EEGs are ordered when clinically indicated — including cognitive decline evaluation, stroke workup, and provider-directed indications. Do not skip or overlook EEG orders.
Coding & Documentation Rules
  • Add all clinically supported diagnoses: When applicable, include diagnoses such as Carpal Tunnel Syndrome (CTS) and upper/lower extremity pain. These are required for proper documentation and billing — omitting them can result in claim denials.
  • Use correct bilateral EMG/NCV templates and CPT codes. Bilateral studies require bilateral codes. Using the wrong template or failing to reflect bilateral testing creates coding errors and underbilling.
  • Upload all EMG and EEG reports to OpenEvidence after completion.
⚠️Hagerstown location: EMG reports should print at the Front Desk, not in the CA room. Confirm this is set up correctly at the start of each EMG day. Misrouted print jobs delay same-day scanning.
📄
EMG / EEG Documentation Requirements
▼

Every completed EMG or EEG report must contain all of the following before it can be considered complete and submitted for billing:

  • ✔ Correct patient history and clinical indication for the study
  • ✔ Appropriate diagnoses — all clinically supported codes included
  • ✔ Correct CPT codes for the procedure performed (including bilateral codes if applicable)
  • ✔ EMG/EEG findings — complete and accurate
  • ✔ Clinical impression / interpretation by provider
  • ✔ Provider signature — report must be signed before chart is closed
  • ✔ EMG tables printed, scanned, and verified against dictation (same day)
🚫A chart with a missing signature, unscanned EMG table, or incomplete findings is not ready to close. Do not mark it ready for billing or signature until every item above is confirmed.
🚫
EMG / EEG — Common Errors to Avoid
▼
  • Missing EMG/NCV CPT codes — especially bilateral codes when both sides were tested
  • Using the wrong documentation template — always confirm the template matches the study performed
  • Omitting CTS or extremity pain diagnoses when they are clinically supported and required for billing
  • Failing to scan EMG tables same day — tables left unscanned are a compliance and billing risk
  • Scheduling EMG for muscular dystrophy patients without a prior consultation
  • Performing EMG too soon after injury — before the 11–14 day window — producing unreliable results
  • Leaving incomplete or unsigned reports — unsigned reports cannot be billed and create a legal documentation gap
✅
EMG / EEG Daily Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Verified that the clinical indication supports each scheduled EMG/EEG study
  • ✓
    Confirmed correct appointment type and scheduling (consultation before EMG for MD patients; correct timing post-injury)
  • ✓
    Equipment and supplies prepared; EMG computer powered on correctly (base power first)
  • ✓
    CPT and ICD-10 codes verified — bilateral codes applied where applicable; CTS and pain diagnoses included when supported
  • ✓
    EMG tables printed and scanned same day — verified against dictation
  • ✓
    EMG/EEG reports uploaded to OpenEvidence
  • ✓
    Provider review and signature confirmed — no unsigned or incomplete reports remaining
📊
EMG / EEG Quality Indicators (KPIs)
▼
Documentation Accuracy
% of EMG/EEG reports with all required elements complete at first submission
Coding Accuracy
Correct CPT and ICD-10 codes applied — including bilateral codes and supporting diagnoses
Same-Day EMG Table Scanning Rate
% of EMG studies where the table was printed, scanned, and verified on the day of the procedure
Report Completion Time
Time between procedure completion and signed, finalized report available in chart
Provider Correction Rate
% of reports requiring corrections after provider review — reflects documentation accuracy upstream
Scheduling Accuracy
Correct study type scheduled, correct timing, correct prerequisite steps followed (e.g., consultation before EMG for MD patients)
🧠
Neurological Examination Documentation
▼
Problem IdentifiedPreviously documented abnormal neurological findings were disappearing from later visit notes. Normal exam findings were being incorrectly carried forward over known abnormal findings, creating a false record of improvement.
Expectations
  • Import the previous neurological examination into each new visit note
  • Update only the findings that have actually changed
  • Do not overwrite known abnormal findings with "normal" unless the provider has documented improvement
  • The documentation must reflect the actual examination performed that day
⚠️If the prior exam showed abnormal reflexes and today's exam did not test them, do not document them as normal. Leave the prior finding intact or flag it for provider review.
Training Points
  • Carry-forward documentation improves consistency and helps track disease progression
  • Inaccurate "normal" findings can affect medication decisions, disability determinations, and legal cases
  • When uncertain whether a finding changed, ask the provider before documenting
📤
Patient Handouts & Education Summaries
▼
Problem IdentifiedEducational materials and visit summaries were not being consistently uploaded to charts, making it impossible to verify what patients received or when.
MA Responsibilities — Every Visit
  • Upload patient visit summary to chart under "Patient Education" category
  • Upload all handouts provided to the patient
  • Document education materials in the chart note
  • Use the upload checkbox in your workflow to confirm completion
💡Supervisor spot audits will be performed. Expect random chart reviews to verify handout uploads are complete and consistent.
🦾
DME / Brace Orders
▼
All MAs Must Be Able to Independently:
  • Prepare brace orders with all required patient and provider information
  • Process carpal tunnel brace orders from start to finish
  • Complete and submit DME documentation to the supplier
  • Obtain provider signature on the order
  • Send orders without needing to rely on another staff member
  • Follow up with the DME supplier to confirm order receipt and delivery
⚠️If you have not processed a DME order before, ask to shadow the process at least once before doing it independently. Do not skip steps.
🏃
Home Physical Therapy (PT) Setup
▼
Problem IdentifiedStaff were relying entirely on one person (Shahzaib) to arrange all home PT services. When that person was unavailable, the process stalled and patients experienced delays in receiving care.
🚫Home PT setup must not depend on a single staff member. All MAs must know this workflow independently.
Home PT Setup Workflow — Full Process
  1. Identify patient need — confirm provider has ordered home PT
  2. Select appropriate agency — use the approved agency list; verify they accept patient's insurance
  3. Send required documentation — patient demographics, insurance info, provider order, diagnosis
  4. Obtain provider signature on the home PT order if not already signed
  5. Confirm receipt by agency — call or fax to verify they received the referral
  6. Document referral in chart — note the agency, date sent, and confirmation received
  7. Follow up until evaluation is scheduled and confirmed with patient
Training Points
  • Escalate early if an agency is not responding — do not wait days before acting
  • If an agency cannot see the patient within a reasonable time, try an alternative agency
  • Document all follow-up attempts with dates and outcomes
🧪
Laboratory Management Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedLab orders are placed but not consistently verified as transmitted. Abnormal results are not always acted upon promptly. Significant findings under another provider's management are occasionally not forwarded to the PCP.
Order Verification & Patient Requisitions
  • Verify that laboratory orders are successfully transmitted to the performing laboratory — do not assume an order was received simply because it was placed in the EHR
  • Provide patients with laboratory requisitions whenever possible so they can present them directly to the lab
  • Encourage every patient to enroll in the patient portal — lab orders can be accessed there, preventing delays when outside facilities cannot locate orders
Daily External Portal Monitoring — Mandatory
🚫Reviewing external lab portals is not optional — it must occur every clinical day. Waiting for results to be faxed is not sufficient.
Portals to Check Daily
  • Epic (Meritus, WVU, UPMC)
  • LabCorp portal
  • Quest portal
  • Any other external facility where results are expected
When Results Are Found
  • Download or save the result
  • Upload to the patient's chart under the correct category and date of service
  • Flag abnormal results immediately to the provider
  • Document in the chart that results were reviewed and communicated
Abnormal Results — Required Actions
  1. Review promptly — do not let abnormal results sit unreviewed
  2. Communicate to the provider — notify via chat with the result and flag severity
  3. Act on the provider's instructions — document the action taken (e.g., callback, order, referral)
  4. Document everything — result received, provider notified, action taken, follow-up planned
Forwarding Results to Other Providers
  • Significant laboratory abnormalities that fall under another provider's management (e.g., endocrinology, PCP) must be sent to the appropriate treating physician
  • Do not assume the patient will relay results to their other doctors
  • Document that results were forwarded: provider name, fax number, date sent
  • Develop a standardized process for CC'ing results to care team members when multiple providers are involved
🔀
Referrals & Scope of Practice Critical
▼
⚠️Differentiating between referrals neurology should initiate and recommendations for the PCP is a core MA skill. Doing it wrong either creates unnecessary work or drops needed follow-up entirely.
Neurology Initiates — Formal Referral Required
  • Any specialist referral directly related to a neurological condition or finding
  • Sleep studies (require active follow-up until scheduling and completion)
  • Physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy for neurological conditions
  • Any referral where the chart states "refer to" a specialist — the referral process must be completed and tracked
PCP Manages — Document as Recommendation
  • Non-neurological conditions identified during a neurology visit
  • Document as: "Recommendation: discuss [finding] with PCP for further management"
  • Do not initiate a formal referral for conditions outside neurology's scope unless the provider explicitly directs it
Active Follow-Up Requirement
  • All referrals — including sleep studies — require active follow-up until scheduling and completion are confirmed
  • A referral is not closed when it is sent — it is closed when results or consultation notes are received and uploaded
  • Track every referral in the centralized tracking sheet; assign a follow-up date
🔔
Patient Follow-Up — Walk-Outs, No-Shows & High-Risk Patients Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedComplex patients who leave before completing their visit are not consistently receiving chart review or follow-up. Walk-outs and no-shows may have outstanding labs, imaging, referrals, or clinical concerns that are being missed.
Walk-Out & No-Show Protocol
  • Any walk-out or no-show triggers a chart review by the assigned MA
  • Review and document status of all outstanding items:
    • Laboratory orders (sent? resulted? reviewed?)
    • Imaging orders (sent? scheduled? completed?)
    • Referrals (sent? confirmed? resulted?)
    • Prescriptions (sent? filled? any prior auth needed?)
    • Prior authorizations (status? expiring?)
    • Provider recommendations (actioned? documented?)
  • Document the chart review with date and findings
  • Notify the provider of any clinically significant outstanding items
Complex Patients Who Leave Before Visit Is Complete
  • Initiate active follow-up — do not wait for the patient to reschedule
  • Attempt contact by phone; document attempts and outcomes
  • Escalate to the provider if a patient cannot be reached and has critical pending items
High-Risk Patients — Proactive Outreach
  • High-risk patients (recent hospitalization, critical labs, complex medication regimens, cognitive impairment) should be proactively contacted and brought back into care when appropriate
  • Do not wait for the patient to call — identify the need from the chart and act first
  • Document all contact attempts and outcomes
🚶
History Taking — Fall Documentation Medicolegal
▼
⚠️A reported fall is not just a clinical finding — it may carry medicolegal significance. Every reported fall must be fully documented.
Required Documentation for Every Reported Fall
Clinical Details
  • Where the fall occurred (home, store, work, public property)
  • How it occurred (tripped, lost balance, syncope, mechanical)
  • Resulting injuries (head strike, fracture, laceration, loss of consciousness)
⚖️ Medicolegal Flags — Document & Flag to Provider
  • Fall at work → possible workers' compensation claim
  • Fall on someone else's property → possible premises liability
  • Fall involving a motor vehicle or third party → possible litigation
  • Any fall that may involve legal proceedings should be flagged to the provider immediately — the documentation in this chart may be used in court
💬
Provider Communication Standards Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedProviders were requesting information before entering exam rooms and receiving no response. They had to ask multiple times or enter without the information needed to care for the patient.
🚫Silence is never acceptable when a provider makes a request. Respond immediately — even if you do not yet have the answer.
Acceptable Immediate Responses When Still Researching
Still Looking"I'm looking into that right now — I'll have an answer for you in a moment."
Waiting on Records"I'm waiting for the records to come through — I'll bring them to you as soon as they arrive."
On the Phone"I'm currently on the phone with the facility to get that information."
Training Points
  • Acknowledging a request is part of patient safety — it keeps the provider informed
  • A simple "I'm on it" buys time and prevents repeat requests
  • If you cannot resolve a request, escalate to a supervisor — do not leave it unaddressed
Closed-Loop Provider Communication — Chat & Verbal
🚫Every provider concern raised in chat must be acknowledged, addressed, documented, and closed. No provider request or clinical concern should remain with an open communication loop.
  • When a provider sends a concern or request via chat, confirm receipt and act on it immediately
  • After a verbal discussion with the provider, send a brief follow-up message in the chat: "Discussed with you and documented in [Patient Name]'s chart."
  • Do not assume a verbal conversation was captured — always close the loop in writing
  • Review open chat threads at the end of every session — no unresolved items should carry over
Chat Follow-Up After Verbal Discussion"Discussed with you — documented in [Patient Name]'s chart under [date of service]. Loop closed."
Interrupt Protocol — During Patient Hours
🚫Do not interrupt the provider during a patient encounter for routine questions, refill requests, or non-urgent tasks. Accumulate non-urgent items and address them between patients or at the end of the session.
🔴 Interrupt Immediately
  • Patient deterioration or acute safety concern in the office
  • Results indicating immediate clinical action (e.g., critical lab values)
  • A family member or emergency contact with urgent clinical information
  • An urgent call from a hospital, ED, or specialist requiring provider decision
🟢 Hold Until Between Patients
  • Routine refill requests
  • Non-urgent patient callback requests
  • Scheduling questions
  • Administrative questions or document requests
  • Non-critical lab or imaging results
🩺
Physical Exam — MA Role & Documentation
▼

The MA plays a critical role in preparing the patient and setting up the exam room before the provider enters. Accurate documentation of vitals, history, and exam setup directly impacts the quality and efficiency of the visit.

Room Setup & Patient Preparation
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Greet patient and escort to exam room
  • ✓
    Obtain and document vitals: blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate, temperature, oxygen saturation, weight, and height
  • ✓
    Document chief complaint in the patient's own words
  • ✓
    Review and reconcile current medications and allergies — update any changes
  • ✓
    Review reason for visit and confirm appointment type (new patient, follow-up, procedure)
  • ✓
    Place gown or drape if required for the exam type
  • ✓
    Ensure exam room is stocked and equipment is functional (reflex hammer, tuning fork, penlight, tongue depressors, gloves)
  • ✓
    Notify provider that patient is ready
Neurological Exam — Key Components to Document
ℹ️For neurology visits the MA may be asked to pre-populate or note specific exam elements in the chart. Know what each component means so you can assist accurately.
Mental Status & Cognition
  • Orientation: person, place, time, situation
  • Alertness and level of consciousness
  • Attention and concentration
  • Short-term and long-term memory
  • Language: fluency, comprehension, naming, repetition
  • Cognitive screening tools: MoCA, MMSE, SLUMS (if administered)
Cranial Nerves
  • CN II: Visual acuity, visual fields
  • CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements, ptosis, pupil response
  • CN V: Facial sensation, jaw strength
  • CN VII: Facial symmetry, eye closure
  • CN VIII: Hearing
  • CN IX/X: Swallowing, gag reflex, palate elevation
  • CN XI: Shoulder shrug, head turn strength
  • CN XII: Tongue movement and midline
Motor & Coordination
  • Muscle tone: normal, increased (spastic/rigid), decreased (flaccid)
  • Muscle strength: graded 0–5 by muscle group
  • Coordination: finger-nose-finger, heel-shin, rapid alternating movements
  • Gait: normal, ataxic, spastic, steppage, antalgic
  • Romberg test (balance with eyes closed)
  • Tandem gait
Sensation
  • Light touch
  • Pinprick / pain sensation
  • Temperature sensation
  • Vibration sense (using tuning fork)
  • Proprioception (position sense)
  • Distribution of any deficits: dermatomal, stocking-glove, hemibody
Reflexes
  • Deep tendon reflexes (DTR): biceps, triceps, brachioradialis, patellar, Achilles — graded 0–4+
  • Plantar response (Babinski sign): flexor (normal) vs. extensor (abnormal)
  • Hoffman sign (upper motor neuron)
  • Clonus if present
Vitals — Normal Ranges Reference
Cardiovascular
  • Blood Pressure: <120/80 mmHg (normal)
  • Heart Rate: 60–100 bpm
  • Respiratory Rate: 12–20 breaths/min
Temperature & Oxygen
  • Temperature: 97.8–99.1°F (36.5–37.3°C)
  • O₂ Saturation: ≥95% on room air
Document & Flag
  • Document all vitals before provider enters room
  • Flag abnormal vitals immediately to the provider
  • Re-take BP if >140/90 after patient has rested 5 minutes
Common Neuro Exam Equipment
Standard Exam Room
  • Reflex hammer (Taylor or Queen Square)
  • Tuning fork (128 Hz for vibration; 512 Hz for hearing)
  • Penlight (pupil response, CN testing)
  • Tongue depressor (gag reflex, CN XII)
  • Cotton wisp or monofilament (light touch, sensation)
Additional for Neuro
  • MoCA / MMSE / SLUMS scoring sheet (if cognitive testing ordered)
  • Ophthalmoscope (funduscopic exam if ordered)
  • Blood pressure cuff — both arms if requested
  • Gloves for all invasive or contact portions of exam
⚠️ Anatomical Terminology — Common Errors That Must Be Corrected
Why This MattersIncorrect anatomical language in a clinical note is not just a writing error — it changes the meaning of the exam finding. The provider must then go back and correct every chart, which wastes their time and delays billing. After six months on the job, VMAs are expected to use correct anatomical terminology without being prompted.
🚫Core Rule: Movements happen at joints, not at body parts. You say "flexion at the knee" not "flexion of the thigh." You say "abduction at the hip" not "abduction of the thigh."
❌ Incorrect — Do Not Write
  • "No weakness of abduction or adduction of either thigh"
  • "No weakness of flexion or extension at either thigh"
  • "Radicular pain in the femoral distribution"
✅ Correct — Write This Instead
  • "No weakness of abduction or adduction at either hip"
  • "No weakness of flexion or extension at either hip or knee" (specify the correct joint)
  • "Pain in the distribution of the femoral nerve" (peripheral nerve, not radicular)
Real Corrected Example
Correct documentation:

"On examination, the right knee jerk is absent, while all other reflexes are normoreactive. She does not have any weakness of abduction or adduction at either hip, nor is there any weakness of flexion or extension at either hip or knee. There is mild give-way weakness of right leg extension with strength graded at 4+/5."
Key Anatomy Terms VMAs Must Know
Movements & Their Joints
  • Abduction / Adduction → at the hip, shoulder
  • Flexion / Extension → at the knee, hip, elbow, wrist, ankle
  • Dorsiflexion / Plantarflexion → at the ankle
  • Pronation / Supination → at the forearm
  • Inversion / Eversion → at the foot/ankle
Strength Grading Scale (MRC)
  • 0/5 — No contraction
  • 1/5 — Flicker only
  • 2/5 — Movement with gravity eliminated
  • 3/5 — Movement against gravity
  • 4/5 — Movement against some resistance (4−, 4, 4+)
  • 5/5 — Full strength, normal
Give-Way Weakness
  • Strength that suddenly gives way during testing — often effort-dependent or pain-related
  • Document as: "give-way weakness of [movement] at [joint], strength graded [X]/5"
  • Distinguish from true neurological weakness — provider will specify
⚠️ Radicular vs. Peripheral Nerve Distribution — Critical Distinction
Frequent Documentation ErrorVMAs have written "radicular pain in the femoral distribution." This is incorrect and contradictory. Radicular symptoms follow a nerve root (dermatomal) distribution. The femoral nerve is a peripheral nerve, not a nerve root. Pain or numbness in the femoral nerve territory is peripheral nerve distribution — not radicular.
