Radicular Pain / Radiculopathy
  • Originates from compression or irritation of a spinal nerve root
  • Follows a dermatomal distribution (e.g., L4, L5, S1, C6, C7)
  • Examples: sciatica (L5/S1), cervical radiculopathy (C6/C7)
  • Correct phrasing: "radicular symptoms in an L4 distribution" or "lumbar radiculopathy at L5"
Peripheral Nerve Distribution
  • Originates from a named peripheral nerve (not a nerve root)
  • Examples: femoral nerve, sciatic nerve, peroneal nerve, ulnar nerve, median nerve
  • Correct phrasing: "pain in the distribution of the femoral nerve" or "femoral neuropathy"
  • Never write "radicular pain in the femoral distribution" — this is contradictory
Correct example:
"The patient does not have radicular symptoms in the distribution of the femoral nerve, making lumbar radiculopathy an unlikely explanation for her symptoms." — This sentence is correctly saying there are NO radicular symptoms; the issue is peripheral nerve, not nerve root.
Incorrect example (do not write):
"Radicular pain in the femoral distribution" — The femoral nerve is peripheral. Radicular symptoms follow nerve roots. These cannot be combined.
Exam Template Standard — Practice Policy
All providers must use the same exam templates that Dr. Mir uses.There is one standard. Every provider in this practice follows Dr. Mir's exam templates exactly — no exceptions, no deviations, no personal formatting preferences. If you are assisting a provider whose documentation does not match this standard, bring it to the attention of management.
History-to-Exam Consistency — Critical Review Step
Real Example: A patient's history documented tremor, but the exam did not reflect any coordination findings in the hands. If tremor is present in the history, coordination disturbance in the hands must be addressed in the exam. Omitting this creates an inconsistent, incomplete, and clinically inaccurate note.
🚫Do not complete an encounter with exam findings that contradict or fail to reflect what is described in the history.
❌ Inconsistent Documentation Example
  • History states: "Patient has tremor in both hands"
  • Exam states: "Coordination — normal"
  • This is clinically contradictory — tremor inherently affects coordination
  • The exam was not reviewed after the history was written
✅ Consistent Documentation
  • History states: "Patient has tremor in both hands"
  • Exam reflects: "Coordination — impaired; intention tremor noted bilaterally in upper extremities"
  • Impression and plan reference the tremor and its functional impact
  • All three sections are internally consistent
📋 Mandatory Review Step: Once you have completed the history and the impression/plan, go back and re-read the exam section. Verify that every symptom or finding mentioned in the history is appropriately addressed in the exam. If something is missing or inconsistent, correct it before finalizing the note.
Common History → Exam Correlations to Check
If History Mentions…
  • Tremor → check coordination, fine motor
  • Weakness → check motor strength by muscle group
  • Numbness / tingling → check sensation (light touch, pinprick)
  • Dizziness / vertigo → check gait, Romberg, nystagmus
If History Mentions…
  • Memory problems → check cognitive/orientation
  • Falls → check gait, balance, lower extremity strength
  • Headache → check cranial nerves, fundoscopic if indicated
  • Slurred speech → check cranial nerves, speech fluency
If History Mentions…
  • Vision changes → check visual fields, cranial nerves II/III/IV/VI
  • Swallowing difficulty → check cranial nerves IX/X
  • Back/neck pain with radicular symptoms → check reflexes, sensation, motor by level
  • Seizures → check post-ictal findings if applicable
Documentation Standards
🚫Never document exam findings you did not personally observe or record. If the provider updates or modifies findings, that is their responsibility — not yours.
  • Document vitals exactly as measured — do not round or estimate
  • Use objective language: "Patient reports…" / "BP measured at…" / "Gait observed as…"
  • Note the time vitals were taken
  • Flag and immediately verbally notify the provider of any critical values (BP >180/110, O₂ sat <90%, HR <50 or >120)
  • If patient refuses any portion of the exam, document the refusal clearly
  • Complete all MA documentation before leaving the room
  • Document everyone present during the encounter — family members, caregivers, interpreters. Example: "Wife present and participating in the visit." This improves documentation reliability and medicolegal protection.
  • Document collateral historians when information is obtained from someone other than the patient. Example: "History provided by patient's daughter, who is the primary caregiver."
🩼
Medical Supplies & Durable Medical Equipment (DME)
▼
General Ordering Process
Step 1 — Create the Prescription
  • Enter the prescription electronically (eRx)
  • If item is not in the medication list, use the Custom Prescription option (medical supplies/equipment only)
  • Select Pharmacare Bedford Road as the pharmacy
  • Enter all necessary instructions in the Sig (Directions) field
  • Send the prescription electronically to the pharmacy
Step 2 — Pharmacy Processing
  • Pharmacare forwards the order to their Home Medical Supplies Department
  • Their team reviews the order and determines insurance requirements
  • Pharmacare is responsible for obtaining prior authorization when applicable
  • They will request supporting documentation from our office as needed
Paper Prescriptions — When & Why
Reasons to Provide a Paper Prescription
  • Pharmacare may be unable to obtain authorization
  • Patient may choose another medical supply company
  • Patient may use a specialty pharmacy
  • Patient may contact the manufacturer directly
Office Responsibilities When Sending Documents
  • Ensure provider notes are completed and signed promptly
  • When requested, fax together: Prescription, Insurance information, Most recent office visit note, and any additional supporting documentation
  • Sending complete documentation together prevents repeat requests and delays
ℹ️Most medical supply and DME companies have their own authorization departments. They will verify insurance, obtain prior authorization, and coordinate delivery — contacting our office only if additional documentation is required. Our office generally does not initiate DME prior authorizations.
Quick Reference — What to Send
Send Orders To

Pharmacare Bedford Road

Always Include
  • Prescription
  • Insurance information
  • Most recent office visit note
  • Any supporting documentation
Remember

The supplier — not our office — typically obtains prior authorization for medical equipment and supplies.

Pharmacare — Available Products Directory
😴 Sleep Therapy
  • CPAP Machines, Travel CPAP, AirMini Portable CPAP, CPAP Supplies
  • AirCurve 10 Bilevel (BiPAP)
  • CPAP Masks: Philips Respironics, Sunset Healthcare, Fisher & Paykel
  • CPAPMax Pillow 2.0, Mask Wipes, Moisture Therapy Products
🫁 Oxygen & Respiratory
  • Oxygen Concentrators, Portable Oxygen Tanks, Rhythm Healthcare Portable Oxygen Systems
  • Nebulizers (standard & portable), Suction Machines, Tracheostomy Supplies
  • Acapella Devices, Incentive Spirometers, Peak Flow Meters
♿ Mobility Aids
  • Wheelchairs, Transport Chairs
  • Rollators, 2-in-1 Rollator Transport Chairs, Stand Up/Rise Up Rollators
  • Folding Walkers, Canes, Crutches, Knee Scooters, Mobility Accessories
🩸 Diabetic Supplies
  • Blood Glucose Testing Supplies
  • Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) Supplies
  • Sharps Containers, Diabetic Socks
🛏️ Home Equipment
  • Hospital Beds, Bedside Tables, Bed Rails
  • Power Lift Chairs, Trapeze Bars, Hoyer Lifts
  • Breast Pumps: Spectra S1/S2, Freemie, Motif, Medela (multiple models)
🚿 Bathroom Safety
  • Bath Chairs, Stools, 360° Swivel Seats, Transfer Benches, Wall Mounts
  • Bedside, Rolling & Shower Commodes
  • Raised Toilet Seats, Risers, Rails, Grab Bars
🦴 Braces, Supports & Footwear
  • Upper/Lower Extremity Braces, Back, Waist & Neck Supports
  • Compression Stockings — fitting appointments: 8:30–10:00 AM
  • Therapeutic Comfort Socks, Dr. Comfort Footwear
🩺 Incontinence & Ostomy
  • Disposable/Reusable Undergarments, Pads, Shields, Catheters, Leg Bags, Bed Pads, Underpads
  • Moisture Barrier Creams/Ointments, Perineal Cleansers
  • One/Two-Piece Ostomy Systems, Adhesives/Removers, Barriers, Deodorizers, Irrigation Kits
🧰 Personal Care & Patient Room
  • Dressing Aids, Footwear Aids, Bed Pans, Urinals, Bed Alarms
  • Blood Pressure Monitors, Cushions, Pillows, Wedges
  • Medical Alert Devices, Patient Room Supplies
Equipped for Life — Hagerstown
ℹ️Equipped for Life is a medical supply store, not a pharmacy. Orders should be handled through the referral process rather than eRx.
Option 1 — Referral (Preferred)
  • Create a referral to Equipped for Life
  • The facility is already in the referral/address book — select it directly
  • Include requested equipment/supplies in the referral documentation
Option 2 — Direct Fax
  • Fax orders directly to Equipped for Life with all required supporting documentation
Option 3 — Paper Prescription
  • Patient can present Rx directly to Equipped for Life
  • Patient may use another vendor or contact the manufacturer directly
🔗Resource Guide: Equipped for Life Medical Supply Resource Guide
U-Step Walker — Special Ordering Process
The U-Step Walker is ordered differently from all other DME.It requires a paper prescription and direct patient involvement. The patient contacts U-Step directly — our office does not submit the order.
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Complete a paper prescription for the U-Step Walker — signed by the provider
  • ✓
    Provide the original signed prescription directly to the patient
  • ✓
    Ensure the provider's office note is completed, signed, and clearly documents medical necessity for the walker
  • ✓
    Give patient the U-Step website: ustep.com
  • ✓
    Give patient phone numbers: 800-558-7837 / 847-676-1275
  • ✓
    Inform patient they are responsible for contacting U-Step, providing insurance info, completing paperwork, and coordinating delivery
  • ✓
    If U-Step contacts our office for notes or documentation — provide records promptly to support authorization
ℹ️U-Step verifies insurance benefits, obtains prior authorization, and coordinates delivery. Our office does not initiate this authorization. If they request office notes or clinical documentation, provide it promptly.
💊
Prescription Refill Management Provider Authority — Always Consult
▼
Provider Maintains Final Authority Over All Prescription Decisions.When in doubt, consult the provider before authorizing or processing any refill. MAs do not independently authorize refills — they prepare, review, and route them to the provider.
1. NSAIDs — Non-Steroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs
Common NSAIDs
  • Diclofenac
  • Naproxen
  • Meloxicam
  • Ibuprofen
GI Protective Agents (Often Used With NSAIDs)
  • Omeprazole
  • Pantoprazole
  • Esomeprazole
  • Rabeprazole
Refill Protocol
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Notify the provider before processing any NSAID refill
  • ✓
    Review medication list for duplicate NSAID therapy — patients should not take multiple NSAIDs simultaneously unless provider-directed
  • ✓
    Assess recent kidney function studies when available
  • ✓
    Review patient history for: renal impairment, GI bleeding, peptic ulcer disease, long-term NSAID use
⚠️NSAIDs may cause renal toxicity. Long-term use increases risk of GI bleeding, gastritis, stomach lining irritation, and peptic ulcer disease.
2. Corticosteroids
Common Oral Steroids
  • Prednisone
  • Prednisolone
  • Methylprednisolone
  • Dexamethasone
Common IV Steroids
  • Methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol)
Refill Protocol
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Confirm the refill request with the provider before processing
  • ✓
    Do not automatically authorize long-term steroid therapy
  • ✓
    Verify the indication for continued treatment
  • ✓
    Determine whether the patient requires a follow-up appointment before additional medication is prescribed
⚠️Long-term steroid use may increase blood glucose, contribute to weight gain, increase diabetes risk, and cause multiple systemic side effects. Most patients receive steroids for short-term/acute conditions — long-term therapy must always be provider-approved.
3. Anti-Epileptic Medications (AEDs)
Common AEDs
  • Levetiracetam
  • Lamotrigine
  • Lacosamide
  • Carbamazepine
  • Valproic Acid
  • Phenytoin
  • Phenobarbital
Refill Protocol
  • Refill up to 90 days when authorized by the provider
  • Schedule follow-up every 90 days (stable patients may be seen every 6 months per provider)
  • Ensure no interruptions in therapy — gaps may cause breakthrough seizures
  • Do not allow refill delays that could result in ER visits or hospitalization
🚨Missing doses or therapy gaps in AED patients may cause breakthrough seizures, ER visits, and hospitalization. Refill continuity is clinically critical for this medication class.
4. Neuro-Modulating Agents
Common Agents
  • Gabapentin
  • Pregabalin
  • Levetiracetam (neuropathic use)
  • Other provider-designated agents
Refill Protocol
  • Review recent kidney function tests before authorizing refills for high-dose patients
  • Ensure patient is seen every 2–3 months
  • Do not allow refill intervals to exceed 90 days without provider review
  • Schedule follow-up before medication supply is exhausted
  • If patient reports significant chronic pain despite escalating doses — discuss Pain Management referral with provider
⚠️Kidney function monitoring is especially important for patients on high-dose Gabapentin, Pregabalin, or Levetiracetam.
5. Opioids & Controlled Analgesics
All Opioid Refills Require Provider Authorization.Do not process opioid refills without explicit provider direction. PDMP review is mandatory before every refill.
Common Opioids
  • Tramadol
  • Oxycodone
  • Hydrocodone
  • Morphine
Refill Protocol
  • Provider authorization required — always
  • Do not provide more than a 2-month supply at one time unless provider-directed
  • Schedule follow-up every 1–2 months
  • Verify a current pain contract is on file
  • Review PDMP before processing — document all PDMP reviews in the chart
🧪 Urine Drug Screening (UDS)
  • Random UDS every 2–3 months for chronic opioid patients
  • Include comprehensive screening + confirmatory testing for positive results + random urine creatinine
💊 Pill Counts
  • Provider may request random pill counts
  • Pill counts may occur prior to refill authorization or after dispensing
🚩 PDMP Red Flags — Escalate to Provider
  • Multiple opioid prescriptions
  • Multiple prescribers
  • Early refill patterns
  • Drug-seeking behavior

Counsel patient regarding potential pain contract violation and refer to provider immediately.

6. SSRIs & Other Antidepressants
Medication Classes
  • SSRIs
  • SNRIs
  • Other antidepressants
Refill Protocol
  • Follow the provider's treatment plan
  • Ensure patients are seen within 3 months
  • Confirm medication compliance
  • Review medication list for: duplicate antidepressants, drug interactions, outside provider prescriptions
⚠️Concurrent use of multiple serotonergic medications may increase the risk of Serotonin Syndrome. Signs and symptoms should be promptly reported to the provider.
7. Nutritional Supplements
Common Supplements & Typical Doses
SupplementTypical Dose
Thiamine100 mg
Zinc Picolinate30 mg
Copper Gluconate2 mg
Magnesium Glycinate400 mg
Riboflavin (B2)25 mg
Pyridoxine (B6)25 mg
Cyanocobalamin (B12)1,000–2,000 mcg
Vitamin DProvider-directed
Refill Protocol
  • Verify current supplementation plan
  • Determine whether insurance covers the supplement
  • Inform patient when over-the-counter purchase is required
  • Reinforce compliance with provider recommendations
General Refill Checklist — Before Processing Any Refill
Progress:
0 / 11
  • ✓
    Verify patient identity
  • ✓
    Review last office visit date
  • ✓
    Confirm follow-up requirements have been met
  • ✓
    Review medication list
  • ✓
    Check for duplicate therapies
  • ✓
    Review recent laboratory studies when applicable
  • ✓
    Assess for medication interactions
  • ✓
    Verify provider refill plan
  • ✓
    Review PDMP for controlled substances
  • ✓
    Ensure required UDS testing is up to date when applicable
  • ✓
    Document all refill-related communications in the medical record
Provider Notification — Always Required For
🔔 Notify Provider Immediately
  • Any NSAID refill request
  • Any steroid refill request
  • Any controlled substance refill
  • Abnormal laboratory findings
🔔 Also Notify Provider For
  • Medication interactions identified
  • Duplicate prescriptions found
  • Suspected drug-seeking behavior
  • Any situation involving patient safety concerns
🔢
Diagnosis Entry — Every Encounter Critical
▼
🚫Diagnosis codes must reflect what the provider documented in this encounter — not the codes from the referring PCP's note. Always read the provider's note before entering diagnoses.
Diagnosis Entry Workflow
  1. Read the provider's complete note for this encounter
  2. Identify every diagnosis the provider documented — including new diagnoses, acute conditions, and chronic conditions addressed today
  3. Enter all diagnoses in TalkEHR using correct ICD-10 codes with appropriate specificity (laterality, severity, acuity)
  4. Add any new diagnoses to the problem list
  5. Do not use only the referring PCP's codes — the provider may have established different or additional diagnoses
  6. If a diagnosis is unclear or code is unknown, ask the provider — do not skip it
Common Errors to Avoid
  • Copying ICD-10 codes from PCP referral without adding new diagnoses from today's visit
  • Using unspecified codes when laterality or specificity is documented (e.g., G56.00 vs. G56.01 right carpal tunnel)
  • Missing a new diagnosis the provider established during the encounter
  • Omitting a procedure diagnosis (EMG, EEG, Botox) — these require a billable ICD-10 code
🔬
Laboratory Orders — Placing & Verifying
▼
Standard Laboratory Order Workflow
  1. Identify the labs ordered by the provider in the note or via verbal/written instruction
  2. Enter the order in TalkEHR with the correct ICD-10 diagnosis code(s) for medical necessity
  3. Select the appropriate lab (Quest, LabCorp, or facility-specific per patient's insurance)
  4. For fasting labs — instruct the patient on fasting requirements (typically 8–12 hours)
  5. Print or send the lab requisition to the patient
  6. Document order placed in chart with date
Key Rules
ICD-10 Must Match the Lab
  • Each lab ordered must have a supporting diagnosis code
  • Do not use a generic or unrelated code — it will cause a denial
  • For monitoring labs (e.g., CBC on Tysabri) — use the MS diagnosis + the monitoring indication
Prescription Math Verification
  • When verifying controlled medication refills, calculate the expected supply vs. fill date
  • Example: 30 tablets prescribed monthly — patient should not request refill before day 25–28
  • Flag early refill requests to the provider before processing
📊
Laboratory Follow-Up & No-Show Review
▼
Pending Lab Follow-Up
  • Review the pending lab list daily — identify any labs ordered but not yet resulted
  • For labs ordered >7 days ago with no result: call the lab to confirm receipt and status
  • For critical or urgent labs ordered: follow up within 24–48 hours if no result
  • Document all follow-up attempts in the chart
  • When a result returns: review for abnormals, route to provider per the Abnormal Lab Results protocol
Abnormal Results — Immediate Action Required
🚫Abnormal lab results must be reviewed and routed to the provider the same day they are received. Never file a result without review.
  • Flag any result outside reference range for provider review
  • Critical values (e.g., potassium <3.0, sodium <125, INR >4.0) — notify provider immediately
  • Document provider notification and any orders given in response
  • Follow up with the patient per provider instruction
No-Show Review — Lab & Imaging
  • Weekly: review patients who were ordered labs or imaging but have not completed them within 30 days
  • Attempt patient contact (call + portal message) to reschedule
  • Document contact attempts in chart
  • Notify provider if patient is refusing or cannot be reached — provider may need to document the offer and patient refusal
🏥
Imaging Orders — Placing & Coordinating
▼
Imaging Order Workflow
  1. Identify the imaging study ordered (MRI brain, MRI spine, CT, ultrasound, etc.)
  2. Enter the order in TalkEHR with the correct ICD-10 code(s)
  3. Determine whether prior authorization is required (see Prior Auth — Proactive Initiation)
  4. Generate the imaging requisition / order form
  5. Send or provide to patient with facility options per their insurance network
  6. For urgent imaging — call the facility to schedule directly and confirm availability
  7. Document order placed, facility name, and scheduled date in the chart
MRI-Specific Rules
Contrast vs. Non-Contrast
  • Confirm whether provider ordered with contrast, without contrast, or both
  • Include this clearly on the requisition — facilities will not assume
  • For MS monitoring — typically without and with contrast unless provider specifies otherwise
Follow-Up After Order
  • Track MRI orders to completion — confirm facility received the requisition
  • When report is returned: scan into chart under Imaging category with correct DOS
  • Route completed report to provider for review same day received
🚨
Urgent Imaging & Safety Concerns — MA Escalation Protocol Required
▼
🚨When a patient presents with any of the following symptoms and imaging has been ordered, treat it as urgent. Do not process these as routine imaging requests. Flag immediately and escalate to the provider if anything is causing a delay.
Presentations That Require Urgent Escalation
  • Possible cord compression — any spinal level; risk of permanent neurological damage
  • Worsening or progressive weakness — new or rapidly worsening weakness in any limb
  • Cauda equina symptoms — bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness or numbness
  • Severe radiculopathy — debilitating nerve root pain with neurological deficits (foot drop, hand weakness, sensory loss)
  • Rapidly progressive neurological symptoms — any symptom worsening over hours to days
MA Action Steps for Urgent Imaging:
  1. Place the imaging order immediately and flag it as urgent in the chart and on the PA tracking sheet (Orange row)
  2. Notify the PA team that this is an urgent imaging request requiring expedited prior authorization — provide the clinical reason
  3. If the PA is pending or delayed, notify the provider the same day — do not wait
  4. If the PA is denied, bring it to the provider's attention immediately — the same day. The provider will decide next steps (peer-to-peer, appeal, or direct-to-imaging)
  5. Document all steps taken and the timeline in the patient's chart
🧠
OpenEvidence — Clinical Decision Support Workflow
▼
What OpenEvidence Is

OpenEvidence is an AI-powered clinical reference tool that provides evidence-based answers for clinical questions. MAs and VMAs are expected to use it when facing unfamiliar clinical scenarios, medication questions, or documentation uncertainty — rather than guessing or leaving items blank.

When to Use OpenEvidence
  • Confirming the correct ICD-10 code for an unfamiliar diagnosis
  • Looking up monitoring requirements for a specialty medication
  • Verifying drug interactions when reconciling a complex medication list
  • Understanding clinical terminology in a specialist note before documenting
  • Confirming appropriate lab panels for a diagnosis or condition
Key Rules
⚠️OpenEvidence is a reference tool — do not use it to make clinical decisions independently. If a result raises a clinical concern, bring it to the provider. Never document clinical conclusions that the provider has not reviewed.
  • Use for reference and verification — not independent clinical judgment
  • Always confirm with the provider if you are uncertain after consulting OpenEvidence
  • Document your actions based on provider direction — not solely on what OpenEvidence states
🔄
Referral Management — MA Role
▼
ℹ️The MA is responsible for initiating referrals, confirming receipt, and tracking completion. Referrals are not complete when the fax is sent — they are complete when the specialist appointment is confirmed and the note is received.
Referral Initiation Workflow
  1. Identify the referral ordered by the provider
  2. Confirm the correct specialist (or ask provider if unspecified)
  3. Verify the patient's insurance and confirm the specialist is in-network
  4. Determine whether prior authorization is required for the referral
  5. Prepare the referral packet: provider note, relevant labs/imaging, insurance information, reason for referral
  6. Fax or submit the referral to the specialist's office
  7. Document referral sent in the chart: date, specialist name, practice, fax number, reason
  8. Confirm receipt with the specialist's office within 2–3 business days
Referral Follow-Up
  • Track all open referrals — review the pending referral list weekly
  • If no appointment confirmation within 7–10 days, call the specialist's office
  • When the specialist's note is received, scan into chart under Consultations with correct DOS
  • Route the specialist note to the provider for review
  • For urgent referrals — call to confirm receipt same day and obtain earliest available appointment
Controlled Medication Follow-Up Scheduling
🚫Patients on controlled medications must be seen at the provider-specified interval. Same-day or same-week controlled substance prescriptions are not permitted without a scheduled follow-up appointment in the system.
  • When a controlled medication refill is processed, confirm a follow-up appointment is scheduled
  • Standard intervals: opioids every 1–3 months; benzodiazepines every 1–3 months; stimulants every 3 months — follow provider-specified interval
  • If no follow-up is scheduled, schedule before releasing the refill or notify the provider
💊
Patient Assistance Programs (PAPs)
▼
Purpose

Patient Assistance Programs help patients who cannot afford specialty medications obtain them at reduced cost or free of charge through the manufacturer. MAs are responsible for identifying eligible patients and initiating enrollment.

When to Initiate a PAP Application
  • Patient reports inability to afford their specialty medication
  • Insurance denies coverage and appeal is pending or exhausted
  • Patient is uninsured or underinsured
  • Copay is prohibitively high despite coverage
Enrollment Workflow
  1. Identify the medication manufacturer
  2. Visit the manufacturer's PAP website or call the copay/assistance program line
  3. Determine eligibility criteria (income thresholds, insurance status)
  4. Collect required documentation: provider signature, patient income verification, insurance information
  5. Complete and submit the application
  6. Document application submission and reference number in the patient chart
  7. Follow up on application status within 10 business days
  8. Notify patient of approval and coordinate medication delivery or dispensing
Common PAP Programs
MS Medications
  • Ocrevus: Genentech Access Solutions
  • Tysabri: Biogen Patient Assistance
  • Kesimpta: Novartis Patient Assistance
  • Mayzent: Novartis Patient Assistance
  • Gilenya: Novartis Patient Assistance
  • Aubagio: Sanofi Patient Connection
Other Specialty Medications
  • Vyepti: Lundbeck Patient Assistance
  • Leqembi: Eisai Assistance Program
  • Botox: Allergan Botox Patient Assistance
  • IVIG: varies by product — check manufacturer
🚨
Prior Authorization Denials — Escalation & Provider Notification Critical
▼
Real Case: A patient's Botox prior authorization was denied every month starting in October. The prior auth staff continued re-submitting the same request each time without ever informing the provider. The patient's appointments were rescheduled repeatedly — for months — with no resolution. The provider was never made aware of the denials. This is unacceptable. Repeated re-submission without a strategy change does not resolve a denial. The provider must be notified so the clinical approach can be adjusted.
🚫Do not continue re-submitting a prior authorization that has been denied multiple times without first notifying the provider and discussing a different strategy.
When a PA Is Denied — Required Actions
❌ What Was Done Wrong
  • PA was denied — re-submitted without notifying provider
  • This cycle repeated for months
  • Patient kept getting rescheduled with no progress toward approval
  • Provider had no knowledge of the ongoing denials
  • No alternative strategy was ever considered
✅ Correct Process After a Denial
  • Notify the provider and/or the MA immediately after the first denial
  • Provide the denial reason in full — do not summarize it down to "denied"
  • Ask for provider guidance before submitting again
  • Do not re-submit the same request without a revised clinical strategy
  • Document all denials and communications in the patient chart
Alternative Strategies — Discuss With Provider
When a PA is repeatedly denied, the prior auth staff must bring it to the provider or MA for guidance. The provider may direct one of the following strategies:
📋 Schedule a Documentation Visit
  • Schedule a dedicated visit to update clinical documentation
  • Provider adds stronger, more specific medical necessity language
  • Updated notes are then used to support the new PA submission
  • Insurance often requires more recent or detailed clinical evidence
📞 Peer-to-Peer Review
  • Provider calls the insurance medical reviewer directly
  • Often resolves denials that paperwork submissions cannot
  • Prior auth team must obtain the peer-to-peer contact number from the insurer
  • Schedule the call and notify the provider with all denial details
📂 Formal Appeal
  • Submit a formal written appeal with updated clinical records
  • Include a letter of medical necessity from the provider
  • Reference the specific denial reason and address it directly
  • Track appeal deadlines — missing them forfeits the right to appeal
Communication Standard — Every Denial Must Be Documented
Every PA denial must be:
1. Documented in the patient chart (date, insurer, denial reason, reference number)
2. Communicated to the provider or MA on the same day it is received
3. Followed by a documented plan — either revised submission strategy or provider guidance
4. Never left unaddressed while appointments continue to be rescheduled
📋
Prior Authorization — Proactive Initiation Important
▼
ℹ️Prior authorizations must be initiated before services are rendered — not after a denial. The MA's role is to identify when a PA is required and start the process proactively.
When to Initiate a PA
  • Any new specialty medication (DMTs, biologics, Botox, Vyepti, Leqembi)
  • MRI or advanced imaging ordered — many insurers require prior auth
  • EMG and EEG — verify payer requirements
  • Cognitive assessment testing
  • Home PT or outpatient PT referrals (select payers)
  • IVIG infusions
  • Any procedure the payer lists as requiring authorization
PA Initiation Workflow
  1. Identify the service or medication requiring PA
  2. Verify payer PA requirements (call insurer or check payer portal)
  3. Collect required clinical documentation: diagnosis codes, clinical notes, previous treatment failures, labs
  4. Submit PA request through appropriate channel (payer portal, fax, or phone)
  5. Document submission date and reference number in the patient chart
  6. Track pending PAs — follow up if no response within payer's stated timeline (typically 3–5 business days for standard; 24–72 hours for urgent)
  7. Notify the provider and patient of approval or denial
Documentation Requirements
Always Include
  • ICD-10 diagnosis code(s)
  • CPT procedure code(s)
  • Letter of medical necessity (provider signs)
  • Most recent clinical note supporting the request
  • Previous treatment history / step therapy documentation if required
Track in Chart
  • Submission date and reference number
  • Payer contact and case manager name if available
  • Expected decision date
  • Approval / denial outcome with reason
  • Any follow-up actions taken

📄 Documentation & Scanning Standards

Charting standards, document categorization, date of service rules, and diagnosis coding accuracy that apply to all Medical Assistants and VMAs.

⚖️
Neurological Clearance Language Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedStaff were repeatedly writing "patient is medically cleared" on forms and letters. This practice implies the neurology practice is clearing the patient for all aspects of surgery or procedures — including cardiac, pulmonary, anesthesia, and surgical risk — which is outside the scope of neurology.
❌Never write: "Patient is medically cleared" or any variation implying general medical clearance.
Correct Template Language — Always Use This Exact Wording
Standard Neurological Clearance Statement"From a neurological standpoint, there is no neurological contraindication to the planned procedure at this time."
When Risk Exists or More Information Is Needed"Neurological recommendations cannot be finalized until the requested records/details are reviewed."
Staff Workflow for All Clearance Forms
  1. Identify what procedure is being requested
  2. Confirm who is requesting the form (surgeon's office? anesthesia? PCP?)
  3. Confirm whether they are asking for general medical clearance or neurology-specific input
  4. If they want general medical clearance — redirect them to the appropriate provider (PCP, cardiology, etc.)
  5. If neurology input is appropriate — use the approved template language only
  6. Send to the provider for review and approval before faxing or releasing
  7. Save completed and signed form in chart under correct category
ℹ️Neurology can only comment on neurological contraindications. General medical clearance must come from PCP, cardiology, pulmonology, anesthesia, or the surgical team as appropriate.
🗂
Correct Chart Categorization & Document Labeling
▼
Problem IdentifiedDocuments were indexed under wrong chart tabs, new duplicate categories were being created, and inconsistent naming prevented clinical staff from finding documents quickly.
Approved Standard Categories — Use These Only
  • Consultations
  • Hospital Records
  • Labs
  • Imaging
  • Procedures
  • Administrative
  • Patient Education
Document Placement Guide
  • Hospital discharge summaries → Hospital Records
  • Emergency department notes → Hospital Records
  • Specialist consult notes → Consultations
  • Lab results → Labs
  • MRI / CT / X-ray reports → Imaging
  • EMG tables → Procedures
  • Patient handouts and summaries → Patient Education
  • Consent forms, insurance documents → Administrative
⚠️Do not create new categories. Do not create duplicate tabs. If you are unsure where a document belongs, ask before scanning.
📅
Correct Date of Service (DOS) Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedStaff were using today's date (the scan date) instead of the original Date of Service when indexing documents. This caused records to appear out of chronological order, making it difficult to review patient history accurately.
🚫Always use the original Date of Service from the document — never the date you scanned it.
  • Look at the document itself for the date of the service, visit, or result
  • For lab results — use the date the lab was drawn or resulted
  • For imaging — use the date the study was performed
  • For consult notes — use the date of the consult visit
  • For hospital records — use the date of discharge or the date of the specific note
  • If the DOS is unclear, check with your supervisor before indexing
👨‍⚕️
Physician Name Accuracy
▼
Problem IdentifiedPhysician names were repeatedly misspelled in documentation, including on referral letters and forms sent externally. Misspelled names create professionalism issues and can cause confusion with document matching.
  • Verify physician names before entering them in documentation
  • Use the facility directory, NPI lookup, or web search to confirm spelling
  • Do not guess — confirm before submitting any external document
  • Example of correct spelling: Dr. Okoye
  • When in doubt, call the physician's office to verify name spelling and fax number
💰
Diagnosis Coding & Billing Accuracy Critical — Claim Denials
▼
This Is a Financial Issue, Not Just a Documentation IssueIncorrect or incomplete diagnosis coding directly causes claim denials. When a VMA fails to add a new diagnosis the provider established during an encounter, or uses ICD-10 codes copied from a PCP referral without adding new diagnoses, claims will be denied or underbilled. This has a direct impact on practice revenue. Careless documentation is not acceptable.
Rule: Every Diagnosis the Provider Makes Must Be Coded in That Encounter
🚫Do not simply copy the ICD-10 codes from a PCP referral note and use them as the encounter diagnoses. The provider may have established a new or different diagnosis during this visit. That new diagnosis must be added.
❌ What Happened — Real Example
  • Provider evaluated a patient and diagnosed femoral neuropathy
  • The VMA used only the ICD-10 codes that came in from the PCP's referral note
  • The new diagnosis of femoral neuropathy was never added to the encounter
  • The claim was submitted without the correct diagnosis — it was denied
✅ What Should Have Happened
  • VMA reads the provider's note and identifies all diagnoses documented in this encounter
  • Any diagnosis new to this encounter is added to the problem list and encounter diagnoses
  • Femoral neuropathy → ICD-10: G57.20 (lesion of femoral nerve, unspecified lower limb) or laterality-specific code
  • If unsure of the correct code, ask the provider — do not skip it
EMG & EEG — Billable ICD-10 Codes Are Required
⚠️EMG and EEG procedures will be denied if the encounter does not contain a diagnosis code that is on the approved billable list for that procedure. If no such code is in the chart, the VMA must ask the provider before submitting.
EMG — Common Billable Diagnoses
  • G54.2 — Cervical root disorders
  • G54.4 — Lumbosacral root disorders
  • G56.0x — Carpal tunnel syndrome
  • G56.2x — Lesion of ulnar nerve
  • G57.0x — Sciatic nerve lesion
  • G57.20 — Lesion of femoral nerve
  • G60.0 — Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy
  • G62.9 — Polyneuropathy, unspecified
  • M54.12 — Radiculopathy, cervical
  • M54.16 — Radiculopathy, lumbar
  • M54.17 — Radiculopathy, lumbosacral
EEG — Common Billable Diagnoses
  • G40.x — Epilepsy and recurrent seizures (various)
  • G40.009 — Localization-related epilepsy, unspecified
  • G40.309 — Generalized idiopathic epilepsy, unspecified
  • R56.9 — Unspecified convulsions
  • R55 — Syncope and collapse (for differential)
  • G93.40 — Encephalopathy, unspecified
  • R41.3 — Other amnesia (confusion/altered mental status workup)
  • F44.5 — Conversion disorder with seizures
⚠️This list is not exhaustive. A complete payer-approved list of billable ICD-10 codes for EMG and EEG is maintained in the chart. Consult it before submitting. When in doubt — ask the provider. Never submit without a confirmed billable diagnosis code.
Diagnosis Coding Checklist — Every Encounter
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Read the provider's full note — identify every diagnosis documented in this encounter
  • ✓
    Do not simply copy ICD-10 codes from the referring PCP's note — add any new diagnoses the provider established today
  • ✓
    If an EMG or EEG was ordered or performed, confirm a billable ICD-10 code is present in the chart
  • ✓
    If no billable code is present for a procedure, ask the provider before submitting — do not skip it
  • ✓
    Add new diagnoses to the encounter problem list with the correct ICD-10 code
  • ✓
    Verify laterality is captured where required (right vs. left — e.g., femoral neuropathy, carpal tunnel, radiculopathy)
  • ✓
    If unsure of any code, consult the billable code list or ask the provider — never guess

✅ Chart Completion, Quality & Competency

End-of-encounter chart completion checklist, common MA errors, and Section 3 competency assessment.

✅
Chart Completion Checklist — Every Encounter
▼
Progress:
0 / 14
  • ✓
    All diagnoses from today's encounter entered in TalkEHR with correct ICD-10 codes
  • ✓
    Medication list verified and reconciled — including any changes made during this visit
  • ✓
    Allergy list confirmed with patient and updated if needed
  • ✓
    Lab orders placed with correct diagnosis codes — requisition sent to patient
  • ✓
    Imaging orders placed — prior auth initiated if required, requisition provided
  • ✓
    Referrals initiated — fax sent, receipt confirmation documented
  • ✓
    Prior authorization initiated for any new medication, procedure, or service requiring PA
  • ✓
    Patient handouts and education materials uploaded to chart and provided to patient
  • ✓
    EMG table printed, scanned, and verified if EMG was performed today
  • ✓
    All documents scanned under correct category with correct Date of Service
  • ✓
    Follow-up appointment scheduled — including controlled medication follow-up if applicable
  • ✓
    DME or brace orders placed and processed if ordered today
  • ✓
    Neurological clearance language correct — never wrote "medically cleared"
  • ✓
    Chart marked ready for provider signature only after all above steps are complete
⚠️
Common MA / VMA Errors — What to Avoid
▼
🚫Documentation Errors
  • Copying PCP's ICD-10 codes without adding new diagnoses from today's visit
  • Using unspecified codes when laterality is documented
  • Using today's date instead of the original Date of Service when scanning
  • Creating new chart categories instead of using the approved 7 categories
  • Writing "patient is medically cleared" on neurological clearance forms
  • Marking charts ready for signature before EMG table is uploaded and verified
⚠️Workflow Errors
  • Failing to flag abnormal lab results for provider review same day
  • Sending referrals without confirming receipt
  • Processing controlled substance refills without verifying a follow-up appointment is scheduled
  • Interrupting the provider during a patient encounter for non-urgent tasks
  • Initiating PA only after service is rendered (too late)
  • Failing to notify provider of a repeated PA denial
  • Missing infusion medication details during medication reconciliation
  • Failing to enroll patients who cannot afford specialty medications in PAPs
⭐
Section 3 KPIs & Competency Assessment
▼
Key Performance Indicators — Medical Assistants & VMAs
📊 Chart Quality
  • All encounter diagnoses entered same day
  • Medication list reconciled at every visit
  • Zero "medically cleared" language in documentation
  • EMG table verified before chart marked ready for signature
⏱ Timeliness
  • Chart preparation complete before provider enters room
  • Abnormal lab results routed to provider same day
  • Referrals faxed and confirmed within 1 business day of order
  • PA initiated before service is rendered
🔄 Follow-Through
  • No open referrals >10 days without follow-up attempt
  • No open lab orders >7 days without follow-up
  • Controlled medication patients have scheduled follow-up appointments
  • PA denials escalated to provider within same day of receipt
Section 3 Competency Checklist — MA/VMA Sign-Off
Progress:
0 / 18
  • ✓
    Completed pre-visit chart preparation independently for a full clinic day
  • ✓
    Reconciled medication list including infusion therapies without errors
  • ✓
    Identified and documented all allergies with reaction type at a patient encounter
  • ✓
    Completed EMG workflow — printed table, scanned, verified, chart marked correctly
  • ✓
    Entered all encounter diagnoses with correct ICD-10 codes after reading provider note
  • ✓
    Placed lab orders with correct diagnosis codes and requisition sent to patient
  • ✓
    Followed up on pending labs — contacted lab for overdue results
  • ✓
    Placed imaging orders including PA initiation where required
  • ✓
    Used correct neurological clearance language — no "medically cleared" used
  • ✓
    Initiated a referral correctly — faxed, confirmed receipt, documented in chart
  • ✓
    Initiated a prior authorization proactively before service was rendered
  • ✓
    Escalated a PA denial to provider same day with full denial details
  • ✓
    Used OpenEvidence to verify a clinical question and documented appropriately
  • ✓
    Enrolled or assisted a patient with a Patient Assistance Program application
  • ✓
    Applied interrupt protocol correctly — held non-urgent items for between patients
  • ✓
    Confirmed controlled medication patient had follow-up scheduled before releasing refill
  • ✓
    Scanned documents under correct category with correct Date of Service
  • ✓
    Completed Section 3 Chart Completion Checklist without prompting for a full clinic day
ℹ️Section 3 Key Principle: "An MA who catches a problem before the provider sees the patient is the most valuable person in the room." Chart prep, medication reconciliation, and diagnosis accuracy are not administrative tasks — they are clinical quality work. Hold yourself to that standard every day.

🧠 MS Patient Monitoring Protocols

Required standards for Multiple Sclerosis patients — visit frequency, controlled medications, disease-modifying therapy (DMT) monitoring, and prior authorization.

⚕
MS Visit Frequency & Controlled Medication Rules Required
▼
MS patients must be seen regularly. Controlled medications cannot be refilled without an in-person visit.
Visit Frequency Standards
  • MS patients must be seen every 3 months as the standard
  • Every 6 months is only acceptable if the patient is stable AND not taking any disease-modifying medication
  • Patients on disease-modifying therapy (Ocrevus, Tysabri, Briumvi, Aubagio, Tecfidera, etc.) must be seen every 3 months without exception
Controlled Medications — In-Office Visit Required
  • Modafinil (Provigil) is a Schedule IV controlled substance
  • Patients must be seen in the office before a controlled medication can be prescribed or refilled
  • Do not refill or issue controlled medications for patients who have not been seen — regardless of how long they have been a patient
  • If a patient has not been seen since their last visit and requests a controlled medication: schedule an appointment first
🔬
Disease-Modifying Therapy (DMT) — CBC Monitoring & PA Requirements Required
▼
Any patient on a disease-modifying drug for MS must have a CBC every 3 months. These medications suppress the immune system and reduce the absolute lymphocyte count — which must be monitored to prevent dangerous immunosuppression.
CBC Monitoring — Every 3 Months
  • Required for all patients on MS disease-modifying therapies: Ocrevus, Tysabri, Briumvi, Aubagio, Tecfidera, Gilenya, Mayzent, Kesimpta, and others
  • The absolute lymphocyte count must be reviewed before each infusion and at 3-month intervals
  • If a patient does not have a recent CBC on file, do not proceed — order the labs and reschedule if necessary
  • Flag any absolute lymphocyte count below the normal threshold for immediate provider review
Prior Authorization — Must Be Confirmed Before Continuing
  • Any patient continuing on a disease-modifying drug must have an active, documented prior authorization in the chart
  • "The patient says they've been on it" is not sufficient — the PA must be on file
  • If a new patient reports they were on Ocrevus (or any DMT): confirm PA status before scheduling an infusion or continuation
  • Track PA expiration dates — begin re-authorization well before expiration (see Prior Authorization tab)
⚠️A patient was continued on Ocrevus with no documented prior authorization and no CBC on file. This is a serious compliance and safety risk. Every MS patient on a DMT must have both a current CBC and a valid PA documented before the next dose is administered.

🤕 Headache & Migraine Program

Medication management, in-office treatments, prior authorization, patient assistance, and urgent result protocols for headache and migraine patients.

Key Principle

Headache care requires timely treatment, careful medication management, and proactive follow-up. Delays in prior authorization, missed prescriptions, or failure to act on urgent findings can significantly affect patient outcomes. Every headache patient should receive prompt, coordinated, and evidence-based care.

📌
Headache & Migraine Policies Required
▼
In-Office Treatment — Toradol (Ketorolac)
  • Offer office Toradol injections for patients with severe headaches or migraines when ordered by the provider
  • Screen for contraindications before administering: anticoagulant use, significant kidney disease, NSAID allergy, active gastrointestinal bleeding
  • Oral Toradol may be used when clinically appropriate per provider instructions
  • Keep Toradol and injection supplies in stock at all times — check inventory regularly and reorder before running low
🚨 Temporal Arteritis — Urgent ESR / CRP
  • In any patient with suspected temporal arteritis (giant cell arteritis), ESR and CRP must be reviewed urgently
  • Elevated results must be brought to the provider's attention immediately
  • Delayed treatment of temporal arteritis can lead to permanent vision loss or stroke — this is a true clinical emergency
  • Do not file these results without provider review — escalate the same day they are received
Formulary & Prior Authorization
  • Check the patient's prescription insurance formulary before prescribing high-cost migraine medications — do not assume coverage
  • Medicare plans vary significantly — do not assume all Medicare plans cover the same medications
  • If a medication is covered but requires PA (e.g., Nurtec), prepare the required documentation and initiate the PA process promptly
  • Track all pending PAs until a decision is received (see PA tab for tracking protocol)
Patient Assistance Programs
  • Use the correct manufacturer assistance form for each medication:
  • Emgality → Lilly assistance program
  • Ubrelvy → AbbVie assistance program
  • Track all patient assistance applications until approval, denial, or final resolution — do not submit and forget
  • Patients with high copays may be directed to manufacturer copay card programs or other affordability options when appropriate
💊
Medications Commonly Used — Headache & Migraine
▼

Be familiar with these medications — their coverage requirements, PA needs, and patient assistance programs. Formulary status varies by plan and changes frequently.

Nurtec (rimegepant) Ubrelvy (ubrogepant) Ajovy (fremanezumab) Emgality (galcanezumab) Qulipta (atogepant) Botox (onabotulinumtoxinA) Toradol — Office Injection Toradol — Oral Rescue
ℹ️For Emgality loading dose and two-prescription protocol, see the Prior Authorization tab — Specialty Drug Prescription Notes section.
🚫
Headache & Migraine — Common Errors to Avoid
▼
  • Prescribing without checking formulary — patient receives a prescription they cannot afford or that requires a PA you didn't initiate
  • Assuming all Medicare plans cover the same medications — formularies differ significantly across Medicare Part D plans
  • Failing to initiate prior authorization — medication gets denied because PA was never started
  • Using the wrong manufacturer assistance form — each manufacturer has a different program; using the wrong one wastes time and delays access
  • Not following up on patient assistance paperwork — applications submitted but never tracked to resolution
  • Missing urgent ESR/CRP results in a patient with suspected temporal arteritis — failure to escalate can result in permanent vision loss
  • Forgetting to send prescribed migraine medications — prescription discussed at the visit but never transmitted to the pharmacy
  • Not documenting medication discussions or treatment plans — verbal conversations that are never charted create liability gaps
✅
Headache & Migraine Daily Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Reviewed today's headache/migraine patients — prescribed medications confirmed sent to pharmacy
  • ✓
    Formulary and PA requirements checked for any new or changed prescriptions
  • ✓
    Pending prior authorizations and patient assistance applications reviewed — status updated in tracking sheet
  • ✓
    Toradol and injection supplies confirmed in stock
  • ✓
    Abnormal headache-related lab results reviewed (especially ESR/CRP) — provider notified of urgent findings
  • ✓
    Follow-up appointments confirmed for patients awaiting PA decisions or medication access
  • ✓
    Patient education documented — medication instructions and treatment plan discussed and charted
📊
Headache & Migraine Quality Indicators (KPIs)
▼
Migraine Medication Approval Rate
% of prescribed migraine medications successfully approved (through formulary, PA, or assistance program)
PA Turnaround Time
Average time from PA initiation to decision for migraine-related medications
Patient Assistance Approval Rate
% of submitted patient assistance applications resulting in approval
Prescription Accuracy
Correct medication, dose, and quantity sent to the correct pharmacy with no errors
ESR / CRP Follow-Up Compliance
% of ESR/CRP results in headache patients escalated to provider same day — critical for temporal arteritis screening
Rescue Treatment Availability
Toradol and injection supplies in stock — no missed in-office treatment opportunities due to supply gaps
Patient Satisfaction — Headache Management
Patient-reported satisfaction with medication access, treatment promptness, and care coordination
Overall Quality Principle — Closed-Loop Mandate

Every patient care task — including laboratory orders, imaging, referrals, medical record requests, provider communications, prescriptions, and follow-up recommendations — must follow a closed-loop workflow.

An order, request, or recommendation is not considered complete until it has been performed, documented, verified, and appropriately followed up. This principle is the foundation of all clinical and operational processes within Mir Neurology.

📋
CPT Code Reference — Procedures & Billing
▼

Quick reference for codes used in MA workflows. Always verify with the billing team before submitting. Part B drugs (J-codes) require medical PA, not pharmacy PA.
