💊
Schedule II Prescription Limits & Prior Authorization by Payer Maryland Reference
▼

Last updated: July 2026  ·  Verify against each payer's live formulary at least annually — plan edits change with the plan year.

Several Maryland payers — especially Medicare Advantage plans — will only cover a short initial fill of a Schedule II (CII) prescription (typically a 7-day supply) and require prior authorization or a coverage determination for anything beyond that. Use this section to set patient expectations and start the PA early.

⚠️
This mostly affects OPIOIDS, not stimulants. The 7-day limits below target opioid prescriptions for acute pain in opioid-naïve patients. Schedule II stimulants neurology commonly prescribes (methylphenidate, amphetamine salts, dextroamphetamine, lisdexamfetamine — for ADHD/narcolepsy) are generally not subject to the 7-day acute-pain edit, though they may carry their own quantity limits or PA on specific formularies. Always confirm on the member's active formulary.
How the Limits Stack — Three Layers (check all three)
Layer 1 — Federal (Medicare)

CMS requires every Medicare Advantage–PD and Part D plan to run the same opioid point-of-sale safety edits. This is why CareFirst, UnitedHealthcare, Aetna, and Humana MA plans all behave identically for opioids.

Layer 2 — Maryland State Law

Applies to the prescriber regardless of payer — lowest effective dose, quantity no greater than needed for the expected duration of pain.

Layer 3 — Plan / Formulary Edits

Commercial and Medicaid plans layer on their own quantity limits and PA criteria (MME thresholds, early-refill blocks, etc.).

Layer 1 — Federal: CMS Medicare Part D Opioid Safety Edits (CY 2026)

Applies to ALL Medicare Advantage (MA-PD) and standalone Part D plans in Maryland — CareFirst, UnitedHealthcare, Aetna, Humana, Wellpoint, Kaiser, and any other MA plan a patient carries.

Key Edits
  • 7-day hard edit: Initial opioid fill for acute pain in an opioid-naïve patient is limited to 7-day supply. More than 7 days rejects at the pharmacy — requires coverage determination (PA)
  • 90 MME/day care-coordination edit: Soft edit prompting pharmacist review when cumulative daily morphine milligram equivalents reach 90
  • 200 MME/day hard edit: Some plans block fills at/above 200 MME/day pending PA
Exemptions (edit does NOT apply)
  • Hospice care
  • Cancer-related pain
  • Palliative / end-of-life care
  • Long-term-care residents (per plan)
📝Document the exemption in the chart — it removes the edit at the pharmacy.
Layer 2 — Maryland State Law (All Payers)

Md. Health Occupations §1-223 (HB 1432, 2017). When treating pain, the prescriber must order the lowest effective dose of an opioid and a quantity no greater than needed for the expected duration of the pain.

Exemptions from the prescribing limit: substance-use-disorder treatment, cancer pain, hospice/palliative/end-of-life care, and chronic pain. Violations are grounds for Board discipline.

Layer 3 — Payer-Specific Rules: Medicare Advantage Plans

All MA plans follow the federal 7-day opioid-naïve edit above. Plan-specific details below.

PlanSchedule II LimitPrior Authorization
CareFirst BCBS Medicare AdvantageOpioid-naïve fill >7-day supply rejects; pharmacist may dispense 7 daysPA/coverage determination for >7 days; also for >90 MME or >14 days. Post-limit approvals up to 200 MME/day and 30-day supply
UnitedHealthcare Medicare Advantage (2026)Opioid limited to 7-day supply if no recent opioid history; cumulative MME limit appliesPrescriber may request coverage of additional quantity
Aetna / Humana / Other MA-PD PlansSame CMS 7-day opioid-naïve edit (federal requirement)Coverage determination for quantities beyond the edit
Layer 3 — Payer-Specific Rules: Commercial Plans
PlanSchedule II LimitPrior Authorization
CareFirst BCBS (Commercial)Opioid-naïve fill >7-day supply rejects (7-day allowed)PA for additional quantity; also for >90 MME or >14 days. Post-limit up to 200 MME/day, 30-day supply

Commercial plans vary by employer group and formulary — verify each member's benefit. CII stimulant quantity limits, when present, are formulary-specific.

Layer 3 — Maryland Medicaid HealthChoice MCOs
Statewide Medicaid Rule (FFS and All MCOs)
  • PA required for opioid-naïve patient receiving >7 days or >50 MME/day
  • PA required for any opioid regimen exceeding 90 MME/day
  • Immediate-release opioids need PA beyond 7 days (acute pain) or 30 days (chronic pain)
  • Early refills of controlled medications are generally not authorized
MCOSchedule II LimitPrior Authorization / Notes
Aetna Better Health of MarylandState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
CareFirst Community Health Plan MDIR opioid >7 days (acute pain) rejectsPA approves up to 1-month supply
Jai Medical SystemsState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Kaiser PermanenteState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Maryland Physicians CareState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
MedStar Family ChoiceState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editNo early refills of controlled meds (since 1/1/2019); exceptions: cancer, sickle cell, hospice/palliative
Priority PartnersState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
UnitedHealthcare Community PlanState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Wellpoint MarylandState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Clinical & Front-Desk Workflow
  • At scheduling or check-in, identify the patient's plan type (Medicare Advantage, commercial, or Medicaid MCO)
  • For any opioid-naïve patient starting a CII opioid for acute pain, expect a 7-day fill — start the PA/coverage determination at the same visit if a longer supply is clinically needed
  • For CII stimulants (ADHD/narcolepsy), the 7-day acute-pain edit generally does not apply — but check the member's formulary for quantity limits or step/PA requirements
  • Document the exemption (cancer, hospice, palliative, chronic pain, SUD) when one applies — it removes the edit at the pharmacy
  • Keep this list current — verify each payer's rule against its live formulary at least annually; plan edits change with the plan year
Sources
  • Federal / CMS: CY 2026 Opioid Safety Edit Submission Instructions; AMCP summary
  • Maryland law: Md. Health Occupations §1-223; HB 1432 (2017)
  • CareFirst: Opioids IR MME Limit & Post-Limit PA criteria; CareFirst Medicare drug-management programs; CareFirst CHPMD pharmaceutical management
  • UnitedHealthcare: 2026 Complete Drug List (Formulary), Medicare Advantage
  • Maryland Medicaid: Pharmacy Program Quantity Limits; MedStar Family Choice opioid PA requirements; HealthChoice MCO list
🎓
Medical Assistants Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Medical Assistants Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

📅 Scheduling Department Training

Section 4 — Official training guide for all Scheduling staff. Covers new patient and follow-up scheduling, clinical prioritization, procedure scheduling (EMG, Botox, infusions, CA, biopsy), referral tracking, no-show management, PA status review, insurance verification, and daily operations.

Section 4 RequiredEMG TimingBiopsy RulesMVA SeparationReferral UrgencyProcedure ProtocolsBotox SchedulingInfusion SchedulingCognitive AssessmentPatient PrioritizationNo-Show Workflow
📋
Scheduler Responsibilities Overview
▼

Scheduling is not simply booking appointments. Schedulers must understand clinical priorities, provider preferences, referral urgency, follow-up intervals, and patient needs. Every appointment should reflect the patient's clinical needs.

Appointment Types
  • New patient scheduling
  • Follow-up scheduling
  • Procedure scheduling
  • Cognitive Assessment scheduling
  • EMG / EEG scheduling
  • Botox scheduling
  • Infusion scheduling
  • Radiology scheduling
  • Referral appointment scheduling
Ongoing Duties
  • Appointment confirmations
  • No-show follow-up
  • Cancellation management
  • Patient reminder calls
  • Referral tracking
  • Prior Authorization status review
  • Maintaining provider schedules
Coordination & Escalation
  • Procedure timing requirements (EMG, biopsy, EEG)
  • MVA intake and workflow requirements
  • Referral urgency — when to escalate immediately
  • Communicate scheduling conflicts to clinical staff promptly
  • Confirm insurance eligibility for all scheduled visits
  • Notify provider if urgent referral cannot be seen within appropriate timeframe
📌
Scheduling Principles & Patient Prioritization
▼
Core Scheduling Principles — Always Apply
ℹ️Always schedule based on clinical urgency, provider instructions, established office protocols, and patient safety. Never schedule simply because a slot is available.
✅ Always Schedule Based On
  • Clinical urgency of the patient's condition
  • Provider's specific instructions or protocols
  • Established office scheduling policies
  • Patient safety considerations
Follow-Up Intervals — Provider Protocols
  • Controlled substances → Every 3 months
  • Oxycodone / Hydrocodone → Every other month (practice policy)
  • High-dose Lyrica → Every 6 months
  • Parkinson disease → Usually within 3 months
  • Cognitive impairment → Annual CA or every 6 months per provider
  • MS on DMT → Per provider and monitoring schedule
High-Priority Patients — Bring to Provider Attention if Delayed
🚨The following patient types must not wait in standard scheduling queues. If an appropriate appointment cannot be offered promptly, notify the provider or MA immediately.
🔴 Urgent — Prioritize
  • Stroke or TIA patients
  • Suspected Multiple Sclerosis (new presentation)
  • New neurological deficits
  • Severe headaches with concerning features
  • Urgent abnormal MRI or CT findings
  • Patients requiring urgent referrals
🟡 Prioritize — Time-Sensitive
  • Controlled medication patients due for follow-up
  • High-dose Lyrica patients requiring monitoring
  • Patients with significant abnormal laboratory results
  • Patients awaiting urgent imaging or procedures
⚡
EMG Scheduling — Injury Timing Rule Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedEMGs were being scheduled within days of an injury. Studies performed too early after nerve injury will not show abnormal findings, resulting in wasted visits, unreliable results, and possible incorrect clinical decisions.
⏱️EMGs must not be scheduled earlier than 11–14 days after injury. This is a clinical requirement, not a preference.
Expectations
  • Always ask the patient for their exact date of injury before scheduling
  • Calculate the number of days between injury and proposed appointment
  • If the appointment would fall before day 11, reschedule to day 14 or later
  • If the injury date is unknown, hold the EMG slot until it is confirmed
  • Educate patients who request earlier appointments — explain the clinical reason
Patient Communication — When They Push for Earlier"The provider recommends waiting at least 11–14 days after your injury for the EMG to give accurate results. Scheduling earlier may mean the test needs to be repeated. We want to make sure we get useful information for your care."
Consultation-First — Do NOT Schedule EMG For
🚫EMG is not the appropriate first step for the following presentations. Schedule a consultation visit first; the provider will order EMG if indicated.
  • Suspected muscular dystrophy
  • Known muscular dystrophy
  • Suspected inherited myopathies
🔬
Skin Punch Biopsy — Day & Time Rules Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedBiopsies were being scheduled on Thursdays and Fridays. The specimen lab is closed on Fridays and specimens take 24 hours to arrive — meaning a Thursday biopsy would arrive Saturday when the lab is closed, and a Friday biopsy cannot be shipped at all. This caused specimen loss and wasted procedures.
🚫Never schedule a skin punch biopsy on Thursday or Friday. The lab is closed Fridays and specimens take 24 hours to arrive — a Thursday specimen arrives Saturday (lab closed). The specimen becomes unusable.
  • Schedule Monday, Tuesday, or Wednesday only
  • Schedule in the morning — confirm the exact time window with clinical staff
  • Specimen must be shipped same day — the lab is closed on Fridays and transit takes 24 hours
  • If no morning slot is available Monday–Wednesday, schedule the following week rather than using Thursday, Friday, or an afternoon slot
  • Inform the patient of the scheduling restriction when booking
Scheduling Prep — Notify & Verify Before the Visit
  • Reserve approximately 30 minutes for the biopsy appointment
  • Notify the MA — advance notice is required to prepare the procedure room and supplies
  • Confirm the biopsy kit is available and all supplies are stocked before the patient arrives
  • Always indicate "Small Fiber Skin Biopsy" explicitly in the appointment type and scheduling notes
🚗
MVA Visit Scheduling Important
▼
Problem IdentifiedMVA and routine neurology visits were being scheduled together without separation. This created documentation confusion and billing issues because MVA billing flows through auto insurance, not health insurance.
Scheduling Rules for MVA Visits
  • Keep MVA visits separate from routine neurology visits whenever scheduling allows
  • Keep EEGs and procedures separate from routine office visits
  • Use the correct visit type when entering the appointment
Required Information to Collect at Scheduling
  • Date of accident
  • Auto insurance claim number
  • Insurance carrier name
  • Adjuster name and contact
  • Attorney name and fax (if represented)
⚠️If any MVA intake information is missing at scheduling, flag the appointment for Front Desk to complete at check-in. Do not allow the visit to proceed without this information.
📘
Scheduling SOPs — Purpose & Scope
▼

To ensure all appointments scheduled for Dr. Mir are properly verified, clinically appropriate, and supported with complete medical records, insurance verification, and patient communication prior to the visit.

📋 New Patient Visits

Referral verification, myelopathy screening, records collection, demographics, portal setup, and appointment confirmation.

🔄 Established Patient Follow-Ups

Reason for visit, clinical screening, testing completion verification, referral tracking, ER records, and appointment typing.

🏥 Insurance & Eligibility

Coverage verification, in-network status, MVA/Workers' Comp screening, and financial responsibility disclosure.

🆕
New Patient Visit SOP Step-by-Step
▼
A. Referral Verification — Before Confirming the Appointment
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Verify referral has been received
  • ✓
    Verify referring provider name and specialty
  • ✓
    Confirm diagnosis / reason for referral
  • ✓
    Ensure referral authorization is valid (if required by insurance)
  • ✓
    Review referral for critical lab values, critical imaging findings, severe neurological deficits, or emergency-level symptoms
  • ✓
    Prioritize patients with urgent concern or urgent consideration noted by the referring physician
  • ✓
    Prioritize patients scheduled for upcoming surgery — assist them before their procedure date
🚨If referral indicates emergency-level symptoms, inform the patient to visit the ER immediately — do not schedule a routine appointment for an emergency presentation.
⚠️ Myelopathy Screening — Critical Exclusion
Dr. Mir's office does NOT see myelopathy patients.Screen every new patient referral before scheduling. If any of the following are present, escalate to the provider or supervisor before confirming the appointment — do not schedule independently.
🚫 Screen for These Symptoms
  • Severe gait instability
  • Loss of balance
  • Loss of bowel or bladder control
  • Progressive weakness
  • History of cervical cord compression
If Any Are Present
  • Do not confirm the appointment
  • Escalate to provider or supervisor immediately
  • Document concern in scheduling notes
  • Await provider decision before proceeding
B. Records Collection
🧪 Last Lab Location
  • Ask patient: Where were last labs completed?
  • Ask patient: Approximate completion date?
  • Request or fetch records if unavailable
🖼️ Last Imaging Location
  • Ask: Facility name, type of imaging, date completed
  • Types: MRI, CT, X-ray, EMG/NCS
  • Request reports and images if needed
C. Patient Demographics Verification
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    PCP information (name, practice, fax)
  • ✓
    Pharmacy information
  • ✓
    Phone number and address verified
  • ✓
    Date of birth confirmed
  • ✓
    Alternative contact number collected and documented (appreciated whenever possible)
  • ✓
    Updated medication list requested — remind patient to bring it to the visit
D. Patient Portal
Progress:
0 / 3
  • ✓
    Invite patient to the patient portal
  • ✓
    Confirm email address and/or mobile number for portal activation
  • ✓
    Document portal invitation status in EMR
E. Appointment Confirmation
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Confirm appointment date, time, and location with patient
  • ✓
    Inform patient of required documents to bring (ID, insurance card, medication list)
  • ✓
    Advise arrival time (typically 15 minutes early for new patients)
  • ✓
    Document confirmation in EMR using standard template
🔄
Established Patient / Follow-Up SOP All Follow-Ups
▼
A. Determine Reason for Appointment

Always specify the visit type — never schedule as a generic "follow-up" without documenting the reason.

  • Follow-up visit
  • Procedure
  • Consultation
  • Lab review
  • Imaging review
  • Medication discussion
  • Worsening symptoms
  • Paperwork / disability / FMLA issues
B. Clinical Screening
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Ask: Any worsening symptoms? Any new neurological symptoms?
  • ✓
    Ask: Any recent ER or hospital visits?
  • ✓
    Ask: Any recent surgery or procedure?
  • ✓
    Document all screening responses clearly in scheduling notes
C. Verify Ordered Testing Completion
✅ Confirm Completion Of
  • Blood work / labs
  • Imaging (MRI, CT, X-ray)
  • Outside referrals
If Not Completed — Verify Reason
  • Insurance delay
  • Scheduling issue
  • Patient declined
  • Transportation issue
Patient Education Script — Incomplete Testing"The testing was ordered by the provider for an important clinical reason and is necessary for a proper follow-up evaluation. We strongly encourage you to complete it before your visit so the provider has the information needed to help you."
D. Referral Tracking — Track Every Referral Until Complete

If the patient was referred to another provider, verify the status before the follow-up visit. Never assume a referral has been completed.

🔄Referral Workflow: Referral Ordered → Referral Sent → Receipt Confirmed → Appointment Scheduled → Patient Seen → Consultation Notes Received → Provider Review
Action Items
  • Verify whether referred appointment was completed
  • Request consultation notes if not yet received
  • Fetch outside records from: pain management, neurosurgery, physical therapy, PCP, other specialists
E. ER / Hospital Records
  • If patient had recent ER visit or hospitalization, obtain hospital name and date
  • Request records before the appointment whenever possible
F. Referral & Insurance Updates
Progress:
0 / 3
  • ✓
    Verify whether patient has a new referral or updated authorization
  • ✓
    Verify whether patient has a new insurance plan — update records accordingly
  • ✓
    Schedule appropriate visit type: procedure, consultation, follow-up, or in-office
🏥
Insurance & Eligibility Verification SOP
▼
A. Insurance Verification
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Confirm insurance company name
  • ✓
    Confirm Member ID and Group Number
  • ✓
    Confirm subscriber information (patient vs. policy holder)
  • ✓
    Verify active coverage and effective dates
  • ✓
    Verify provider participation / in-network status
  • ✓
    Verify referral and authorization requirements
C. MVA / Workers' Compensation & Fall History Screening
🚗Always ask whether the visit is related to a Motor Vehicle Accident, a Workers' Compensation claim, or a fall that occurred at work or on someone else's property. These require separate billing and intake workflows.
🚶Fall History: If a patient's visit involves a fall, always ask where the fall occurred. A fall at work may be a Workers' Compensation case. A fall at a place of business may involve premises liability. Flag these to the provider — do not schedule as a routine visit without noting the circumstance.
If MVA or Work Injury — Collect
  • Date of accident or fall
  • Location of fall (home, work, store, public property)
  • Auto insurance claim number (if MVA)
  • Auto insurance carrier name (if MVA)
  • Adjuster name and contact information
  • Attorney name and fax (if patient is represented)
D. Financial Responsibility Disclosure

Inform patient in advance and document notification.

  • Copays due at time of service
  • Deductibles and coinsurance amounts
  • Self-pay balances (if applicable)
  • Outstanding balances on account
📋
Workers' Compensation Intake Form Print & Complete
▼
⚠️Complete this form for every Workers' Compensation visit. All fields must be filled before the patient leaves. Missing information causes billing failures and legal documentation gaps.

WORKERS' COMPENSATION INTAKE FORM
Mir Neurology & Spine Center · (301) 797-7600
Patient Information
Workers' Compensation Insurance Information
Insurance Adjuster Information
★ If Applicable — Attorney Information
📝
Documentation Standards & EMR Templates
▼

All staff must document the following for every appointment interaction:

Required Documentation
  • Records requested and records received
  • Appointment confirmation
  • Patient communication (calls, texts, portal)
  • Insurance verification status
  • Any clinical concerns escalated
Also Document
  • Reason for visit specifics
  • Testing completion status
  • Portal invitation status
  • Medication list reminder given
  • MVA / Workers' Comp intake if applicable
Standard EMR Documentation Templates — Copy & Paste
Appointment Confirmed
"Appointment confirmed. Confirmation text sent to patient."
Records Requested
"Requested labs/imaging records from outside facility."
Testing Incomplete
"Patient has not completed the ordered testing. Importance of testing explained to patient."
Insurance Verified
"Insurance eligibility and in-network status verified."
Portal Invitation
"Patient invited to patient portal."
Medication List Reminder
"Patient instructed to bring updated medication list to appointment."
🚨
Escalation Guidelines — Escalate Immediately Critical
▼
Do Not Schedule — Escalate FirstIf any of the following are identified during scheduling, stop and escalate to the provider or supervisor before confirming any appointment. Do not proceed independently.
🚨 Escalate Immediately If
  • Critical imaging findings in referral (e.g., acute stroke, mass, cord compression)
  • Severe neurological deficits documented by referring provider
  • Suicidal ideation expressed by patient
  • Emergency-level symptoms described
  • Incomplete referral with urgent diagnosis
  • Suspected myelopathy (see Myelopathy Screening above)
Escalation Steps
  • Do not confirm the appointment
  • Notify provider or supervisor immediately — same call if possible
  • Document the concern and escalation in scheduling notes with time stamp
  • Await provider instruction before proceeding
  • If patient is in immediate danger, direct to ER or call 911
Urgent Referral Protocol — When Specialist Wait Time Is Too Long
⏱️If a specialist appointment cannot be offered within a timeframe appropriate for the patient's clinical condition, notify the provider immediately — do not simply book the next available slot.
  • Notify the provider immediately if the available specialist appointment is too far out for the patient's clinical condition
  • The provider may choose to contact the consultant directly to expedite — support this by providing the specialist's direct contact information
  • Maintain direct contact information for frequently used tertiary care specialists and their assistants
  • Document all communications regarding urgent referral escalation with date and time
📞
Appointment Confirmation Workflow Important
▼
Problem IdentifiedPatients were missing phone calls because they were at work, and staff were cancelling appointments unnecessarily as a result. This leads to lost patient visits and frustrated patients.
Expectations
  • Use text confirmations as the primary confirmation method whenever possible
  • Phone calls should mainly be used for cancellations or rescheduling — not initial confirmations
  • Never cancel an appointment solely because the patient did not answer a phone call
  • Attempt multiple methods before considering a no-confirm cancellation
  • Document all confirmation attempts in the system
Training Points
  • Many patients work during business hours and cannot take calls
  • Text messages are seen faster and are less intrusive than calls
  • A confirmed appointment that doesn't cancel is always preferable to a preventive cancellation
  • If a patient truly cannot be reached, escalate to supervisor before cancelling
Required Documentation — Record One of the Following for Every Contact Attempt
  • Confirmed — patient confirmed the appointment
  • Left voicemail — message left, awaiting callback
  • No answer — no voicemail available or no response
  • Rescheduled — appointment moved at patient request
🗓
Procedure Scheduling Rules Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedProcedures were being scheduled at incorrect times of day and incorrect days of the week, resulting in specimen shipping failures and invalid EMG results.
Skin Punch Biopsy — Strict Rules
🚫Never schedule on Thursday or Friday. The specimen lab is closed Fridays and transit takes 24 hours — a Thursday specimen arrives Saturday (closed). The specimen becomes unusable.
  • Schedule Monday, Tuesday, or Wednesday only
  • Schedule as early in the morning as possible
  • Same-day shipping is required — lab is closed Fridays, transit is 24 hours
  • If no early Monday–Wednesday slot is available, reschedule to the following week — never use Thursday or a late-day slot
EMG After Injury — Timing Rule
⏱️EMGs scheduled too soon after injury will not show accurate nerve conduction findings. Results will be invalid.
  • EMGs should not be scheduled earlier than 11–14 days after injury
  • Always verify the exact date of injury before booking
  • If injury date is unknown, hold scheduling until confirmed
  • Communicate this rule to patients who push for earlier appointments
Appointment Type Separation
  • Keep MVA visits separate from routine neurology visits
  • Keep EEGs separate from routine office visits when possible
  • Procedures should have their own scheduling blocks
🧠
Cognitive Assessment (CA) Scheduling Important
▼
🚫Do not begin a Cognitive Assessment before the provider sees the patient unless specifically instructed. The provider's clinic flow takes priority.
Before Scheduling — Verify All 4 Items
  • Provider has ordered the CA — confirm in TalkEHR before scheduling
  • Prior Authorization has been approved (if required by the patient's insurance)
  • Appropriate appointment length is allocated — CA requires dedicated time blocks
  • CA encounter will be created in the system — confirm with MA that setup is correct
Notes for Appointment
  • Indicate "Cognitive Assessment" clearly in appointment type and notes
  • Confirm with the MA that the test battery is assigned before the appointment date
  • If PA is pending, do not schedule CA until PA is confirmed — check with PA team first
💉
Botox Scheduling Important
▼
Appointment Notes Must Include
Required Documentation in Notes
  • Dose (units)
  • Buy & Bill vs. Patient-Supplied Medication
  • Prior Authorization status (approved / pending / denied)
  • Medication availability (confirm medication is in stock or ordered)
Before Confirming the Appointment
  • Only schedule Botox with providers who are authorized to perform Botox injections
  • Confirm PA is approved before booking — do not assume it will be approved in time
  • If Buy & Bill: confirm medication has been ordered and will arrive before the appointment date
⚠️Never schedule a Botox appointment without documenting the dose, medication source, and PA status. Incomplete scheduling notes create last-minute cancellations and billing errors.
🩺
Infusion Scheduling
▼
Coordination Required

Infusion scheduling requires coordination with three parties: the Prior Authorization team, the infusion center, and the patient.

Prior Auth Team
  • Confirm PA is approved before scheduling infusion
  • Obtain PA approval date and authorization number
  • Verify how many infusions are approved and within what date range
Infusion Center
  • Confirm infusion center accepts patient's insurance
  • Verify they have the medication or will receive it
  • Obtain available appointment dates
Patient
  • Confirm patient availability and preferences
  • Provide infusion center name, address, and appointment date
  • Remind patient of any pre-infusion requirements (fasting, labs)
What to Track & Document
  • PA approval date — when was the authorization approved?
  • Infusion location — which infusion center?
  • Scheduled date — when is the infusion booked?
  • Next infusion due date — when is the next dose due? (e.g., Ocrevus every 6 months)
ℹ️Ocrevus scheduling information (and other infusion medications) should always be documented in the Prior Authorization Sheet. Do not rely on memory or informal notes.
🖼️
Radiology Scheduling
▼
Imaging Scheduling Workflow
  1. Schedule imaging promptly — imaging studies should receive scheduling priority
  2. Verify the facility accepts the patient's insurance before confirming
  3. Confirm the appointment with both the facility and the patient
  4. Notify the patient of appointment date, time, and any preparation instructions (contrast, fasting)
  5. Document the scheduled facility and date in the patient chart
🚫If imaging is denied by insurance — notify the provider immediately. Do not simply inform the patient; the provider needs to know so they can initiate a peer-to-peer review or appeal.
📵
No-Show & Cancellation Workflow
▼
When a Patient Cancels Repeatedly or No-Shows
  1. Document the cancellation or no-show in the scheduling system
  2. Notify the assigned MA
  3. The MA reviews the chart for: outstanding labs, abnormal results, pending imaging, open referrals, pending follow-up items
  4. If significant unresolved clinical issues are identified, the MA discusses with the provider
  5. Attempt to reschedule the patient — document all contact attempts
⚠️Patients with unresolved clinical issues (abnormal labs, overdue controlled med follow-ups, urgent referrals) must not simply be left without an appointment. Escalate to the MA or provider if a patient cannot be reached.
🤝
Communication with Other Departments & Customer Service
▼
Using Tracking Sheets

Scheduling staff should have access to and regularly consult three tracking sheets:

📋 Prior Authorization Sheet

Use to answer patient questions about PA status without transferring the call.

📋 Referral Tracking Sheet

Use to check the status of any outstanding referral instantly.

📋 Forms Tracking Sheet

Use to answer questions about pending forms, FMLA, or disability paperwork status.

ℹ️One-call resolution is the standard. When a patient calls about PA status, referral status, or forms — check the tracking sheet and answer them on that call. Avoid transferring the call whenever possible.
Cross-Department Coordination — Required Standards
Issue IdentifiedCommunication and coordination among the front desk, scheduling, MAs, and PA teams needs improvement. Workflow gaps are causing patient delays, scheduling errors, and missed prior authorizations that could have been prevented with earlier communication.
Front Desk → MAs
  • Notify MAs immediately when a patient is checked in and roomed
  • Flag any missing items (insurance, forms, records) before the patient reaches the room
  • Alert MAs to any patient concerns, complaints, or requests noted at check-in
Front Desk → Scheduling
  • Confirm appointment types are correct before patients arrive
  • Report any scheduling errors immediately — do not wait until the appointment date
  • Communicate provider-plan changes that affect future scheduling
Front Desk → PA Team
  • When a patient mentions a medication not covered or a PA denial at check-in, immediately flag it to the PA team — do not wait
  • Notify the PA team of any formulary restriction identified during insurance verification
  • Proactive early communication prevents treatment delays
Customer Service Standards
✅ Always
  • Listen carefully and verify the patient's concern
  • Resolve the issue on the call whenever possible
  • Communicate clearly and professionally
❌ Avoid Saying
  • "I don't know."
  • "Someone else will call you back."

Instead: "I'll check the tracking sheet and update you right now."

✅ Daily Operations, Quality & Competency

Daily schedule review checklist, common scheduling errors, KPIs, and Section 4 competency sign-off.

📅
Daily Schedule Review Checklist
▼
Before Clinic Begins
Progress:
0 / 9
  • ✓
    Review provider schedules for the day
  • ✓
    Identify urgent patients and flag for provider/MA
  • ✓
    Review controlled medication follow-ups
  • ✓
    Review Cognitive Assessments scheduled today
  • ✓
    Review EMGs and any procedures on today's schedule
  • ✓
    Review Botox appointments — confirm dose/PA/medication notes are documented
  • ✓
    Review new patients — confirm records and referrals received
  • ✓
    Review outstanding referrals and PA status
  • ✓
    Notify provider or MA of any potential issues before clinic starts
During Clinic
  • Schedule follow-up appointments as patients are seen
  • Coordinate referrals ordered during today's clinic
  • Assist with urgent scheduling requests as they arise
  • Update PA and referral tracking sheets with new information
End of Day
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Confirm next-day appointments
  • ✓
    Review outstanding referrals — update tracking sheet
  • ✓
    Review today's cancellations and no-shows — notify MAs
  • ✓
    Notify MAs of any unresolved clinical issues from no-shows
  • ✓
    Update PA and referral tracking sheets with day's activity
⚠️
Common Scheduling Errors — What to Avoid
▼
🚫Procedure & Clinical Errors
  • Scheduling EMG for suspected or known muscular dystrophy or inherited myopathy patients before consultation
  • Scheduling EMG too soon after injury (before 11–14 days)
  • Scheduling skin biopsy on Thursday or Friday — lab closed Fridays, 24-hour transit means Thursday specimens arrive Saturday (lab closed)
  • Beginning Cognitive Assessment before provider evaluation
  • Scheduling Botox without documenting dose, PA status, and medication source
  • Scheduling infusion without confirmed PA approval
⚠️Operational & Communication Errors
  • Wrong provider assigned to appointment
  • Wrong appointment type selected — always review the provider's documented plan; if an EMG (e.g., lower extremity EMG) is ordered, schedule the EMG, not a routine follow-up
  • Not checking insurance participation before scheduling referral
  • Not documenting urgent referral escalation
  • Allowing urgent patients to wait months for specialist appointments without escalating
  • Transferring calls instead of checking tracking sheets
  • Cancelling appointments solely because patient did not answer a phone call
  • Scheduling referrals to out-of-network providers without provider/patient discussion
  • Calling a patient without first verifying their status — before calling, confirm the patient is not already checked in, roomed, or currently being seen; patients must never receive outbound calls from the office while in an exam room
  • Relying on memory for verbal provider instructions — any follow-up task from a verbal instruction must be documented in the chart or tracking system immediately
⭐
Section 4 KPIs & Competency Assessment
▼
Key Performance Indicators — Scheduling Department
📊 Access & Timeliness
  • New patient wait time
  • Time to specialist appointment (referral to appointment)
  • Imaging scheduling turnaround
  • Infusion scheduling turnaround
📋 Quality
  • Follow-up compliance rate
  • No-show rate
  • Referral completion rate
  • Appointment confirmation rate
  • Scheduling error rate
🤝 Patient Experience
  • One-call resolution rate
  • Patient satisfaction scores
  • Callback timeliness
Section 4 Competency Checklist — Scheduler Sign-Off
Progress:
0 / 14
  • ✓
    Scheduled all appointment types correctly (new patient, follow-up, EMG, CA, Botox, infusion, biopsy)
  • ✓
    Applied provider scheduling protocols and follow-up intervals without prompting
  • ✓
    Identified and appropriately prioritized urgent patients
  • ✓
    Applied EMG injury timing rule correctly — did not schedule before 11–14 days post-injury
  • ✓
    Applied biopsy scheduling rule — Monday–Wednesday, morning only (lab closed Fridays; 24-hour transit rules out Thursdays)
  • ✓
    Reviewed referral and PA tracking sheets to answer patient questions without transferring
  • ✓
    Coordinated referral scheduling — tracked from order to appointment confirmation
  • ✓
    Verified insurance participation before scheduling a referral or procedure
  • ✓
    Managed a no-show correctly — notified MA and documented contact attempts
  • ✓
    Escalated an urgent referral to provider when specialist wait time was clinically too long
  • ✓
    Communicated effectively with patients and staff — resolved issues on the call
  • ✓
    Maintained accurate scheduling documentation and notes in EMR
  • ✓
    Completed daily schedule review checklist before clinic started
  • ✓
    Demonstrated one-call resolution for PA, referral, and forms inquiries
ℹ️Section 4 Key Principle: "The Scheduling Department is responsible for timely access to care. Every appointment should reflect the patient's clinical needs, provider instructions, and practice protocols. Scheduling does not end when an appointment is booked — it ends when the patient has received the care they need."
⚠️
Operational Concerns & Quality Standards Quality Standard
▼
Issues IdentifiedScheduling errors have included: ordering wrong appointment types when providers documented a specific procedure; ordered tests (labs, imaging, referrals) not tracked through to completion; verbal provider instructions lost without documentation; patients called while already checked in and being seen; and complex neurological patients assigned to staff not yet approved to manage them.
1 · Order Tracking & Loop Closure
🚫A provider must never discover during an encounter that an ordered test was never sent, scheduled, or completed. Every open order must be tracked from placement to result.
  • Every laboratory, imaging, and referral order must be entered into the centralized tracking sheet at the time it is placed
  • Confirm: order was sent → appointment was scheduled → results were received — before the patient's follow-up visit
  • Review outstanding orders during pre-visit chart prep; flag any gaps to the provider or MA immediately
  • Every tracked item must have a defined owner, current status, and next follow-up date — not just an entry date
2 · Accurate Procedure Scheduling — Read the Provider's Plan
⚠️Do not default to a routine follow-up when the provider has documented a specific procedure. Scheduling the wrong appointment type wastes a visit and delays care.
  • Before scheduling any follow-up, review the provider's documented plan in the chart
  • If an EMG is ordered (e.g., lower extremity EMG), schedule the EMG — not a routine office visit
  • If EEG, imaging, or another study is ordered, schedule it at the same time as the follow-up visit, or as directed by the provider
  • When uncertain about the correct appointment type, confirm with the MA or provider — do not guess
3 · Document Provider Instructions — Never Rely on Memory
  • Any verbal instruction from the provider that requires follow-up action (e.g., "schedule a pre-op visit," "repeat the MRI in 6 weeks," "call the patient about their lab result") must be documented in the chart or tracking system immediately
  • Do not assume it will be remembered — document it before moving to the next task
  • Verbal instructions with no written record are lost; patient care consequences fall on the practice
4 · Patient Communication — Verify Status Before Calling
🚫Patients must never receive outbound calls from the office while they are in an exam room. Before calling any patient, verify their current status in the schedule.
  • Before placing any outbound call to a patient, check the schedule: is the patient checked in, roomed, or currently being seen?
  • If yes — do not call. Hold the task and address it after the visit concludes
  • This applies to all outbound calls: appointment reminders, PA status, lab results, referral updates
5 · Provider Assignment — Complex Patients Require Competency Approval
⚠️Newly hired Nurse Practitioners must not be scheduled to manage Multiple Sclerosis patients or other complex neurological cases until they have completed the required competency assessments and been explicitly approved to do so.
  • MS patients and other complex neurological cases require specialized training; do not assign these to a newly hired NP without supervisor approval
  • If you are unsure whether a provider is cleared to see a specific patient population, confirm with the supervisor before scheduling
  • Scheduling a complex patient with an unqualified provider — even inadvertently — creates a patient safety risk
6 · Communication & Accountability
  • Use standardized documentation and shared tracking systems for all orders, appointments, and follow-up tasks — personal notes and memory are not acceptable substitutes
  • Every task must have a defined owner, completion deadline, and documented status
  • Closed-loop communication is the standard — a task is not complete until it has been performed, confirmed, documented, and followed up
  • Clear responsibilities and shared visibility prevent delays, scheduling errors, and gaps in patient care
🎓
Schedulers Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Schedulers Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

📋 Referrals & Outside Records

Closed-loop referral tracking, urgent escalation protocols, specialist note communication, and special facility record workflows.

Closed-Loop TrackingUrgent EscalationDirect Call ProtocolPCP CommunicationFederal/VA RecordsConsult Notes
🔄
Closed-Loop Referral System Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedReferrals were being sent but not tracked. Urgent referrals were lost or delayed without provider awareness. Patients arrived at follow-up visits with no specialist notes because no one had confirmed they were ever seen.
🚫A referral is not complete when it is sent. It is only complete when all 5 steps below are done and documented.
The 5-Step Referral Completion Standard
  1. Referral sent — fax or portal submission confirmed, fax confirmation printed
  2. Appointment scheduled — confirm with patient that they received their appointment date
  3. Patient seen — confirm via follow-up call or patient report that the visit occurred
  4. Notes received — specialist note received via fax, portal, or mail
  5. Notes scanned correctly — filed under Consultation with correct DOS in patient chart
Training Points
  • Track every open referral in a log or tracking system — do not rely on memory
  • Review open referrals weekly and update statuses
  • If a referral is stuck at any step, follow up proactively
🚨
Urgent Referral Escalation Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedUrgent referrals were being placed into the same queue as routine referrals. Delays went unnoticed and providers were not informed, potentially delaying time-sensitive care.
🚫An urgent referral that cannot be scheduled within an appropriate timeframe must be escalated to the provider immediately — do not let it sit.
  • Urgent referrals require same-day or next-day follow-up — not routine weekly review
  • If the specialist cannot see the patient within the appropriate window, notify the provider immediately
  • Consider alternative specialists or facilities if the first choice has a long wait
  • Document all escalation actions with timestamps
  • Never silently accept a delayed appointment for an urgent referral without provider approval
📠
Specialist Notes & PCP Communication
▼
Problem IdentifiedSpecialist consult notes were missing from charts and PCPs were not consistently receiving copies of specialist notes, breaking the continuity of care loop.
Expectations
  • Obtain specialist consult notes promptly after each appointment
  • Ensure the PCP receives a copy of every specialist note
  • Verify PCP fax numbers regularly — they change frequently
  • If fax confirmation is not received within 2 business days, resend
  • Actively track all missing consult notes — do not wait for the provider to ask
🏛️
Federal Prison, VA & Special Facility Records
▼

These facilities have unique and often more complex record-release processes. Standard fax requests frequently fail.

Facilities Requiring Special Effort
  • Federal prisons
  • VA (Veterans Affairs) medical systems
  • Nursing homes / skilled nursing facilities
  • Inpatient rehabilitation facilities
Track for Each Facility
  • Correct department contact (not main number)
  • Verified fax number for medical records
  • Required authorization forms specific to that facility
  • Record release timeline expectations
  • Follow-up status and contact log
⚠️VA records requests often require specific VA authorization forms. Federal prison record requests may require inmate consent forms. Confirm requirements before sending.
🧲
MRI / Imaging Order Tracking
▼
Problem IdentifiedImaging centers reported not receiving MRI orders, yet no one had followed up to confirm receipt. Patients showed up for imaging appointments that could not be performed.
  1. Order sent to imaging center — print confirmation or note fax confirmation number
  2. Call imaging center within 24 hours to confirm receipt if no automatic confirmation
  3. Document patient's appointment date and location in chart
  4. After appointment date, follow up to confirm results were received
📞
Expediting Referrals — Provider Direct Call Protocol Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedPatients referred to tertiary care specialists were receiving appointments months out. No protocol existed for escalating when a patient needed to be seen sooner. In one case, the specialist never received the referral at all — discovered only after a direct provider-to-provider call.
If a referred patient's appointment is too far out and they need to be seen sooner, the provider must call the specialist directly. Do not rely solely on fax or portal — urgent timing issues require a personal call.
Protocol: When the Appointment Is Too Far Out
  1. Identify the gap early. When scheduling confirmation comes back, review the appointment date. If it is more than the clinically appropriate wait time for that patient's condition, flag it immediately — do not wait for the next visit.
  2. Notify the provider. Inform the provider that the appointment has been scheduled but may be too far out given the clinical urgency. Do not resolve this quietly — the provider needs to know.
  3. Provider calls the specialist directly. The provider (or the provider's MA acting on explicit instruction) calls the specialist or their office to request an earlier appointment. This carries significantly more weight than a staff-to-staff call.
  4. Confirm referral was received. During the same call or a separate call, confirm the specialist's office actually has the referral on file. Referrals are frequently not received despite a fax confirmation — always verify.
  5. Document the call. Record date, who was spoken to, what was discussed, and any new appointment date or promised callback.
Build a Direct Contact List for Frequently Used Specialists
We should proactively collect cell phone numbers or direct lines to the provider's assistant at every tertiary care center we refer to frequently. A personal number gets calls returned. A main line gets you to a queue.
  • When speaking to a specialist or their office, ask for the assistant's direct line or preferred contact method
  • Store these numbers in a dedicated section of the manual and in the EMR
  • Update the list whenever a contact changes — do not let it go stale
  • Share the list across all staff so anyone can reach the right person quickly
Always Confirm Referral Receipt
  • Do not assume the referral arrived because a fax was sent. Fax transmission confirmations do not mean the document was received, processed, or linked to the correct patient
  • For any referral where timing matters, call the specialist's office within 3–5 business days to confirm it is in their system
  • If the specialist does not have the referral, resend immediately and follow up again the next day
  • When a referral is missing, check whether other referrals sent around the same time were received — this can identify whether the problem is systematic (wrong fax number) or isolated
Real Case — Dr. Porter at UMG (Training Example)
Use this case to train staff on why this protocol exists.
What Happened
  • Patient was referred to Dr. Porter at UMG on 04/28/26
  • Appointment was scheduled for the fall — several months out, which was not appropriate given the clinical need
  • Provider called Dr. Porter directly on 05/15/26 to request an earlier appointment
  • Dr. Porter confirmed he did not have the referral on file for this patient
  • He did have referrals on file for patients Mongan and Watson — these need to be confirmed seen
Action Taken / Pending
  • Provider will call Dr. Porter to get the earlier appointment scheduled and resend the referral
  • Staff to confirm: Have patients Mongan and Watson been seen by Dr. Porter? Pull their charts and verify. If no specialist note is on file, call Dr. Porter's office.
Key lesson: A fax confirmation does not mean the referral arrived. For any patient where timing matters, follow up directly with the specialist's office to confirm receipt.

📄 Documentation & Scanning Standards

Chart categorization, correct Date of Service usage, neurological clearance language, physician name accuracy, and document labeling standards that apply to all staff.

Clearance LanguageChart IndexingDate of ServicePhysician NamesDocument Categories
⚖️
Neurological Clearance Language Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedStaff were repeatedly writing "patient is medically cleared" on forms and letters. This practice implies the neurology practice is clearing the patient for all aspects of surgery or procedures — including cardiac, pulmonary, anesthesia, and surgical risk — which is outside the scope of neurology.
❌Never write: "Patient is medically cleared" or any variation implying general medical clearance.
Correct Template Language — Always Use This Exact Wording
Standard Neurological Clearance Statement"From a neurological standpoint, there is no neurological contraindication to the planned procedure at this time."
When Risk Exists or More Information Is Needed"Neurological recommendations cannot be finalized until the requested records/details are reviewed."
Staff Workflow for All Clearance Forms
  1. Identify what procedure is being requested
  2. Confirm who is requesting the form (surgeon's office? anesthesia? PCP?)
  3. Confirm whether they are asking for general medical clearance or neurology-specific input
  4. If they want general medical clearance — redirect them to the appropriate provider (PCP, cardiology, etc.)
  5. If neurology input is appropriate — use the approved template language only
  6. Send to the provider for review and approval before faxing or releasing
  7. Save completed and signed form in chart under correct category
ℹ️Neurology can only comment on neurological contraindications. General medical clearance must come from PCP, cardiology, pulmonology, anesthesia, or the surgical team as appropriate.
🗂
Correct Chart Categorization & Document Labeling
▼
Problem IdentifiedDocuments were indexed under wrong chart tabs, new duplicate categories were being created, and inconsistent naming prevented clinical staff from finding documents quickly.
Approved Standard Categories — Use These Only
  • Consultations
  • Hospital Records
  • Labs
  • Imaging
  • Procedures
  • Administrative
  • Patient Education
Document Placement Guide
  • Hospital discharge summaries → Hospital Records
  • Emergency department notes → Hospital Records
  • Specialist consult notes → Consultations
  • Lab results → Labs
  • MRI / CT / X-ray reports → Imaging
  • EMG tables → Procedures
  • Patient handouts and summaries → Patient Education
  • Consent forms, insurance documents → Administrative
⚠️Do not create new categories. Do not create duplicate tabs. If you are unsure where a document belongs, ask before scanning.
📅
Correct Date of Service (DOS) Critical
▼
Problem IdentifiedStaff were using today's date (the scan date) instead of the original Date of Service when indexing documents. This caused records to appear out of chronological order, making it difficult to review patient history accurately.
🚫Always use the original Date of Service from the document — never the date you scanned it.
  • Look at the document itself for the date of the service, visit, or result
  • For lab results — use the date the lab was drawn or resulted
  • For imaging — use the date the study was performed
  • For consult notes — use the date of the consult visit
  • For hospital records — use the date of discharge or the date of the specific note
  • If the DOS is unclear, check with your supervisor before indexing
👨‍⚕️
Physician Name Accuracy
▼
Problem IdentifiedPhysician names were repeatedly misspelled in documentation, including on referral letters and forms sent externally. Misspelled names create professionalism issues and can cause confusion with document matching.
  • Verify physician names before entering them in documentation
  • Use the facility directory, NPI lookup, or web search to confirm spelling
  • Do not guess — confirm before submitting any external document
  • Example of correct spelling: Dr. Okoye
  • When in doubt, call the physician's office to verify name spelling and fax number
💰
Diagnosis Coding & Billing Accuracy Critical — Claim Denials
▼
This Is a Financial Issue, Not Just a Documentation IssueIncorrect or incomplete diagnosis coding directly causes claim denials. When a VMA fails to add a new diagnosis the provider established during an encounter, or uses ICD-10 codes copied from a PCP referral without adding new diagnoses, claims will be denied or underbilled. This has a direct impact on practice revenue. Careless documentation is not acceptable.
Rule: Every Diagnosis the Provider Makes Must Be Coded in That Encounter
🚫Do not simply copy the ICD-10 codes from a PCP referral note and use them as the encounter diagnoses. The provider may have established a new or different diagnosis during this visit. That new diagnosis must be added.
❌ What Happened — Real Example
  • Provider evaluated a patient and diagnosed femoral neuropathy
  • The VMA used only the ICD-10 codes that came in from the PCP's referral note
  • The new diagnosis of femoral neuropathy was never added to the encounter
  • The claim was submitted without the correct diagnosis — it was denied
✅ What Should Have Happened
  • VMA reads the provider's note and identifies all diagnoses documented
  • Any diagnosis new to this encounter is added to the problem list and encounter diagnoses
  • Femoral neuropathy → ICD-10: G57.20 (lesion of femoral nerve, unspecified lower limb) or laterality-specific code
  • If unsure of the correct code, ask the provider — do not skip it
EMG & EEG — Billable ICD-10 Codes Are Required
⚠️EMG and EEG procedures will be denied if the encounter does not contain a diagnosis code that is on the approved billable list for that procedure. If no such code is in the chart, the VMA must ask the provider before submitting.
EMG — Common Billable Diagnoses
  • G54.2 — Cervical root disorders
  • G54.4 — Lumbosacral root disorders
  • G56.0x — Carpal tunnel syndrome
  • G56.2x — Lesion of ulnar nerve
  • G57.0x — Sciatic nerve lesion
  • G57.20 — Lesion of femoral nerve
  • G60.0 — Hereditary motor and sensory neuropathy
  • G62.9 — Polyneuropathy, unspecified
  • M54.12 — Radiculopathy, cervical
  • M54.16 — Radiculopathy, lumbar
  • M54.17 — Radiculopathy, lumbosacral
EEG — Common Billable Diagnoses
  • G40.x — Epilepsy and recurrent seizures (various)
  • G40.009 — Localization-related epilepsy, unspecified
  • G40.309 — Generalized idiopathic epilepsy, unspecified
  • R56.9 — Unspecified convulsions
  • R55 — Syncope and collapse (for differential)
  • G93.40 — Encephalopathy, unspecified
  • R41.3 — Other amnesia (confusion/altered mental status workup)
  • F44.5 — Conversion disorder with seizures
⚠️This list is not exhaustive. A complete payer-approved list of billable ICD-10 codes for EMG and EEG is maintained in the chart. Consult it before submitting. When in doubt — ask the provider. Never submit without a confirmed billable diagnosis code.
Diagnosis Coding Checklist — Every Encounter
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Read the provider's full note — identify every diagnosis documented in this encounter
  • ✓
    Do not simply copy ICD-10 codes from the referring PCP's note — add any new diagnoses the provider established today
  • ✓
    If an EMG or EEG was ordered or performed, confirm a billable ICD-10 code is present in the chart
  • ✓
    If no billable code is present for a procedure, ask the provider before submitting — do not skip it
  • ✓
    Add new diagnoses to the encounter problem list with the correct ICD-10 code
  • ✓
    Verify laterality is captured where required (right vs. left — e.g., femoral neuropathy, carpal tunnel, radiculopathy)
  • ✓
    If unsure of any code, consult the billable code list or ask the provider — never guess
