⚠️
Operational Concerns — Records & Referral Tracking Quality Standard
▼
Issues IdentifiedMedical record requests are being documented but not consistently sent, tracked, or followed up. Outside records are frequently missing at follow-up visits. Ordered referrals are sometimes never received by the specialist, and the issue is only discovered during the patient's next visit. Critical workflows rely on memory rather than standardized tracking.
1 · Medical Records Follow-Up
  • Medical record requests must be sent, tracked, and followed up — documentation alone is not sufficient
  • Outside records are frequently missing at follow-up visits because there is no centralized tracking system
  • A dedicated Medical Records Tracking Sheet with assigned ownership and follow-up dates is required to ensure every request is completed
2 · Closing the Loop on Referrals & Orders
🚫An order is never complete simply because it was placed. Verify all four steps below.
#StepHow to Verify
1Receiving facility received the referral / orderCall or confirm fax receipt
2Patient has been scheduledConfirm appointment date with facility or patient
3Referral / study has been completedFollow up if appointment date has passed
4Results / consultation note received and uploaded into chartConfirm in EHR before closing the task
3 · Pre-Visit Chart Preparation
  • Follow-up charts must be reviewed before the patient arrives — not during the encounter
  • Identify and resolve missing consultation notes and outside records ahead of time
  • Providers should never have to pause an encounter to contact facilities for missing records — that preparation is staff's responsibility
4 · Accountability & Task Ownership
Every Pending Task Must Have:
  • A responsible staff member assigned
  • A tracking location (Google Sheet or tracking log)
  • A follow-up date
  • A documented completion date when done
Standardized Tracking Required For:
  • Medical record requests
  • Referrals (sent → scheduled → resulted → uploaded)
  • Prior authorizations
  • Any outstanding patient-related task requiring follow-up
5 · Why This Matters — Patient Care Impact
ℹ️Delays in obtaining outside records and referral results can delay diagnosis and treatment. A structured follow-up system improves continuity of care, reduces missed studies, and ensures providers have complete information at the time of the visit. Time lost during encounters to locate missing records directly reduces provider efficiency and patient throughput.
🎓
Referrals & Records Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Referrals & Records Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

🔬 Labs, Imaging & Interfaces

Quest interface verification, lab coding accuracy, MRI order tracking, abnormal lab reference tables, standard lab order templates for neurological work-ups, and verified contact directories for all regional lab and radiology facilities.

Quest InterfaceLab CodingAbnormal ValuesLP TemplateMyasthenia GravisConnective TissueLeqembiAlzheimer'sMS MonitoringDietary DeficiencyLab FacilitiesRadiology Facilities
📊All imaging orders and important ordered studies must be logged in the shared PA & Orders Tracking Google Sheet. Every MRI, CT, ultrasound, and specialty study ordered must have a row in the tracker with status updated as it progresses. See the Prior Authorization tab for the full tracking protocol, status categories, and color-coding guide.
🔌
Quest Interface Verification Important
▼
Problem IdentifiedLab orders were not transmitting through the Quest interface and results were not returning. Staff were not verifying transmission, so failures were only discovered later when results were needed for a visit.
Expectations
  • After placing every lab order, verify that it transmitted successfully through the interface
  • Check for transmission errors in the EMR before the patient leaves
  • If results are not returned by the expected date, actively follow up with Quest
  • Reconcile all missing results before the patient's next visit
  • Escalate interface failures immediately — do not wait until results are critically needed
Training Points
  • Interface failures are not rare — they must be checked every time an order is placed
  • If the interface has been unreliable, consider calling Quest to confirm receipt of each order
  • Document all interface failure incidents and escalate to the supervisor so IT can be notified
💳
Lab Coding Accuracy Important
▼
Problem IdentifiedIncorrect diagnosis codes were linked to lab orders, causing patient billing problems. Labs were billed without medical necessity documentation, leading to denials and patient responsibility balances that should never have been created.
🚫Incorrect diagnosis codes can create unexpected patient bills and insurance denials. Every lab order must be coded accurately and linked to the correct diagnosis.
  • Use the correct diagnosis code that reflects the clinical indication for the test
  • Verify medical necessity — the diagnosis must justify the lab being ordered
  • Do not use a generic or default diagnosis code without provider confirmation
  • If you are unsure which diagnosis to link, ask the provider before submitting the order
  • Review any lab denials with the billing team to identify patterns
🧲
MRI / Imaging Order Tracking — Full Workflow
▼
Problem IdentifiedMultiple imaging centers reported not receiving MRI orders. Patients arrived for MRI appointments and the imaging center had no order on file. Providers did not have results available at follow-up visits.
Track Every MRI Order Through All 4 Stages
  1. Order Sent — fax or portal transmission; save fax confirmation number in chart note
  2. Confirmed Received — call imaging center if no auto-confirmation within 24 hours; document the name of staff member who confirmed
  3. Appointment Scheduled — record date, time, and location of appointment in chart
  4. Results Returned — after appointment date, follow up to verify report was faxed; confirm report is in chart before next visit
⚠️If an MRI is ordered at a visit and results are not available at the next follow-up, the MA must identify the breakdown point and take corrective action before the provider sees the patient.

🩻 Imaging Management — Purpose & Standards

Ordering, tracking, follow-up, and reporting standards for all imaging studies.

Key Principle

An imaging order is not complete when it is entered — it is complete only when the study has been performed, the report has been reviewed by the provider, the patient has been appropriately informed, and any necessary follow-up actions have been taken.

✅
Imaging Key Responsibilities
▼
Ordering
  • Select the correct imaging study and body part — verify the order matches the provider's instruction before submitting
  • Use the appropriate, clinically justified ICD-10 diagnosis for every imaging order
  • Specify contrast protocol correctly per provider instructions (see policies below)
  • Verify the imaging facility participates with the patient's insurance before scheduling — do not assume
Tracking & Follow-Through
  • Track all imaging orders until the study is completed — not just until the order is sent
  • Obtain and upload imaging reports into the chart promptly when received
  • Notify the provider of abnormal or urgent findings the same day they are received
  • Follow up on all missing or overdue imaging studies and reports — do not wait for the next visit
  • For specialized imaging (CTA, 3D CT reconstruction, upright MRI): actively follow through until results are received; research alternative facilities if the study is not available locally
📌
Imaging Major Policies
▼
  • MS patients — MRI Brain contrast protocol: MRI Brain should generally be ordered with and without contrast to evaluate for active enhancement, unless the provider specifically orders otherwise. Do not default to non-contrast only for MS patients.
  • Contrast terminology: Use "with contrast," "without contrast," or "with and without contrast" precisely according to provider instructions. Incorrect contrast specification can invalidate the study and require a repeat scan.
  • MRI denied by insurance: Notify the provider immediately when an MRI or any imaging study is denied. The provider will determine whether to appeal, order an alternative study, or escalate urgently. Do not leave a denial sitting without provider awareness.
  • Insurance participation: Confirm the imaging facility is in-network for the patient's insurance before scheduling. An out-of-network imaging visit can create a large unexpected patient bill.
  • Delayed appointments: If an imaging appointment is delayed beyond what is clinically appropriate for the patient's condition, escalate to the provider — do not simply wait. The provider needs to know and may need to intervene.
  • Specialized imaging follow-through: CTA Head and Neck, 3D CT Reconstruction (Eagle Syndrome), upright MRI, and other non-routine studies require extra diligence. These are not always available at standard facilities. Research and identify alternative facilities when local options are unavailable, and track these studies until reports are received.
  • Tracking is mandatory: All imaging orders must be entered in the shared PA & Orders Tracking Google Sheet (see tracking section above) with status updated at each stage. See Prior Authorization tab for full tracking protocol.
🩻
Common Imaging Studies — Reference List
▼

These are the imaging studies most commonly ordered at Mir Neurology. Be familiar with each — including the correct body part, contrast protocol, and when each is typically indicated.

MRI Brain MRI Cervical Spine MRI Thoracic Spine MRI Lumbar Spine MRI with Contrast MRI with & without Contrast CT Head CTA Head & Neck MRA 3D CT Reconstruction (Eagle Syndrome) Upright MRI
⚠️Eagle Syndrome and upright MRI are specialized studies that may not be available at standard local facilities. When ordered, begin identifying appropriate facilities immediately and keep the provider informed of availability and timing.
🚫
Imaging — Common Errors to Avoid
▼
  • Ordering the wrong imaging study — wrong modality, wrong body part, or wrong laterality
  • Incorrect contrast protocol — ordering MRI without contrast when "with and without" was indicated (especially for MS patients)
  • Incorrect ICD-10 diagnosis — code doesn't match the clinical reason for the study; causes insurance denials
  • Non-participating facility — routing the patient to a facility not covered by their insurance
  • Failing to follow up on denied studies — denial received but provider not notified and no appeal initiated
  • Not confirming imaging was completed — order placed and assumed done without verification
  • Missing imaging report before follow-up visit — patient arrives for review and report is not yet in the chart
  • Failing to obtain outside imaging and discs — patient has relevant prior imaging at another facility that was never requested
✅
Daily Imaging Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Reviewed all outstanding imaging orders — confirmed none are overdue or unscheduled
  • ✓
    Verified appointments are scheduled and insurance participation confirmed for each facility
  • ✓
    Urgent imaging studies tracked — status confirmed and escalated if delayed
  • ✓
    Completed imaging reports obtained from facilities
  • ✓
    Reports uploaded into TalkEHR chart and referenced in OpenEvidence
  • ✓
    Provider notified of any abnormal or urgent imaging findings — same-day notification documented
  • ✓
    Tracking sheet updated with current imaging order statuses
  • ✓
    All imaging reports confirmed in chart before any scheduled follow-up appointments today
📊
Imaging Quality Indicators (KPIs)
▼
Imaging Order Accuracy
Correct study, correct body part, correct contrast protocol, correct ICD-10
Imaging Completion Rate
% of ordered studies confirmed completed within the expected timeframe
Imaging Turnaround Time
Average time from order to report received and uploaded in chart
MRI Denial Response Time
Time between denial received and provider notification — must be same day
Missing Report Rate
% of follow-up visits where the imaging report was not yet in the chart
Urgent Imaging Follow-Up Compliance
% of urgent imaging orders confirmed scheduled and tracked to completion
Documentation Accuracy
Completeness of imaging-related chart entries — order, facility, report, provider review, patient notification

📋 Laboratory Management — Purpose & Standards

Foundational responsibilities, policies, and common errors for all staff handling laboratory orders and results.

Purpose

Ensure all laboratory orders are accurate, completed on time, reviewed promptly, and followed through until the patient has been informed and appropriate action has been taken. Every laboratory order must be tracked from the time it is placed until the result has been reviewed, documented, communicated, and acted upon. No abnormal result should ever fall through the cracks.

✅
Key Responsibilities
▼
Ordering & Verification
  • Enter accurate laboratory orders with correct, clinically justified ICD-10 codes
  • Double-check all orders before submission — confirm the correct test, the correct patient, and the correct facility
  • Verify the laboratory participates with the patient's insurance before sending orders — do not assume all labs accept all plans
  • Order follow-up testing promptly when instructed by the provider
Monitoring & Follow-Through
  • Monitor all laboratory portals daily: Meritus, WVU, UPMC, LabCorp, and Quest
  • Track all outstanding laboratory orders — do not assume a sent order was received
  • Review and escalate abnormal and critical results immediately — same day, same visit if possible
  • Ensure all laboratory results are added to TalkEHR and referenced in OpenEvidence as applicable
  • Close the loop on every laboratory order — no order is "done" until the result is reviewed, documented, and communicated
Documentation & Communication
  • Document provider review of every result — note who reviewed it, when, and what was decided
  • Document patient notification — method, date, what was communicated
  • Communicate important results to other treating providers (e.g., PCP) when clinically appropriate
📌
Major Policies
▼
  • Patient safety standard: Missing an ordered test (e.g., Vitamin B12) is not a minor oversight — it is a patient safety issue. Treat every missing result accordingly.
  • Never wait until the patient's next appointment to address an abnormal or critical result. Abnormal results require same-day action: provider notification and, where appropriate, patient contact.
  • ICD-10 codes must be clinically appropriate. Never add a diagnosis to a lab order solely to obtain insurance coverage. The code must reflect the actual clinical indication.
  • Verify insurance participation before routing orders. Sending a patient to a non-participating laboratory creates an unexpected bill — confirm network status first.
  • Maintain a fail-safe for abnormal results. Use the tracking sheet and daily portal review as a backstop so no abnormal result goes unnoticed — even when staff transitions occur.
  • Cancellations and no-shows: When a patient cancels or does not show, review whether there are any outstanding laboratory results for that patient. Unresolved results must be discussed with the provider and addressed — do not allow them to remain pending indefinitely.
  • Cross-provider communication: When a laboratory result has meaningful implications for the patient's other treating providers (e.g., a newly discovered B12 deficiency reported to the neurologist but not the PCP), communicate it appropriately.
  • Daily review is mandatory. Review pending and missing results every day — not only when a patient is scheduled. Critical findings can arrive between visits.
🚫
Common Errors to Avoid
▼
  • Missing ordered tests — an order placed but never completed or never followed up
  • Incorrect ICD-10 codes — wrong diagnosis linked to lab order causes denials and unexpected patient bills
  • Sending patients to non-participating laboratories — always verify insurance network before routing
  • Failing to follow up on abnormal results — results returned but not escalated or acted upon
  • Failing to document provider discussion — result reviewed verbally but not charted; creates a liability gap
  • Missing repeat laboratory orders — provider instructs follow-up labs but they are never placed
  • Not adding results to the chart — result received outside the EMR but never scanned or entered
  • Not notifying the patient of significant abnormalities — patient has a right to know and to understand next steps

🔬 Abnormal Lab Results, Interventions & Order Templates

Reference tables for abnormal lab values and standard lab order templates for common neurological work-ups. Provider approval is required before initiating any intervention.

🔬
Abnormal Lab Results & Interventions Critical — Provider Authority Required
▼
Provider Maintains Final Authority. The interventions listed below are standard protocols used at Mir Neurology. MAs use this reference to prepare recommendations and notify the provider — they do not independently prescribe or initiate interventions without provider approval. Always route abnormal results to the provider first.
Nutritional & Supplement Lab Values
Lab / TestNormal RangeIntervention / PrescriptionClinical Notes
Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) 8–30 nmol/L (Plasma/Serum)
70–180 nmol/L (Whole Blood)
  • Below lower normal: Thiamine 100 mg once daily
  • URGENT Schedule EEG urgently
 Low thiamine can cause permanent brain damage and ultimately dementia. EEG required urgently when critically low.
Copper Level 70–140 mcg/dL
  • Below lower normal: Copper Gluconate 4 mg once daily
  • Near lower normal: Copper Gluconate 2 mg once daily
 Checked when patient has gait ataxia. High copper → low zinc → increased infection risk.
Zinc Level 60–130 mcg/dL
  • Below lower normal: Zinc Picolinate 60 mg once daily
  • Near lower normal: Zinc Picolinate 30 mg once daily
 Low zinc impairs immunity and increases infection risk.
Folate, Serum 2.5–20.0 ng/mL
  • Below lower normal: Folic Acid 2 mg once daily
  • Near lower normal: Folic Acid 1 mg once daily
 Can ultimately lead to anemia.
Vitamin B12 180–914 pg/mL
  • Below lower normal: Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg/mL injection — once weekly ×4 weeks, then once monthly ×6 weeks
    Syringe: 3 mL, 25 Gauge ×1"
  • 250–300 pg/mL: Cyanocobalamin 2500 mcg once daily (oral)
  • 300–400 pg/mL: Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg once daily (oral)
 Can lead to neuropathy and anemia.
Vitamin D Total (25OH) 25–80 ng/mL
  • Below lower normal: Vitamin D 50,000 units once weekly
  • 25–30: 5,000 units once daily
  • 30–33: 2,000 units once daily
  • 33–40: 1,000 units once daily
 Can cause low energy, paresthesia, and neuropathy.
Magnesium 1.7–2.2 mg/dL (Labcorp)
1.5–2.5 mg/dL (Quest)
  • Near or below lower normal: Magnesium Glycinate 400 mg once at bedtime
 Can cause insomnia, cramps, and polyneuropathy.
Critical Values — Immediate Escalation Required
The following abnormal values require immediate action. Notify the provider and/or the patient's PCP on the same day results are received.
Lab / TestNormal RangeAction Required
Potassium3.7–5.4 mmol/LALARMING If below normal: notify PCP immediately. Low potassium can be fatal.
Glucose70–99 mg/dLALARMING If below normal: notify PCP immediately.
Sodium136–147 mmol/LALARMING If below normal: can lead to hypotension. Immediately inform patient and call PCP.
Lithium Level~0.4–1.2 mEq/L
Toxicity ~1.2+		IMMEDIATE ESCALATION Level ≥1.2 — notify provider immediately. Lithium toxicity is a medical emergency.
Dilantin (Phenytoin)Therapeutic: 10–20 mcg/mL
Toxic: above 20		IMMEDIATE ESCALATION Level above 20 mcg/mL — notify provider immediately.
ESRAbove 20 = abnormalIMMEDIATE ESCALATION Elevated ESR — notify provider immediately for further work-up.
Hematology — Inform PCP if Abnormal
Lab / TestNormal RangeAction Required
Hemoglobin13.5–17.5 g/dL (M)
12–15 g/dL (F)
  • Near low normal: MONITOR Inform PCP
  • Below normal: CALL PCP May indicate anemia
Hematocrit41–50% (M)
36–44% (F)
  • Near low normal: MONITOR Inform PCP
  • Below normal: CALL PCP May indicate anemia
ANA & Rheumatoid FactorNegativeACTION REQUIRED If positive: refer to rheumatologist. May indicate connective tissue disorder.
🧠
Leqembi (Lecanemab) — Pre-Referral Requirements Required Before Referral to Infusion Center
▼
All of the following documentation must be obtained BEFORE referring any patient to Dr. Tang's office or any other infusion center for Leqembi therapy. Do not make the referral without confirming every item is complete.
Required Pre-Referral Documentation
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Beta Amyloid Scan or LP — confirmation of biomarkers (PET amyloid scan or cerebrospinal fluid analysis)
  • ✓
    MRI Brain — must be within the past 1 year
  • ✓
    Cognitive Impairment Assessment — any 2 of: AD8 Score, CDR Global Score, Mini-Cog Score, MMSE, MoCA, SLUMS Examination
  • ✓
    Functional Impairment Confirmation — any 1 of: FAQ Score, FAST Score, CDR-SB, ADL
  • ✓
    APOE-4 Test Result — genetic testing result on file
Referral cannot proceed without all 5 items confirmed. If any item is missing, coordinate with the provider to obtain it before submitting the referral.
💉
Lumbar Puncture — Multiple Sclerosis Template
▼
If ordering both MRI and LP: Advise the patient to have the MRI done first, then go for the LP. LP performed first can affect MRI findings.
ICD-10: G37.9 — Demyelinating disease of the central nervous system, unspecified
Patient Instructions
Patient must have blood drawn first before proceeding to Radiology for the lumbar puncture. CSF will then be collected and forwarded to Pathology for evaluation.
Orders to Radiology
  • Lumbar Puncture (XR/Fluoroscopy guided)
  • Instructions to Radiology: "Opening and closing pressures need to be checked in the lateral decubitus position. Send a portion of the specimen for further evaluation and freeze the remaining specimen."
  • CSF Laboratory Studies
Orders to Laboratory (Bloodwork)
  • PT/INR
  • CBC
  • Additional tests as clinically indicated
CSF Tests to Order
  • 82164 Angiotensin I–Converting Enzyme (ACE) — Blood/Serum
  • 84157 Protein, Total (CSF)
  • 88108 Cytopathology, concentration technique
  • 87070 Bacterial Culture, Aerobic
  • 86592 Syphilis Test, Non-treponemal Antibody, Qualitative — Blood
  • 86617 Antibody, Borrelia burgdorferi, Western Blot — Blood
  • 87205 Smear, Gram or Giemsa
  • 83873 Myelin Basic Protein, CSF
  • 82784 Immunoglobulins (IgA, IgD, IgG, IgM) — Blood
  • 83916 Oligoclonal Bands
  • 82945 Glucose, Body Fluid (CSF) — LOINC: 2342-4
  • 7558 IgG Synthesis Rate Index CSF (Quest) — LOINC: 14117-6 (other labs)
🧪
Myasthenia Gravis — Laboratory Order Template
▼
ICD-10: R53.1 — Weakness
Laboratory Orders
  • 34459 — Acetylcholine Receptor Blocking Antibody
  • 206 — Acetylcholine Receptor Binding Antibody
  • 26474 — Acetylcholine Receptor Modulating Antibody
  • 165600 — Myasthenia Gravis Profile (Labcorp — includes all Abs + MuSK Ab)
🧪
Connective Tissue Disease Work-Up Template
▼
ICDs: R53.1 Weakness | M62.838 Other muscle spasm | R20.2 Paresthesia of Skin
Laboratory Orders
  • 86200 — Cyclic Citrullinated Peptide (CCP) Antibody
  • 82552 — Creatine Kinase (CK/CPK), Isoenzymes
  • 86140 — C-Reactive Protein (CRP)
  • 86225 — dsDNA Antibody (Native or Double-Stranded)
  • 85651 — Erythrocyte Sedimentation Rate (ESR)
  • 86618 — Antibody, Borrelia burgdorferi
  • 86617 — Antibody, Borrelia burgdorferi, Western Blot
  • 86430 — Rheumatoid Factor
  • 86431 — Rheumatoid Factor, Quantitative
🧪
Hypercoagulable State Work-Up Template
▼
Laboratory Orders
  • 85307 — Activated Protein C Resistance Assay
  • 85306 — Protein S, Free
  • 85300 — Hypercoagulability Panel
  • 85610 — PT/INR, PTT
  • 85613 — Russell Viper Venom Time (Dilute)
  • 85730 — PTT
  • 86146 — Beta-2 Glycoprotein I Antibody (each)
  • 86147 — Cardiolipin Antibody (each Ig class)
  • 86148 — Anti-Phosphatidylserine Antibody
  • 85220 — Factor V
  • 706994 — Homocysteine
🧪
Dietary Deficiency Work-Up Template
▼
Primary Deficiency Labs (as clinically indicated)
  • Vitamin B12 — ICD: E53.9 Vitamin B Deficiency, Unspecified; R20.2 Paresthesia of Skin
  • Vitamin D — ICD: E55.9 Vitamin D Deficiency
  • Thiamine (B1) — ICD: R20.2 Paresthesia of Skin; E51.9 Thiamine Deficiency
  • Folic Acid — ICD: R20.2 Paresthesia of Skin
  • Magnesium — ICD: R20.2 Paresthesia of Skin
Additional Dietary Deficiencies
  • Zinc — ICD: E60 Dietary Zinc Deficiency
  • Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) — ICD: E53.0 Riboflavin Deficiency
  • Vitamin B6 — ICD: E53.1 Pyridoxine Deficiency
  • Copper — Order only in patients with ataxic gait; use ICD: R27.9 Unspecified lack of coordination
  • Tocopherol (Vitamin E) — ICD: E56.0 Vitamin E Deficiency
🧪
Insulin Resistance Work-Up Template
▼
Laboratory Orders
  • 82746 — Folate, Serum
  • 82607 — Vitamin B12
  • 83735 — Magnesium, Serum
  • 84425 — Vitamin B1 (Thiamine), Whole Blood (LC/MS/MS)
  • 82306 — Vitamin D, 25-OH, Total (IA method)
  • 83036 — Hemoglobin A1c — ICD: Z13.1 Encounter for screening diabetes mellitus
  • 83525 — Insulin (fasting) — ICD: Z13.1 Encounter for screening diabetes mellitus
🧪
Gluten Sensitivity / Celiac Disease Work-Up Template
▼
ICD-10: R19.7 — Diarrhea Unspecified
Laboratory Orders
  • 83516 — Tissue Transglutaminase Antibody (tTG), IgA & IgG
  • 86255 / 86256 — Endomysial Antibody (EMA) Screen, IgA with Reflex to Titer (86255 = screen; 86256 = titer if reflexed)
  • 82784 — Anti-IgA (Total Serum IgA)
🧠
Alzheimer's Disease — Biomarker Laboratory Panel
▼
Use diagnosis: MCI — Mild Cognitive Impairment. Contact Talha Bashir (talha@mirneurology.com) — he has a paper order template created for these tests.
Laboratory Orders (Quest / Labcorp / C₂N)
  • 484440 — Glial Fibrillary Acidic Protein (GFAP)
  • 140555 — Neurofilament Light Chain, Plasma
  • 483745 — Phosphorylated Tau 181 (pTau-181), Plasma — Elevated in early Alzheimer's; correlates with tau tangles and amyloid burden
  • 505809 — Lumipulse® pTau-217/Beta Amyloid 42 Ratio
  • 484390 — Phosphorylated Tau 217 (pTau-217), Plasma
  • 505725 — Beta Amyloid 42/40 Ratio, Plasma
Research & select the appropriate reference lab (Quest, Labcorp, C₂N) based on availability. Use the paper order template on file with Talha Bashir.

Baseline and routine monitoring lab orders for each MS DMT. Use CPT codes as listed when placing orders.

🔍 Common Tests, Daily Checklist & Quality Indicators

🧪
Common Lab Tests Requiring Special Attention
▼

The following tests are commonly ordered at Mir Neurology and require particular vigilance — they are frequently abnormal, frequently missed, and have direct implications for neurological diagnosis and treatment.

Vitamin B12 Vitamin B1 (Thiamine) Vitamin D Folate ESR CRP ANA TSH Hemoglobin A1c SPEP / Immunofixation Homocysteine Autoimmune Panels
ℹ️When any of these tests are ordered, confirm they were received, track the result, and escalate abnormal findings to the provider the same day. See the Abnormal Lab Results reference table above for specific normal ranges and intervention thresholds.
✅
Daily Laboratory Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 9
  • ✓
    Reviewed all pending laboratory results — confirmed none are missing or overdue
  • ✓
    Checked all laboratory portals: Meritus, WVU, UPMC, LabCorp, Quest
  • ✓
    Identified any abnormal or critical values — none left unreviewed
  • ✓
    Provider notified of all abnormal or critical results — same-day notification documented
  • ✓
    Provider recommendations documented in chart for each abnormal result
  • ✓
    Patient notified of significant abnormalities — date, method, and content documented
  • ✓
    Follow-up testing scheduled or ordered as instructed by provider
  • ✓
    PA/Orders tracking sheet updated with lab result statuses
  • ✓
    All confirmed results added to TalkEHR — no results sitting outside the chart
📊
Quality Indicators (KPIs)
▼

These are the performance indicators used to evaluate the quality and completeness of laboratory management. Every staff member handling labs should understand and work toward excellence in each of these areas.

Laboratory Order Accuracy
Correct test ordered, correct ICD-10, correct facility — every time
Abnormal Result Follow-Up Rate
% of abnormal results that received same-day provider notification and documented follow-through
Critical Result Response Time
Time between result receipt and provider notification for critical values — must be immediate
Outstanding Order Completion Rate
% of ordered labs confirmed received by the facility and resulted within expected timeframe
Documentation Completeness
Provider review, patient notification, and follow-up actions documented for every result
Patient Notification Compliance
% of significant abnormal results where patient was contacted and the contact was charted
Laboratory-Related Error Rate
Tracking of errors (wrong lab, missing result, unreported abnormal) to identify and address patterns

⚠ External Lab Results — Patient Notification Protocol

When patients have blood work done outside of Meritus or UPMC, results may not route directly to our EMR. This requires an active process to capture those results.

🧪
Blood Tests at External Facilities — Critical Workflow Required
▼
A patient's treatment was delayed by one month because blood work done at an external facility was never received by our office. The results were filed under the PCP's name at the external facility, and no one at our office knew the tests had been completed. This is a serious medicolegal risk — and it is preventable.
🚨Medicolegal Risk: If a patient has blood tests done at an outside facility and the results are not received and reviewed by the treating provider, we may be held responsible for missing a critical finding. This applies even when the order was sent to an external lab by the patient's choice or convenience.
Instructions for Patients Getting Tests at External Labs
  • When a patient is having blood work done at a facility other than Meritus or UPMC, instruct them to call our office as soon as the tests are completed
  • Explain that results from outside labs may not automatically route to our system
  • Ask the patient to confirm the name of the facility and the name under which the tests will be filed — results are sometimes filed under the PCP's name, not the ordering neurologist's
  • Document this instruction in the patient's chart
When a Patient Calls to Say Tests Are Done
  • The staff member taking the call must immediately notify the patient's assigned MA
  • Do not leave a message or create a task and move on — this is time-sensitive
  • The MA must then contact the external facility to obtain the results or fax authorization
  • The MA must confirm that the provider has reviewed and signed off on the results before the patient's next appointment
  • Document all steps: date patient called, MA notified (name), date results obtained, date reviewed by provider
⚠️Be vigilant about how results are filed at external facilities. Labs completed by a patient who also sees a PCP at the same facility may be filed under the PCP's name or the PCP's order. When requesting records or fax authorization, specify both the patient name and the date of service — do not assume results will be easy to locate.
⚠️
Operational Concerns — Labs, Imaging & Order Tracking Quality Standard
▼
Issues IdentifiedOrdered studies (such as PET scans) are sometimes never received by the performing facility, with the gap only discovered at the patient's follow-up visit. There is insufficient tracking of whether orders were successfully transmitted, scheduled, completed, and resulted. Follow-up charts are not consistently reviewed before appointments, leaving providers to discover missing results mid-encounter.
1 · Labs, Imaging & Referral Tracking
  • A centralized Google Sheet must track every lab order, imaging order, and referral until the loop is fully closed
  • Tracking begins the moment an order is placed — not when results arrive
  • It is not sufficient to verify that an order was entered in the EHR — you must confirm the receiving facility actually received it
2 · Closing the Loop — Four Required Steps
🚫An order is never complete simply because it was placed. Every lab and imaging order must pass all four steps before the task is closed.
#StepHow to Verify
1Receiving facility received the orderCall or confirm fax/interface receipt
2Patient has been scheduledConfirm appointment date with facility or patient
3Study has been completedFollow up if appointment date has passed with no results
4Results have been received and uploaded into the chartConfirm in EHR before the follow-up visit
3 · Pre-Visit Chart Preparation
  • Follow-up charts must be reviewed before the patient arrives — not during the encounter
  • Missing imaging results, laboratory results, and consultation notes should be identified and resolved before the appointment
  • If results are missing: contact the facility before the visit, not while the provider is in the room with the patient
4 · Accountability & Task Ownership
Every Pending Task Must Have:
  • A responsible staff member assigned
  • A tracking location (centralized Google Sheet)
  • A follow-up date
  • A documented completion date when done
Standardized Tracking Required For:
  • Lab orders (sent → completed → resulted → uploaded)
  • Imaging orders (sent → scheduled → completed → report received)
  • Referrals and prior authorizations
  • Any outstanding patient task requiring follow-up
5 · Workflow Standardization
⚠️Critical workflows currently rely on memory rather than standardized tracking. If it isn't in the tracking sheet, it doesn't exist. Every order placed must be logged immediately.
  • Lab and imaging tracking sheets must be maintained and reviewed daily
  • Staff must not rely on the EHR order status alone — interface failures can silently drop orders
  • Outstanding items approaching the follow-up visit date require immediate escalation if not resolved
6 · Why This Matters — Patient Care & Provider Impact
ℹ️Delays in receiving imaging and laboratory results can directly delay diagnosis and treatment. Time lost during encounters to locate missing studies reduces provider efficiency and patient throughput. A structured tracking system ensures providers have complete information at the time of every visit — and that no ordered study falls through the cracks.
🎓
Labs & Imaging Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Labs & Imaging Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

🔒 Operations, Security & Compliance

Prescription security, credential management, unsigned chart workflows, communication consolidation, accountability standards, and capacity/legal question protocols.

Credential SecurityPrescription SafetyTask OwnershipCommunication GroupsCapacity Evaluations
🔐
Prescription & Credential Security Critical
▼
🚫No prescription should ever be sent under a former employee's credentials. This is a serious legal and compliance violation.
Required Actions When a Staff Member Leaves
  • Disable all EMR access immediately on the last day of employment
  • Remove prescribing access from all systems
  • Remove all saved passwords and autofill credentials from clinic computers
  • Verify the departure is documented in your HR and compliance system
  • Confirm with IT that remote access has also been revoked
No Shared Credentials — Absolute Rules
  • Never share your password with any colleague for any reason
  • Never use another employee's login, even temporarily
  • Never leave your account logged in on a shared computer when you step away
  • Lock your screen whenever you leave a workstation
  • Report suspected credential misuse to your supervisor immediately
Why This Matters
  • Prescriptions sent under wrong credentials create DEA and licensing violations
  • Shared credentials mean actions cannot be accurately attributed — destroying the audit trail
  • HIPAA violations occur when unauthorized individuals access patient records using borrowed logins
✅
Accountability & Task Ownership
▼
Problem IdentifiedTasks were started but not followed through to completion. No one knew who owned specific tasks, and follow-up was inconsistent.
Every Important Task Must Have Three Things
  • An assigned owner — one named person responsible for completion
  • A follow-up process — scheduled check-in or tracking method
  • Completion verification — documented confirmation it was done
Areas Where Ownership Must Be Assigned
  • Referrals — each referral has a named owner
  • Lab follow-ups — tracked until results confirmed in chart
  • Imaging orders — tracked through all 4 stages
  • Prior authorizations — tracked until approved or denied
  • Outside records requests — tracked until received and scanned
  • Unsigned chart follow-up — tracked until signed and sent
📝
Unsigned Chart Workflow
▼
Problem IdentifiedSigned charts were remaining unsent for weeks, and unsigned charts were accumulating without any tracking system to identify what needed follow-up.
Suggested Tracking Solutions
  • Use a shared tracking spreadsheet (e.g., Google Sheets) with filters by status
  • Use checkbox tracking — each chart has a column for "Signed" and "Sent"
  • Set up automated reminders or notifications for charts unsigned past a deadline
  • Assign ownership — one staff member responsible for following up on unsigned charts daily
  • Review unsigned chart count at morning meetings
💬
Communication Group Consolidation
▼
Problem IdentifiedToo many overlapping communication groups were created across platforms, creating confusion about where to send messages and causing important messages to be missed.
  • Consolidate overlapping groups into clearly defined single-purpose groups
  • Assign a named manager for each active group
  • Remove members who do not need to be in a group
  • Define the purpose of each group so staff know where to post what
  • Archive or delete unused groups to reduce clutter
⚖️
Capacity & Legal Questions
▼
Problem IdentifiedFamilies were calling to ask general questions about "mental capacity" without specifying the context, and staff were routing these to providers without enough information to be useful.
Before Routing to Provider — Clarify the Specific Context
  • What type of capacity is being asked about?
  • Medical decision-making capacity
  • Financial decision-making capacity
  • Nursing home placement decisions
  • Legal matters (Power of Attorney, guardianship)
  • Informed consent for a specific procedure
Intake Question for Capacity Calls"Can you help me understand what specific decision or situation you're asking about? Capacity evaluations are specific to the type of decision being made, and the provider will need that context."
ℹ️Capacity is task-specific. A patient may have capacity to make some decisions but not others. The provider needs to know exactly what context they are evaluating before the appointment.

⭐ Quality Improvement & Training Culture

The philosophy of quality improvement, suggested audit topics, recurring training meetings, and how to build a culture of accountability without blame.

Quality AuditsTraining MeetingsSystem ImprovementNo Blame Culture
🎯
Quality Philosophy
▼
✅This process is not about blame. Every error documented in this manual exists to help us build a better system — not to punish individuals.
The Purpose of Quality Reviews Is To:
  • Improve systems and standardize workflows
  • Improve staff training and onboarding
  • Reduce repeated mistakes across the team
  • Identify process gaps before they harm patients
  • Improve patient care and patient experience
  • Build better teamwork and communication
  • Create accountability without creating fear
  • Strengthen the clinic's overall reliability
💡When a mistake happens, the first question should be: "What in our system allowed this to happen?" — not "who made this mistake?"
🔍
Suggested Regular Quality Audits
▼

These audits should be performed on a rotating basis. Each represents a category where recurring errors have been identified.

Chart Documentation
  • Missing consult notes in charts
  • Missing EMG tables
  • Incorrect document labels
  • Incorrect Date of Service
Clinical Workflow
  • Medication reconciliation errors
  • Missing or undocumented allergies
  • Missing patient handouts
  • Missing imaging results
Referrals & Follow-Up
  • Referral delays
  • Unsigned charts sitting unsent
  • Unclosed referral loops
  • Urgent referrals without escalation
📚
Suggested Recurring Training Meetings
▼

These topics should be covered in regular staff meetings on a rotating schedule. Each session should include real examples from recent clinic activity.

  • Medication Reconciliation Meeting — review recent errors and high-risk cases
  • EMG Documentation Workshop — verify current scanning and dictation accuracy
  • Referral Tracking Workshop — review open referrals and closure rates
  • Scanning & Indexing Workshop — audit random charts for document placement
  • Scheduling Rules Workshop — review procedure timing rules with schedulers
  • MVA Intake Training — practice data collection scenarios
  • Pre-Visit Preparation Training — timed chart review exercises
  • Communication Standards Workshop — role-play provider request scenarios
📊
Medication Refill & Denial Workflows
▼
Problem IdentifiedPatients reported refill requests going unresolved for extended periods. Denied medications were left with no follow-up, leaving patients without needed medications.
Refill Request Workflow
  • Address every refill request within 1 business day of receipt
  • If the refill requires provider review, route immediately — do not queue
  • If the refill is approved, confirm it was sent to the pharmacy
  • Escalate any request unresolved beyond 24 hours to the supervisor
  • Document all refill request communications with date and outcome
Denied Medication Workflow — All 5 Steps Required
  1. Review the denial — understand the reason (formulary, prior auth, quantity limit)
  2. Check for prior authorization — initiate PA immediately if applicable
  3. Contact provider — present denial and ask for alternative options
  4. Notify patient — communicate the denial and timeline for resolution
  5. Document all actions — every step must be recorded with date and outcome
🎓
Quality Improvement Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Quality Improvement Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

💉 Infusion Services

Standardized process checklists and team handoff workflows for all infusion therapies. Each section covers Provider, MA, Documentation, Prior Auth, Infusion Day, and Post-Infusion responsibilities. Verify payer-specific rules before every submission.

OcrevusLeqembiKisunlaIron InfusionPrior AuthorizationARIA MonitoringCMS Registry
📋
Master Team Handoff Workflow
▼

All infusion products follow the same team handoff sequence. Each role completes their checklist before routing to the next team. Verify payer-specific rules before every submission.

Master Handoff Flow
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Provider identifies candidate and signs order
  • ✓
    MA collects demographics, education, records, labs, medication/allergy list, and infusion location
  • ✓
    Documentation Team validates medical necessity, payer/CMS requirements, signatures, and packet completeness
  • ✓
    Prior Auth Team verifies benefits, submits PA, tracks approval/denial, and documents authorization
  • ✓
    Scheduling sends orders/approval to infusion center and schedules infusion/follow-ups
  • ✓
    Infusion Team completes infusion-day safety checks and documentation
  • ✓
    MA/provider team completes post-infusion monitoring, recalls, labs/MRI, and registry reporting
Product-Specific Quick Checkpoints
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Leqembi: amyloid confirmation, mild AD stage, baseline MRI, MRI before 3rd/5th/7th/14th infusions, ARIA/CMS registry checkpoint
  • ✓
    Kisunla: amyloid confirmation, mild AD stage, baseline MRI, MRI before 2nd/3rd/4th/7th infusions, ARIA/CMS registry checkpoint
  • ✓
    Ocrevus: MS subtype, MRI support, HBV screening, immunoglobulins/liver tests, premedications, 1-hour post-infusion monitoring
  • ✓
    Iron: iron deficiency labs, anemia/medical necessity, oral iron failure/intolerance/contraindication, product-specific PA rules, CMS NCD 110.10 for qualifying dialysis/EPO cases
Ownership Matrix
Provider
  • Eligibility and diagnosis support
  • Risk/benefit discussion
  • Signed order and follow-up plan
Medical Assistant
  • Patient education and demographics
  • Records and lab coordination
  • Communication and task routing
Documentation Team
  • Medical necessity review
  • Signatures and clinical packet
  • CMS/payer checkpoints and quality review
Prior Auth Team
  • Benefits verification
  • Authorization submission and tracking
  • Denial/appeal workflow
Scheduling / Infusion Center
  • Orders and appointment management
  • Medication/dose verification
  • Infusion safety, observation, and documentation
🏥

Ocrevus

Solu-Medrol (Methylprednisolone) — Acute MS Relapse Process Same-Day PA
▼
Urgent — Acute MS Relapse

Prior authorization must be obtained the same day the provider orders Solu-Medrol. Call the patient's insurance directly. This is billed through medical benefits — NOT pharmacy, because it is an infusion.

Step 1 — Call Insurance for Same-Day PA
📞Call the number on the back of the patient's insurance card (medical/provider line). Request a prior authorization for the exact phrasing below.
Exact Verbiage to Use

"I need a prior authorization for Solu-Medrol 1000 mg Injection, Solution Reconstituted, 1 (one) Solution daily, 3 Each, 3 days. IV QD for three days to be infused at an outpatient infusion center."

⚠️You may need to provide the infusion center's NPI and Tax ID#. Have those ready before calling (see Infusion Centers section above).
Step 2 — Fax to Infusion Center

Once authorization is obtained, fax the complete packet to the infusion center:

  • Printed prescription / infusion order
  • Patient demographics
  • Insurance information
  • Most recent office note
  • Authorization number
Step 3 — Call the Infusion Center

After faxing, call the infusion center to notify them of the urgent request. Confirm:

  • Will they call the patient to schedule for today or tomorrow?
  • Or should we send the patient directly?
ℹ️Document the authorization number, infusion center confirmation, and scheduling plan in the patient's chart.
Billing Codes — Solu-Medrol
J-Code
J2930
CPT Codes
96365
96366
ℹ️96365 = Initial infusion, up to 1 hour. 96366 = Each additional hour. Bill through medical benefits (outpatient infusion), not pharmacy benefit.
Solu-Medrol Same-Day PA Checklist
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Provider confirms acute MS relapse and orders Solu-Medrol 1000 mg IV QD × 3 days
  • ✓
    Called patient's insurance (medical benefits line) — stated exact medication, dose, route, frequency, and duration
  • ✓
    PA obtained — authorization number documented in chart
  • ✓
    Infusion center selected and NPI/Tax ID confirmed
  • ✓
    Faxed: prescription, demographics, insurance, last office note, and authorization number to infusion center
  • ✓
    Called infusion center to confirm receipt — clarified if they will contact patient or if patient should report directly
  • ✓
    Patient notified of plan (appointment or direct report) — documented in chart
💊
Iron Infusion — Process Checklist Iron Deficiency
▼
ℹ️Common products: Venofer (iron sucrose) · Injectafer (ferric carboxymaltose) · Feraheme (ferumoxytol) · Monoferric (ferric derisomaltose) · Ferrlecit (sodium ferric gluconate). CMS NCD 110.10 applies for qualifying hemodialysis/EPO patients.
🩺 Provider Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Document diagnosis: iron deficiency anemia, CKD-related iron deficiency, chronic blood loss, malabsorption, oral iron intolerance/failure, or other medical necessity
  • ✓
    Document symptoms and cause/source of deficiency when known
  • ✓
    Document oral iron trial, intolerance, contraindication, malabsorption, or need for rapid repletion
  • ✓
    Order CBC, ferritin, iron/TIBC, TSAT, CMP if needed, and pregnancy status if applicable
  • ✓
    Select iron product, dose, number of infusions, and follow-up lab plan
  • ✓
    Sign infusion order
👩‍⚕️ Medical Assistant Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Verify demographics, insurance, secondary insurance, phone number, pharmacy, and infusion location
  • ✓
    Collect office note, CBC, hemoglobin/hematocrit, ferritin, iron, TIBC, TSAT, CMP if ordered, pregnancy status if applicable, CKD/dialysis/nephrology records if relevant
  • ✓
    Collect oral iron failure/intolerance/contraindication documentation
  • ✓
    Update medication list and allergies
  • ✓
    Educate patient on infusion schedule, side effects, allergic reaction symptoms, and emergency instructions
  • ✓
    Route complete packet to Documentation Team
📋 Documentation Team Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Confirm diagnosis supports IV iron
  • ✓
    Confirm labs support iron deficiency/anemia and are recent enough for payer
  • ✓
    Confirm oral iron failure/intolerance/contraindication or reason oral iron is not appropriate
  • ✓
    Confirm dose, product, frequency, total number of infusions, signed order, and follow-up plan
  • ✓
    CMS checkpoint: for chronic hemodialysis patients receiving EPO/ESA, confirm NCD 110.10 criteria when using iron sucrose or sodium ferric gluconate
  • ✓
    Do not change diagnosis codes; suggest corrections only — prepare packet and send to Prior Auth Team
📑 Prior Authorization Team Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Verify medical vs pharmacy benefit, buy-and-bill, specialty pharmacy, site-of-care restriction, preferred product, step therapy, PA or predetermination need
  • ✓
    Submit demographics, insurance cards, signed order, office note, CBC, ferritin, iron/TIBC, TSAT, oral iron documentation, CKD/dialysis records if applicable, and payer form
  • ✓
    Confirm J-code, dose/units, number of visits, and requested site of service
  • ✓
    Track submitted/pending/additional info/peer-to-peer/approved/denied/appeal
  • ✓
    Upload approval with authorization number, effective dates, approved drug/dose/units/visits/location
💉 Infusion Day Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Verify identity, consent if required, allergies, vitals, current weight if dose is weight-based, and active illness status
  • ✓
    Verify medication, dose, route, lot, and order
  • ✓
    Monitor for hypersensitivity reaction and hypotension
  • ✓
    Complete required observation period if applicable
  • ✓
    Document infusion note and schedule next infusion if series
📞 Post-Infusion Follow-Up
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Screen for delayed reaction and side effects
  • ✓
    Notify provider of reaction or intolerance
  • ✓
    Schedule remaining infusions
  • ✓
    Order repeat CBC, ferritin, iron/TIBC, and TSAT per provider plan
  • ✓
    Document treatment response and need for additional infusions
🚫 Denial Workflow
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Review denial reason
  • ✓
    Add missing labs or oral iron documentation
  • ✓
    Review preferred product substitution with provider
  • ✓
    Prepare appeal/medical necessity letter
  • ✓
    Schedule peer-to-peer if needed
  • ✓
    Document outcome
🔗Reference: CMS IV Iron NCD 110.10
💊
Ocrevus (Ocrelizumab) — Process Checklist Multiple Sclerosis
▼
ℹ️Indication: RMS, active SPMS, or PPMS. Initial dosing: 300 mg IV, then 300 mg IV two weeks later. Maintenance: 600 mg IV every 6 months. Premedications required before each infusion. Monitor during infusion and at least 1 hour post-completion.
⚙️ Medication-Specific Requirements
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Indication documented: RMS, active SPMS, or PPMS
  • ✓
    Baseline MRI brain/spine reports supporting diagnosis uploaded
  • ✓
    HBV screening before first dose: HBsAg and anti-HBc confirmed; active HBV is a contraindication
  • ✓
    Varicella Zoster Virus (VZV) testing before first dose: confirm VZV IgG antibody status; vaccinate seronegative patients with varicella vaccine at least 6 weeks before starting Ocrevus; consider Shingrix (RZV) for eligible patients per provider discretion
  • ✓
    Quantitative immunoglobulins and liver tests obtained before initiation; CBC/CMP per practice/payer protocol
  • ✓
    Premedicate before each infusion: methylprednisolone or equivalent corticosteroid + antihistamine; acetaminophen optional per protocol
  • ✓
    Initial dosing confirmed: 300 mg IV then 300 mg IV two weeks later; maintenance 600 mg IV every 6 months
  • ✓
    Plan to monitor during infusion and at least 1 hour after completion
🩺 Provider Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Confirm MS subtype and medical necessity
  • ✓
    Document McDonald criteria/supporting MRI findings
  • ✓
    Document prior DMT history or reason for first-line therapy
  • ✓
    Discuss risks, infections, vaccines, malignancy warning, pregnancy considerations, and infusion reactions
  • ✓
    Sign infusion order and follow-up plan
👩‍⚕️ Medical Assistant Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Verify demographics, insurance, pharmacy, contact number, and infusion location
  • ✓
    Collect office note, MRI reports, lab results, prior DMT history, medication list, allergies, and signed order
  • ✓
    Confirm HBV labs are ordered/resulted before first infusion
  • ✓
    Screen for active infection or recent hospitalization before scheduling
  • ✓
    Send patient education from assigned MA email
  • ✓
    Route complete packet to Documentation Team
📋 Documentation Team Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Confirm diagnosis and MS subtype are documented
  • ✓
    Confirm MRI evidence and prior DMT history are included
  • ✓
    Confirm HBV results, immunoglobulins/liver tests, CBC/CMP as required are present
  • ✓
    Confirm signed note/order and no missing signatures
  • ✓
    Do not change diagnosis codes; suggest corrections only — prepare packet and send to Prior Auth Team
📑 Prior Authorization Team Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Verify active benefits, medical vs pharmacy benefit, site-of-care, buy-and-bill, specialty pharmacy, step therapy, and payer-specific forms
  • ✓
    Submit demographics, insurance cards, note, MRI reports, labs including HBV, prior DMT history, and signed order
  • ✓
    Request loading and maintenance dose units/visits correctly
  • ✓
    Track approval/denial/peer-to-peer/appeal
  • ✓
    Document authorization number, effective dates, approved units, and approved site
💉 Infusion Day Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Verify patient identity, order, dose, and authorization
  • ✓
    Confirm no active infection and no new contraindication
  • ✓
    Review allergies, vitals, pregnancy status if applicable, and recent vaccines if relevant
  • ✓
    Administer premedications
  • ✓
    Monitor during infusion and for at least 1 hour afterward
  • ✓
    Document reaction, vitals, lot, dose, and next infusion due date
📞 Post-Infusion Follow-Up
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Assess delayed reactions and infections
  • ✓
    Notify provider of reaction or infection concerns
  • ✓
    Schedule second loading dose or 6-month maintenance recall
  • ✓
    Order periodic labs as directed
🔗Reference: Ocrevus FDA Label (2025)
💊
Leqembi (Lecanemab) — Process Checklist Alzheimer's Disease
▼
ℹ️Indication: MCI due to Alzheimer disease or mild Alzheimer dementia with confirmed amyloid pathology. Dosing: 10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks. MRI monitoring required before the 3rd, 5th, 7th, and 14th infusions. CMS registry may be required for Medicare patients.
⚙️ Medication-Specific Requirements
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Dosing schedule documented: 10 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks per prescribing information
  • ✓
    Recent baseline brain MRI obtained prior to initiation
  • ✓
    MRI monitoring schedule set: before the 3rd, 5th, 7th, and 14th infusions; additional MRI for ARIA symptoms
  • ✓
    Amyloid pathology confirmed before treatment
  • ✓
    ARIA counseling documented
  • ✓
    CMS registry/data collection checkpoint completed for Medicare when required
🩺 Provider Checklist
Progress:
0 / 10
  • ✓
    Confirm diagnosis: MCI due to Alzheimer disease or mild Alzheimer dementia
  • ✓
    Confirm amyloid pathology by amyloid PET or CSF biomarkers and document result
  • ✓
    Complete and document cognitive assessment (MoCA, MMSE, SLUMS, or equivalent)
  • ✓
    Document functional assessment / disease stage and medical necessity
  • ✓
    Review recent baseline brain MRI before starting therapy
  • ✓
    Document ARIA risk counseling, infusion risk counseling, and emergency symptoms
  • ✓
    Document APOE e4 testing discussion/counseling when applicable
  • ✓
    Review anticoagulants, thrombolytics, antiplatelets, bleeding history, and MRI exclusion findings
  • ✓
    Complete consent and signed infusion order
  • ✓
    Assign registry/reporting responsibility when Medicare/CMS registry is applicable
👩‍⚕️ Medical Assistant Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Verify demographics, phone number, insurance, secondary insurance, emergency contact, and preferred infusion location
  • ✓
    Update medication list, allergies, anticoagulants/antiplatelets, and pharmacy
  • ✓
    Collect signed office note, cognitive test, amyloid PET or CSF result, baseline MRI report, consent, and infusion order
  • ✓
    Send patient education from assigned MA email and route patient replies to assigned MA/provider
  • ✓
    Create MRI reminder tasks based on product-specific schedule (before 3rd/5th/7th/14th infusions)
  • ✓
    Route complete packet to Documentation Team
📋 Documentation Team Checklist
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Confirm diagnosis and disease stage support indication
  • ✓
    Confirm amyloid confirmation is present and uploaded
  • ✓
    Confirm cognitive testing and functional decline/stage are documented
  • ✓
    Confirm baseline MRI is present and exclusionary findings are addressed by provider
  • ✓
    Confirm signed note, signed order, consent, medication/allergy list, demographics, and insurance cards are included
  • ✓
    CMS checkpoint: confirm Medicare coverage status, qualifying registry/data collection requirement, registry ID or enrollment plan, and responsible staff member
  • ✓
    Do not change diagnosis codes; suggest corrections to provider if documentation/code mismatch found — then send complete packet to Prior Auth Team
📑 Prior Authorization Team Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Verify active coverage, Medicare/Medicare Advantage status, medical vs pharmacy benefit, buy-and-bill rules, specialty pharmacy, site-of-care restrictions, and payer forms
  • ✓
    Submit note, cognitive testing, amyloid confirmation, baseline MRI, consent/order, demographics, insurance cards, and registry documentation if applicable
  • ✓
    Track pending status, additional information requests, peer-to-peer, approval, denial, and appeal
  • ✓
    Document authorization number, effective dates, approved drug, approved dose/units, approved location, and approved visits
  • ✓
    Upload approval/denial and notify scheduling/documentation team
💉 Infusion Day Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Confirm authorization or no-PA-required note is on file
  • ✓
    Confirm no new neurologic symptoms before infusion; route symptoms urgently to provider
  • ✓
    Check vitals, allergies, medication changes, and active illness
  • ✓
    Administer infusion per order and monitor for infusion reaction
  • ✓
    Document infusion note and observation
  • ✓
    Schedule next infusion and product-specific MRI/follow-up
📞 Post-Infusion Follow-Up
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Call patient after infusion when required by practice protocol
  • ✓
    Screen for ARIA symptoms: headache, confusion, dizziness, vision change, gait change, focal neurologic deficit, seizure, or acute mental status change
  • ✓
    Escalate symptoms to provider immediately and arrange MRI/ER instructions as directed
  • ✓
    Confirm next infusion and MRI reminders are active
  • ✓
    Maintain registry/outcomes reporting when required
🚫 Denial / Missing-Information Workflow
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Identify denial or missing-info reason
  • ✓
    Request missing amyloid, MRI, cognitive testing, consent, or registry documentation
  • ✓
    Prepare medical necessity/appeal packet
  • ✓
    Schedule peer-to-peer if requested
  • ✓
    Document outcome and update team
🔗References: Leqembi FDA Label (2025) · CMS Anti-Amyloid mAbs CED/Registry
💊
Kisunla (Donanemab) — Process Checklist Alzheimer's Disease
▼
ℹ️Indication: MCI due to Alzheimer disease or mild Alzheimer dementia with confirmed amyloid pathology. Dosing: 700 mg infusions 1–2, 1050 mg infusion 3, then 1400 mg every 4 weeks. MRI monitoring required before the 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and 7th infusions. CMS registry may be required for Medicare patients.
⚙️ Medication-Specific Requirements
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Dosing schedule documented: 700 mg infusion 1, 700 mg infusion 2, 1050 mg infusion 3, then 1400 mg every 4 weeks unless stopped per amyloid reduction protocol/provider order
  • ✓
    Recent baseline brain MRI obtained prior to initiation

    MRI monitoring schedule set: before the 2nd, 3rd, 4th, and 7th infusions; additional MRI for ARIA symptoms

    Amyloid pathology confirmed before treatment

    ARIA counseling documented

    CMS registry/data collection checkpoint completed for Medicare when required

    Confirm baseline MRI is present and exclusionary findings are addressed by provider
  • ✓
    Confirm signed note, signed order, consent, medication/allergy list, demographics, and insurance cards are included
  • ✓
    CMS checkpoint: confirm Medicare coverage status, qualifying registry/data collection requirement, registry ID or enrollment plan, and responsible staff member
  • ✓
    Do not change diagnosis codes; suggest corrections to provider if documentation/code mismatch found — then send complete packet to Prior Auth Team
📑 Prior Authorization Team Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Verify active coverage, Medicare/Medicare Advantage status, medical vs pharmacy benefit, buy-and-bill rules, specialty pharmacy, site-of-care restrictions, and payer forms
  • ✓
    Submit note, cognitive testing, amyloid confirmation, baseline MRI, consent/order, demographics, insurance cards, and registry documentation if applicable
  • ✓
    Track pending status, additional information requests, peer-to-peer, approval, denial, and appeal
  • ✓
    Document authorization number, effective dates, approved drug, approved dose/units, approved location, and approved visits
  • ✓
    Upload approval/denial and notify scheduling/documentation team
💉 Infusion Day Checklist
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Confirm authorization or no-PA-required note is on file
  • ✓
    Confirm no new neurologic symptoms before infusion; route symptoms urgently to provider
  • ✓
    Check vitals, allergies, medication changes, and active illness
  • ✓
    Administer infusion per order and monitor for infusion reaction
  • ✓
    Document infusion note and observation
  • ✓
    Schedule next infusion and product-specific MRI/follow-up
📞 Post-Infusion Follow-Up
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Call patient after infusion when required by practice protocol
  • ✓
    Screen for ARIA symptoms: headache, confusion, dizziness, vision change, gait change, focal neurologic deficit, seizure, or acute mental status change
  • ✓
    Escalate symptoms to provider immediately and arrange MRI/ER instructions as directed
  • ✓
    Confirm next infusion and MRI reminders are active
  • ✓
    Maintain registry/outcomes reporting when required
🚫 Denial / Missing-Information Workflow
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Identify denial or missing-info reason
  • ✓
    Request missing amyloid, MRI, cognitive testing, consent, or registry documentation
  • ✓
    Prepare medical necessity/appeal packet
  • ✓
    Schedule peer-to-peer if requested
  • ✓
    Document outcome and update team
🔗References: Kisunla FDA Label · CMS Anti-Amyloid mAbs CED/Registry

🩼 Medical Supplies & Durable Medical Equipment (DME)

Ordering process, supplier contacts, and product directories for medical supplies and DME. Covers the Cumberland (Pharmacare) and Hagerstown (Equipped for Life) offices, plus special ordering for the U-Step Walker.

PharmacareEquipped for LifeU-Step WalkerPrior AuthorizationeRxCumberlandHagerstown
📋
General Ordering Process
▼

Standard workflow for ordering medical supplies and DME through Pharmacare Bedford Road (Cumberland office).

Step 1 — Create the Prescription
  1. Enter the prescription electronically (eRx)
  2. If the item is not in the medication list, use the Custom Prescription option — for medical supplies and equipment only
  3. Select Pharmacare Bedford Road as the pharmacy
  4. Enter all necessary instructions in the Sig (Directions) field
  5. Send the prescription electronically to the pharmacy
Step 2 — Pharmacy Processing
  • Pharmacare forwards the order to their Home Medical Supplies Department
  • Their team reviews the order and determines insurance requirements
  • Pharmacare is responsible for obtaining prior authorization when applicable
  • They will request supporting documentation from our office as needed
Paper Prescriptions
📄A paper prescription may also be prepared for the patient when Pharmacare cannot obtain authorization, or when the patient chooses another supplier, specialty pharmacy, or manufacturer.
Office Responsibilities
  • Ensure provider notes are completed and signed promptly
  • When requested, fax: Prescription · Insurance information · Most recent office visit note · Any additional supporting documentation
  • Send complete documentation together to prevent repeat requests and delays
Prior Authorization — Important
ℹ️Most medical supply companies and DME suppliers have their own authorization departments. They will verify insurance benefits, obtain prior authorization, coordinate delivery, and contact our office only if additional documentation is required. Our office generally does not initiate DME prior authorizations.
Quick Reference — Send Orders To
Pharmacare Bedford Road
Always include: ✓ Prescription   ✓ Insurance Information   ✓ Most Recent Office Visit Note   ✓ Any Supporting Documentation

Remember: The supplier—not our office—typically obtains prior authorization for medical equipment and supplies.
🏪

Equipped for Life — Hagerstown Referral Process
▼
ℹ️In Hagerstown, we utilize Equipped for Life for medical supplies and DME. Because it is a medical supply store rather than a pharmacy, orders go through the referral process — not eRx.
Ordering Options
Option 1 — Referral Order (Preferred)
  1. Create a referral to Equipped for Life
  2. The facility is already entered in our referral/address book — select it directly
  3. Include the requested medical equipment or supplies in the referral documentation
Option 2 — Direct Fax
  • Fax orders directly to Equipped for Life with required supporting documentation
Paper Prescription
  • Patient presents prescription directly to Equipped for Life
  • Patient may utilize another vendor or contact manufacturer directly
Documentation Requirements
  • Prescription / order
  • Insurance information
  • Most recent office visit note
  • Any supporting clinical documentation
Prior Authorization
ℹ️The authorization process is generally handled by Equipped for Life. They may request additional documentation from our office to obtain authorization from the patient's insurance company.
Resource Guide
Equipped for Life Medical Supply Resource Guide
https://www.meritushealth.com/files/EFL-Medical-Supply-Resource-Guide-v2-3.pdf
Refer to this guide for additional information on available equipment, services, and ordering guidance.
🚶
U-Step Walker — Special Ordering Process Patient-Initiated
▼
⚠️The U-Step Walker is ordered differently from most DME. It requires direct patient involvement — the patient contacts the vendor and coordinates delivery themselves.
Office Responsibilities
Step 1 — Paper Prescription
  • Complete a paper prescription for the U-Step Walker
  • Ensure the prescription is signed by the provider
  • Provide the original prescription directly to the patient
Step 2 — Documentation
  • Ensure the provider's office note is completed and signed promptly
  • Documentation should clearly support the medical necessity for the walker
Step 3 — Provide Vendor Information to Patient
  • 🌐 Website: ustep.com/product/standard-model
  • 📞 800-558-7837
  • 📞 847-676-1275
Patient Responsibilities
  • Contact U-Step directly using the provided phone number or website
  • Provide insurance information to the company
  • Complete any paperwork requested by the vendor
  • Coordinate delivery of the walker
Prior Authorization — U-Step Handles
  • U-Step company verifies insurance benefits
  • U-Step obtains prior authorization when required
  • U-Step coordinates shipment and delivery
  • Our office does not initiate the authorization process
  • If U-Step requests records, provide office notes and supporting documentation promptly
Quick Reference Checklist
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Provide signed paper prescription to patient
  • ✓
    Ensure provider notes are completed and signed
  • ✓
    Give patient the U-Step website: ustep.com/product/standard-model
  • ✓
    Give patient the U-Step phone numbers: 800-558-7837 / 847-676-1275
  • ✓
    Patient contacts U-Step directly
  • ✓
    U-Step verifies benefits and obtains authorization
  • ✓
    Provide supporting documentation if U-Step requests records

🔐 HIPAA & Privacy Training — All Staff

Required for every employee at Mir Neurology regardless of role. HIPAA violations carry federal penalties and can result in termination. This training covers the Privacy Rule, Security Rule, Breach Notification Rule, day-to-day compliance, and what to do when something goes wrong.

📘
What Is HIPAA & Why It Matters
▼
The Three HIPAA Rules
  • Privacy Rule — governs who can see, use, and disclose Protected Health Information (PHI). Sets patient rights.
  • Security Rule — governs how electronic PHI (ePHI) must be protected: access controls, encryption, audit logs, workstation security.
  • Breach Notification Rule — requires covered entities to notify patients, HHS, and sometimes media when unsecured PHI is improperly disclosed.
Who Is Covered
  • Covered Entities: healthcare providers (us), health plans, clearinghouses
  • Business Associates: vendors, IT, billing companies, transcription services — all must sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA)
  • You: every staff member who touches patient data in any form — paper, electronic, verbal — is bound by HIPAA
⚖️HIPAA is federal law. Violations are investigated by the HHS Office for Civil Rights (OCR). Penalties range from $100 to $1.9 million per violation category per year. Criminal violations can result in prison time. Ignorance of the law is not a defense.
🏷️
Protected Health Information (PHI) — What It Is
▼

PHI is any information that can identify a patient AND relates to their health, care, or payment for care. It includes 18 specific identifiers:

Identity Identifiers
  • Name
  • Address (including street, city, county, zip)
  • Dates (birth, admission, discharge, death)
  • Phone numbers
  • Fax numbers
  • Email addresses
Number Identifiers
  • Social Security number
  • Medical record number
  • Health plan beneficiary number
  • Account numbers
  • Certificate/license numbers
  • VINs / license plate numbers
Other Identifiers
  • Device identifiers and serial numbers
  • Web URLs / IP addresses
  • Biometric identifiers (fingerprints, voice)
  • Full-face photographs
  • Any other unique identifying number or code
⚠️ePHI is PHI stored or transmitted electronically — in the EHR, email, texts, fax servers, cloud storage, USB drives, laptops. The Security Rule applies specifically to ePHI.
✅
Permitted Uses & Disclosures — When You Can Share PHI
▼
✅ Permitted WITHOUT Patient Authorization
  • Treatment: sharing with other providers directly involved in the patient's care (ordering tests, referrals, consultations)
  • Payment: billing, prior authorizations, claims submissions
  • Operations: quality review, audits, staff training with de-identified cases
  • Required by law: subpoenas, court orders, mandatory reporting (abuse, public health)
  • Patient present and agrees verbally: discussing with a family member the patient has authorized
🚫 Requires Written Patient Authorization
  • Releasing records to attorneys, employers, life insurance companies
  • Marketing purposes
  • Sale of PHI
  • Releasing records to family members WITHOUT the patient's consent
  • Psychotherapy notes (additional special protections)
  • Sending records to anyone not directly involved in treatment or payment
Minimum Necessary Standard

Even when disclosure is permitted, you must share only the minimum necessary information to accomplish the purpose. Do not send an entire chart when only a specific note or lab result is needed. This applies to internal use as well — access only the records you need for your work.

🚫Looking up a record out of personal curiosity — even for a family member or yourself — is a HIPAA violation and a terminable offense.
👤
Patient Rights Under HIPAA
▼
Rights Patients Have
  • Right to access: patients can request copies of their medical records; we must respond within 30 days
  • Right to amend: patients can request corrections to their records
  • Right to an accounting of disclosures: patients can ask who we shared their PHI with
  • Right to restrict: patients can request we limit how we use/disclose their PHI (we may or may not be required to agree)
  • Right to confidential communications: patients can ask to be contacted only by certain methods or locations
How We Handle Requests
  • All record requests must go through the designated process — do not hand records directly to patients without following the release of information workflow
  • Verify patient identity before releasing any records (government-issued photo ID or verbal verification with DOB + address)
  • Log all disclosures as required
  • Never deny a request without consulting the Privacy Officer or supervisor
  • Direct patient complaints about privacy to the Privacy Officer — do not handle them informally
🏥
Day-to-Day HIPAA — Common Scenarios by Role
▼
Front Desk & Schedulers
❌ What NOT to Do
  • Do not call out a patient's name loudly in the waiting room beyond what is necessary for check-in
  • Do not discuss a patient's appointment reason, diagnosis, or medications where other patients can hear
  • Do not leave the patient intake screen visible to other patients approaching the desk
  • Do not confirm a patient's appointment or any health information to a caller without verifying their identity first
  • Do not leave sign-in sheets visible with previous patients' names and appointment times
✅ What to Do Instead
  • Use a privacy screen on patient-facing monitors
  • Step away from the desk or lower your voice when discussing clinical topics
  • Use non-descriptive appointment types on public-facing displays ("appointment" not "seizure follow-up")
  • Verify caller identity (full name + DOB) before confirming any appointment details
  • Use a cover sheet or rolling sign-in strip — never a full list
Medical Assistants & VMAs
❌ What NOT to Do
  • Do not leave a patient chart open on the screen when you leave the workstation
  • Do not discuss a patient's condition in the hallway, break room, or in front of other patients
  • Do not text PHI (name + diagnosis, DOB + labs, etc.) to anyone using a personal cell phone
  • Do not access a patient record you are not actively working on
  • Do not email PHI from a personal email account
✅ What to Do Instead
  • Lock your screen whenever you step away (Windows: Win+L; Mac: Ctrl+Cmd+Q)
  • Use the EHR's secure messaging for clinical communications
  • If you need to discuss a patient, do it in a private area out of earshot
  • Access only the records relevant to your current task
  • Report any accidental access to a wrong record to your supervisor immediately
Faxing & Sending Records
❌ What NOT to Do
  • Do not fax without a HIPAA-compliant cover sheet
  • Do not fax to an unverified number — confirm the receiving fax number before sending every time
  • Do not leave received faxes sitting in the machine accessible to anyone passing by
  • Do not send more records than requested — minimum necessary applies
✅ What to Do Instead
  • Always use the HIPAA cover sheet with the confidentiality notice
  • Verify fax numbers against the facility directory or NPI lookup — never from memory
  • Retrieve faxes from the machine promptly; check the received queue regularly
  • If a fax is sent to the wrong number, report it immediately — this is a potential breach
Phone Calls
📞Never confirm a patient's appointment, diagnosis, medications, or any health information to someone who calls in without first verifying the caller's identity. Ask for the patient's full name and date of birth at minimum. If someone claims to be the patient's family member, confirm the patient previously authorized that person — check the authorization on file.
Leaving Voicemails
  • Leave only the minimum: your name, practice name, callback number, and a general message ("please call us back regarding your appointment")
  • Do not leave diagnosis names, medication names, or appointment reasons on a voicemail unless the patient has given specific permission
  • If uncertain, keep the message neutral
Talking with Family
  • Patients may bring family members — does not automatically mean the family member has authorization to receive PHI
  • If the patient is present and verbally agrees to include the family member, this is generally permitted
  • If the patient is not present, check for a signed authorization before discussing anything
💻
Electronic Security — Protecting ePHI
▼
🔑 Access Controls
  • Never share your EHR login or password with anyone — not even your supervisor or IT staff
  • Do not write passwords on sticky notes or store them unencrypted
  • Use strong passwords: 12+ characters, mix of letters, numbers, symbols
  • Change your password if you suspect it has been compromised
  • Log out of all systems before leaving your workstation
🖥️ Workstation Security
  • Position screens so patients and visitors cannot see patient data
  • Enable auto-lock after 10 minutes of inactivity
  • Do not install unauthorized software on work computers
  • Do not use personal USB drives or external storage devices on work computers without IT approval
  • Report lost or stolen devices immediately
📧 Email & Messaging
  • Do not send PHI via personal email (Gmail, Yahoo, etc.)
  • Do not send PHI via regular SMS/text message
  • Use only the practice's HIPAA-compliant messaging system
  • If you receive a suspicious email (phishing), do not click links — report to IT
  • PHI sent to a wrong email address is a reportable breach
🚫Do not access patient records from personal devices (phones, personal laptops) unless the practice has specifically approved a HIPAA-compliant remote access solution. Accessing patient data on an unsecured personal device is a Security Rule violation.
🚨
Breach Response — What to Do When Something Goes Wrong Critical
▼
