⚠️A breach is any impermissible acquisition, access, use, or disclosure of unsecured PHI that compromises the privacy or security of that information. Examples: fax sent to wrong number, email to wrong patient, paper chart left in waiting room, EHR accessed by unauthorized person, lost laptop, ransomware attack.
Step-by-Step Breach Response
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Stop the breach if possible — retrieve fax, secure device, revoke access, contain the incident immediately
  • ✓
    Report immediately — notify your supervisor and the Privacy Officer the same day you discover it. Do not wait and hope it resolves itself.
  • ✓
    Document everything — what happened, when, what PHI was involved, who was affected, how it was discovered
  • ✓
    Privacy Officer assessment — the Privacy Officer conducts a risk assessment to determine if the incident meets the legal definition of a reportable breach
  • ✓
    Patient notification — if a reportable breach, affected patients must be notified within 60 days of discovery (written notice, specific content requirements)
  • ✓
    HHS reporting — breaches affecting fewer than 500 people: log annually and report to HHS by March 1 of the following year. Breaches of 500+ people: report to HHS within 60 days and notify prominent local media.
✅ Reportable Breach Examples
  • Fax with PHI sent to wrong provider office
  • Email with patient records sent to wrong email address
  • Paper chart left in an unsecured public area
  • EHR login used by an unauthorized person
  • Laptop or device with unencrypted PHI lost or stolen
  • Ransomware or unauthorized system access
⚠️ NOT Reportable (Safe Harbor)
  • PHI that was encrypted and the key was not compromised
  • PHI that was destroyed (properly shredded, wiped) and cannot be reconstructed
  • Unintentional access by an authorized workforce member acting in good faith with no further impermissible use
  • Inadvertent disclosure between two authorized people at the same covered entity with no further disclosure
Do Not Self-Determine That Something Is Not a BreachIt is not your job to decide whether an incident is legally a reportable breach. That determination belongs to the Privacy Officer after a formal risk assessment. Your job is to report it immediately and completely. Failing to report an incident that turns out to be a breach — because you thought it was minor — is itself a violation and can increase penalties significantly.
💰
Penalties & Real Enforcement Cases
▼
Civil Penalty Tiers (Per Violation Category, Per Year)
Tier 1 — Did Not Know

Violation the entity did not know about and could not have known with reasonable diligence.

$100 – $50,000 per violation
Max $25,000 / year per category

Tier 2 — Reasonable Cause

Violation due to reasonable cause (not willful neglect); corrected within 30 days.

$1,000 – $50,000 per violation
Max $100,000 / year per category

Tier 3 & 4 — Willful Neglect

Willful neglect — corrected or not corrected within 30 days.

$10,000 – $1,900,000 per violation
Max $1.9M / year per category

⚖️Criminal penalties: Knowingly obtaining or disclosing PHI: up to $50,000 and 1 year in prison. Under false pretenses: up to $100,000 and 5 years. Intent to sell or harm: up to $250,000 and 10 years.
Real Enforcement Examples — What Gets Practices Fined
📋 Small Practice Violations
  • $100,000: Solo provider practice — medical records sold to a personal injury attorney without patient authorization
  • $65,000: Small practice — paper records containing PHI left in a dumpster, found by a passerby
  • $32,150: Practice — workforce member accessed ex-partner's medical records out of personal curiosity
  • $10,000: Practice — faxes with PHI repeatedly sent to wrong numbers without corrective action
⚠️ Common Triggers for Investigations
  • Patient complaints filed directly with HHS OCR
  • News reports of data incidents
  • Breach reports submitted by the practice itself
  • Disgruntled employee tips
  • Patterns identified during routine audits
🔍HHS investigated over 35,000 complaints in a recent year. About 1 in 5 resulted in corrective action or fines.
👥
Role-Specific HIPAA Responsibilities
▼
🗂️ Front Desk
  • Verify caller identity before discussing any patient information
  • Use privacy screens; never leave patient demographics visible to waiting room
  • Collect only necessary information at intake
  • Handle ROI (release of information) requests through proper process only
  • Shred all PHI documents — never discard in regular trash
  • Do not share patient appointment details with callers who are not verified
🩺 Medical Assistants & VMAs
  • Lock screen every time you leave a workstation
  • Access only records relevant to your current task
  • Do not discuss patient cases in common areas or hallways
  • Never text or email PHI from personal devices
  • Report any accidental access or disclosure immediately
  • Do not add diagnoses or notes not directed by the provider
📅 Schedulers
  • Do not share scheduling details with anyone who is not the patient or a verified authorized contact
  • Use neutral appointment type names on shared or visible screens
  • Verify identity before any callback that discusses appointment specifics
  • Do not share patient contact information with other patients
📋 Management & Compliance (Privacy Officer Responsibilities)
  • Maintain and update the Notice of Privacy Practices (NPP) — ensure patients receive it and sign acknowledgment
  • Ensure all Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) are signed and current for all vendors who handle PHI
  • Conduct annual HIPAA training for all staff; document completion
  • Investigate all reported incidents and determine breach status
  • Maintain a breach log; submit annual report to HHS for minor breaches
  • Conduct periodic risk assessments of ePHI security
  • Maintain access control lists — terminate access promptly when employees leave
🌐 Remote & Work-From-Home Staff
  • Work only on devices approved by the practice for remote access
  • Use a VPN or the practice's approved remote access portal
  • Do not work on PHI in public places (coffee shops, airports) where screens can be seen
  • Do not print PHI at home unless specifically authorized and the printer is secured
  • Lock your home office when not in use if documents are present
  • Report any home device loss or breach of remote access credentials immediately
🧠
Knowledge Check — HIPAA Compliance Checklist
▼

Use this checklist to confirm your understanding. Every item should be second nature.

Progress:
0 / 12
  • ✓
    I know what PHI is and can identify all 18 identifiers
  • ✓
    I know the difference between treatment/payment/operations (no authorization needed) and other disclosures (authorization required)
  • ✓
    I understand the Minimum Necessary standard and apply it when sharing records
  • ✓
    I lock my screen every time I step away from a workstation
  • ✓
    I never share my EHR login credentials with anyone
  • ✓
    I verify fax numbers before sending and never fax without a HIPAA cover sheet
  • ✓
    I verify caller identity before discussing any patient information over the phone
  • ✓
    I do not send PHI via personal email or personal text messages
  • ✓
    I know what constitutes a breach and I report incidents immediately to my supervisor — I do not self-determine that it's "not a breach"
  • ✓
    I access only the patient records I need for my current task — not out of curiosity
  • ✓
    I know that HIPAA violations can result in personal fines and criminal prosecution — not just practice penalties
  • ✓
    I shred all paper PHI — I never discard patient information in regular trash
📚Reference: HHS HIPAA for Professionals · Breach Notification Rule · Privacy Rule Summary
📱
Social Media & Public Privacy
▼
🚫Never post, share, or discuss patient information on any social media platform — including Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, LinkedIn, Snapchat, or any other service — regardless of whether the patient's name is mentioned. Even posting a photo taken in the office that incidentally shows a patient's face, name on a whiteboard, or a chart is a potential HIPAA violation.
❌ Prohibited Actions
  • Posting photos from inside the office that could reveal patient identity
  • Sharing "interesting cases" on personal or professional social media — even without a name
  • Commenting on a patient's condition, appointment, or outcome online
  • Searching for or following patients on social media to gather personal information
  • Responding to a patient's public post about their care in a way that confirms they are your patient
✅ Acceptable Use
  • Sharing general health education content (not patient-specific)
  • Sharing the practice's official published posts and news
  • Acknowledging publicly that you work at Mir Neurology — but not commenting on any specific patient
  • If a patient contacts you via social media: direct them to call the office; do not discuss their care in DMs or public comments
⚠️Even if a patient publicly posts about their own care, responding to confirm details or add clinical context from your role as staff is a HIPAA violation. "I can't confirm whether someone is a patient here" is always the correct response to public or social media inquiries.
🔒
Extra-Sensitive PHI — Special Protections
▼
ℹ️Some categories of health information carry additional legal protections beyond standard HIPAA. Extra care is required when handling these records — even for treatment and payment purposes.
Categories With Extra Protections
  • Mental health / psychotherapy notes: Require separate authorization even for treatment. Notes from therapy sessions are protected more strictly than general mental health records.
  • Substance use disorder treatment (42 CFR Part 2): Federal law provides protections stricter than HIPAA. Cannot be re-disclosed without specific consent.
  • HIV/AIDS status: Many states (including Maryland) have additional privacy protections beyond HIPAA.
  • Genetic information (GINA): Cannot be used for employment or insurance decisions; extra care when sharing.
  • Minors: Parents generally have access to minor children's records, but exceptions apply (e.g., reproductive health, substance use, mental health — check Maryland law).
When Encountered at Mir Neurology
  • Neurology patients may have co-occurring psychiatric diagnoses — be careful about what is included in records released to non-treating parties
  • Requests for records that include substance use or mental health notes should be reviewed by the Privacy Officer before release
  • Genetic testing results (e.g., CYP2C9 for Mayzent, Huntington testing) should be treated with heightened sensitivity
  • When in doubt: ask before releasing. It is always better to briefly delay a release than to disclose improperly.
📄
Business Associate Agreements (BAAs)
▼
What Is a Business Associate?
  • Any person or organization that performs services for the practice that involve access to PHI — billing companies, IT vendors, transcription services, shredding companies, EHR vendors, answering services, cloud storage providers
  • Business Associates are legally required to sign a Business Associate Agreement (BAA) before accessing any PHI
  • The BAA obligates them to protect PHI to the same standards as HIPAA requires of covered entities
Your Responsibilities
  • Do not share PHI with any vendor, contractor, or third-party service without first confirming a BAA is on file
  • If you are approached by a new vendor wanting access to patient data, escalate to management before sharing anything
  • If a vendor's BAA expires or the vendor has a breach, the practice must be notified and act accordingly
  • Free personal tools (Google Drive, Dropbox, personal email) are NOT covered by a BAA and cannot be used for PHI under any circumstances
🗂️
Records Retention, Access Requests & Proper Disposal
▼
Retention Requirements
  • Maryland law: medical records must be retained for a minimum of 5 years from the date of service for adults
  • For minors: retained until the patient turns 21 or for 5 years from the last service date, whichever is longer
  • HIPAA policies and procedures: must be retained for 6 years
  • Do not destroy records on your own — all destruction must follow the approved retention schedule
Patient Records Requests
  • Patients have a right to access their records — we must respond within 30 days
  • Verify patient identity before releasing: photo ID + DOB, or written authorization
  • We may charge a reasonable cost-based fee for copies (Maryland sets specific fee limits)
  • We cannot deny access because an outstanding balance exists — this is a HIPAA violation
  • All requests go through the designated release of information (ROI) process — never hand records directly to a patient without logging the release
Proper Disposal of PHI
  • Paper PHI: Must be cross-cut shredded using the designated shredding bin — never placed in regular trash or recycling
  • Electronic devices: Must be wiped or destroyed by IT before disposal — do not throw away old computers, hard drives, or USB drives
  • Fax paper with PHI: Shred promptly — do not leave in a bin or on a desk
  • When in doubt: shred it or ask your supervisor. There is no such thing as "harmless" patient data in the trash.
📲
Texting, Mobile Devices & Patient Communication Apps
▼
🚫Standard SMS text messaging is NOT HIPAA-compliant. Sending PHI (any identifier + health information) via regular text is a Security Rule violation, even if you think the message is brief or innocent.
Prohibited
  • Texting patient names + diagnoses, medications, lab results, appointment reasons, or any combination of identifier + clinical information from personal phones
  • Using personal email (Gmail, Yahoo, iCloud) for any patient-related communication
  • Using WhatsApp, iMessage, Facebook Messenger, or other consumer messaging apps for PHI
  • Taking photos of patient documents or screens with a personal phone
  • Accessing the EHR from a personal device not approved by the practice
Acceptable Alternatives
  • Use the practice's EHR secure messaging system for all clinical communications
  • Use the practice's approved patient communication platform for appointment reminders and general messages
  • If you need to communicate urgently with a provider, use the secure internal channel — not personal text
  • HIPAA-compliant texting platforms (approved by the practice and covered by a BAA) may be used if the practice has established and authorized one
⚠️Even if a coworker sends you PHI via personal text, you have an obligation to report it — both the incident and the sender's conduct. Participating in a HIPAA-noncompliant communication chain can implicate you as well.
🎓
HIPAA Compliance Quiz & Certificate Required — All Staff
▼
🎓

HIPAA Compliance Quiz

20 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

Your certificate is ready — click below to download as a PNG image.


💵 Billing

Claim submission standards, procedure code rules, modifier requirements, and special billing workflows for Dr. Mir's practice.

Claim SubmissionCPT CodesModifiersICD-10Workers' CompMVA
📋
Claim Submission — SOPs for Production Step-by-Step
▼
Before You Begin — Patient & Provider Verification
Pick Patient from Scheduling
  • Select the patient from the scheduling queue
  • If a duplicate patient exists in scheduling, first verify whether a claim has already been created with correct details
  • If a correct claim already exists — do not create a duplicate
  • If no correct claim exists — proceed with the steps below
Verify Provider
  • Verify the provider details from the encounter
  • Bill the claim under the signed provider's name — not the ordering provider or another clinician
Step-by-Step Claim Submission Checklist
Progress:
0 / 13
  • ✓
    Step 1 — Verify Eligibility: Verify the patient's insurance eligibility for the date of service before proceeding
  • ✓
    Step 2 — Verify Encounter: Confirm the encounter is available for the date being billed. If multiple encounters exist for the same date, review all of them and create a single claim from all documented data
  • ✓
    Step 3 — Open Claim Section: If a draft claim already exists for the date, use the Edit option to update charges. If no claim exists for the date, use the Add Claim option
  • ✓
    Step 4 — Import Chart Details: All data must be entered in the claim from the encounter — do not manually key information that is documented in the encounter
  • ✓
    Step 5 — Verify Providers & Location: Confirm Rendering Provider, Billing Provider, and Service Location are all correct
  • ✓
    Step 6 — Review Procedure Codes: Verify all CPT codes, units, and any special modifiers (see detailed rules below)
  • ✓
    Step 7 — Review Billable ICDs: Review all diagnosis codes across all encounters for the date; assign correct primary and supporting diagnosis pointers to each CPT code
  • ✓
    Step 8 — Review Modifiers: Check if any additional modifiers are required and apply them appropriately
  • ✓
    Step 9 — Referral / Authorization: For Tricare, Workers' Comp, auto insurance, and any other insurance requiring auth — verify documents and enter Auth/Referral number; apply PCP name in ref physician field if required
  • ✓
    Step 10 — Finalize the Claim: Apply the Primary New option
  • ✓
    Step 11 — Save the Claim: Save the claim as a draft
  • ✓
    Step 12 — CCM/PCM/BHI/RPM: Update Date of Service to the date after 30-day period completion; include original encounter date in the 30-day count
  • ✓
    Step 13 — Workers' Comp & MVA: Verify insurance type, accident details, and complete all required WC/MVA fields; print HCFA 1500 if required for submission
⚕️
Procedure Code Rules — Step 6 Detail Critical
▼
General Rules
Telehealth Claims
  • Always use Place of Service 02 for telehealth
  • Always apply Modifier 95 to all telehealth procedure codes
Modifier 25 — Office Visit + Same-Day Service
  • Apply Modifier 25 to the office visit (E&M) code only if another service was performed on the same day
  • If no other service was performed alongside the office visit — do not apply Modifier 25
96132 & 96138 — Neuropsychological Testing
  • Add Modifier 59 to both CPT codes 96132 and 96138
EEG Claims
  • For telehealth EEG claims always use Place of Service 02 and Modifier 95
CPT J3420 — Vitamin B12 Injection (NDC Required)
💉If CPT code J3420 is on the claim, you must add the NDC code. Review the encounter first for the NDC. If it is not documented in the encounter, verify the label name and add the correct NDC accordingly.
CPT J0585 — Botulinum Toxin (Botox) — NDC + Units + Modifier Rules
J0585 Requires Careful Review — Two Different ScenariosAlways verify the units and whether any toxin was wasted before choosing how to bill J0585.
✅ Scenario A — All Units Used (No Waste)
  • Units are 200 or 100 and fully performed
  • Apply JZ modifier to J0585
  • Add NDC code: 00023-3921-02
  • Add UN – Unit in the qualifier tab
  • Bill J0585 once
⚠️ Scenario B — Some Units Performed, Some Wasted
  • Bill J0585 twice on the same claim:
  • Line 1: J0585 with performed units — no waste modifier
  • Line 2: J0585 with wasted units — apply JW modifier
  • Add NDC code 00023-3921-02 to both lines
  • Add UN – Unit in the qualifier tab on both lines
🏷️
ICD-10 & Diagnosis Pointer Rules — Step 7 Detail Critical
▼
⚠️Review all diagnosis codes across all encounters for the date of service. Apply only the relevant, billable, clinically justified ICD codes. Remove any unrelated or unsupported diagnosis codes before finalizing the claim.
Diagnosis Pointer Requirements for Each CPT Code
Primary Diagnosis Pointer
  • Assign the correct primary diagnosis pointer to each CPT code
  • The primary diagnosis must directly justify the procedure performed
Supporting Diagnoses
  • Include appropriate supporting diagnosis pointers as documented in the encounter
  • Do not link diagnoses that are not clinically related to the procedure
Clean-Up Before Finalizing
  • Remove any remaining or unrelated diagnosis codes from the claim
  • All selected diagnosis pointers must be billable and supported by documentation
  • If currently assigned ICDs are not appropriate for a CPT code based on the encounter, suggest and apply the correct ICD-10 codes
Diagnosis Pointer Checklist
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Reviewed all diagnosis codes across all encounters for the date of service
  • ✓
    Assigned correct primary diagnosis pointer to each CPT code
  • ✓
    Linked appropriate supporting diagnoses to each CPT code
  • ✓
    Removed all unrelated or unsupported diagnosis codes from the claim
  • ✓
    Confirmed all remaining diagnosis pointers are billable and clinically justified by documentation
🔑
Authorization & Referral Requirements — Step 9 Detail
▼
⚠️The following insurance types require referral/authorization before the claim can be submitted: Tricare, Workers' Comp, Auto Insurance (MVA), and any other plan that lists authorization or referral as a requirement. Always verify before finalizing.
Steps
  • Verify that a referral or prior authorization document is available
  • Enter the Auth number or Referral number in the appropriate field on the claim (Referral # or PA #)
  • If the insurance requires PCP information, apply the PCP name in the Referring Physician field
  • Ensure the authorization matches the date of service and the procedures being billed
Workers' Comp & MVA — Additional Requirements
  • Set claim to the correct insurance type (WC or MVA)
  • Verify accident date, claim number, employer/auto carrier information
  • Confirm all WC/MVA-specific fields are completed
  • Print HCFA 1500 form for submission if required by the carrier
🗓️
CCM / PCM / BHI / RPM — Date of Service Rules — Step 12 Detail
▼
ℹ️CCM, PCM, BHI, and RPM claims have a specific Date of Service rule — the DOS on the claim is not the encounter date. It must be calculated based on 30-day period completion.
30-Day Calculation Rule
✅ Correct Approach
  • Update the Date of Service field to the date that falls after completion of the full 30-day period
  • When counting the 30 days, include the original encounter date as Day 1
  • Ensure the full 30-day period is completed before billing — do not bill early
❌ Common Errors
  • Using the encounter date as the Date of Service
  • Not including the encounter date in the 30-day count
  • Billing before the full 30 days are completed
⚡
Quick Reference — Special Code & Modifier Rules
▼
Telehealth (All Claims)
  • Place of Service: 02
  • Modifier: 95 on all codes
Modifier 25
  • Apply to E&M code only if another service was performed same day
  • Do NOT apply if office visit was the only service
96132 & 96138
  • Add Modifier 59 to both codes
J3420 — B12 Injection
  • Add NDC code from encounter
  • If not in encounter, verify label and add correct NDC
J0585 — Botox (No Waste)
  • Modifier: JZ
  • NDC: 00023-3921-02
  • Qualifier: UN – Unit
  • Bill once
J0585 — Botox (With Waste)
  • Bill J0585 twice
  • Line 1 (performed): no waste modifier
  • Line 2 (wasted): Modifier JW
  • NDC 00023-3921-02 on both lines
  • Qualifier UN – Unit on both lines
💰
Accounts Receivable (AR) Workflow Step-by-Step
▼
Step 1 — Patient Account & Claim Review
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Open the patient's chart in TalkEHR and navigate to the Claims section
  • ✓
    Review the patient's account thoroughly to identify all outstanding balances for the relevant dates of service
  • ✓
    Select the claim corresponding to the specific date of service under review
  • ✓
    Open claim details and click Edit to access the claim information for further analysis and processing
Step 2 — Rejected Claims
If Claim Was NOT Passed via EDI
  • First verify the claim was submitted through EDI from the claim notes option
  • If not passed via EDI — review patient eligibility
  • Verify demographics, insurance information, payer selection, and payer ID
  • Correct any issues identified
  • Resubmit claim with correct claim status:
    • New claim: status = Primary New
    • Correction claim: status = Primary Billed
Claim Status Quick Reference
  • Primary New — first-time submission of a new claim
  • Primary Billed — correction/resubmission of a previously billed claim
  • Primary ReBilled — resubmission after a denial (see Step 4)
  • Patient New — transferring balance to patient responsibility
Step 3 — Not On File Claims
🔍If the claim was transmitted to the payer but no ERA response has been received, check the payer portal to verify claim status.
If Claim IS Found on Portal
  • Review status and proceed according to denial/payment instructions
If Claim is NOT Found on Portal
  • Escalate the issue immediately for confirmation over the phone
  • Do not resubmit without confirming — could result in a duplicate claim
Step 4 — Denials
Review ERA payment section first. Click the View option to check the breakdown status of all CPT codes. If any CPT code or the full claim is denied, review the remark code and resolve the denial reason per billing guidelines before resubmitting.
Standard Denial Resubmission
  • Resolve the denial reason per billing guidelines
  • Resubmit with claim status: Primary ReBilled
  • Select the Corrected Claim checkbox
  • Apply the correct ICN number
Medicare Denials — Different Process
  • Select status: Primary Rebill only
  • Do NOT select the Corrected Claim option for Medicare
  • Remove the ICN number if one was applied
If No Payment Showing in Payment Section & Claim Was Passed via EDI
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Log in to the specific insurance portal for the payer with the pending balance
  • ✓
    Enter patient details in the portal and check claim status for the specific date of service
  • ✓
    If paid: Download the EOB
  • ✓
    If denied: Work the denial per the instructions above
  • ✓
    If not on file: Escalate the issue for confirmation over the phone
PR Balance — Secondary Insurance Available
  • Review ERA payment section — click View to verify whether the claim was forwarded to secondary insurance
  • If forwarded: follow portal instructions above to get secondary claim status
  • If View option is not available in payment section: follow portal instructions above for secondary insurance directly
CCM / PCM / RPM / BHI & Cognitive Services — Noncovered Denials
  • If denied with noncovered charges — do not resubmit
  • Adjust the balance by selecting the Payment option (+)
  • Apply the adjustment for each denied CPT code individually
🔐If any portal requires an OTP (one-time passcode) for login — stop and ask for the OTP every time. Do not proceed without it.
Step 5 — Workers' Comp & Auto Insurance
📞For all Workers' Comp and auto insurance claims — escalate for confirmation over the phone. Do not attempt to work these independently through the portal workflow above.
Step 6 — Timely Filing Limit (TFL) Claims
If Denied as TFL Passed
  • Open the specific insurance portal
  • Enter patient details and retrieve claim status
  • Submit a TFL appeal through the portal
  • Provide claim creation proof and supporting documents for the specific date of service
After TFL Appeal
  • If appeal is upheld: proceed with adjustments (see Step 7)
  • If appeal is denied: escalate for further review
Step 7 — Adjustment SOPs
ℹ️Adjustments apply when there is a PR (patient responsibility) balance and no secondary insurance is available — OR when noncovered service codes need to be written off.
PR Balance — No Secondary Insurance
  • Transfer the balance to the patient
  • Change claim status to Patient New
CCM / PCM / RPM / BHI & Cognitive Services
  • These codes cannot use the Patient New status for adjustment
  • Instead: select the Payment option (+)
  • Apply the adjustment for each CPT code individually
AR Review — Complete Workflow Checklist
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Step 1 — Chart opened in TalkEHR, claim identified and opened in Edit mode
  • ✓
    Step 2 — EDI submission verified; if not passed via EDI, demographics/insurance corrected and resubmitted with correct status
  • ✓
    Step 3 — If no ERA response, portal checked; escalated if not on file
  • ✓
    Step 4 — ERA breakdown reviewed; denial reason identified and resolved; resubmitted with correct status and ICN (Medicare: rebill only, no corrected claim)
  • ✓
    Step 5 — Workers' Comp / MVA claims escalated for phone confirmation
  • ✓
    Step 6 — TFL denial appealed via portal with claim creation proof; adjustment applied if appeal upheld
  • ✓
    Step 7 — PR balance transferred to patient (Patient New) or adjusted via Payment (+) for CCM/PCM/RPM/BHI/cognitive services
🧾
Patient Collections SOP Collections Team
▼
Practice Policy — Upfront Collection RequiredAll patient deductibles and high coinsurance amounts must be collected before the appointment is confirmed. Do not confirm any appointment without first verifying the patient's financial responsibility and collecting what is owed. This applies to all visit types including new patients, follow-ups, and procedures.
Upfront Collection — What to Collect & When
Must Be Collected Before Appointment Confirmation
  • Deductibles — full or remaining deductible balance owed by the patient
  • High coinsurance amounts — collect at time of scheduling or at the latest before confirming the visit
  • Outstanding balances — existing unpaid patient balances should be addressed before a new appointment is confirmed
How to Verify Before Confirming
  • Run eligibility verification to confirm deductible and coinsurance amounts
  • Check the patient's account for any existing outstanding balance
  • Inform the patient of the amount due before confirming the appointment date
  • Collect payment or arrange a payment plan before the appointment is locked in
  • Document collection or arrangement in the patient's account
⚠️If the patient is unable or unwilling to pay the deductible or high coinsurance upfront, do not confirm the appointment without escalating to the supervisor or billing team for guidance. Do not independently waive this requirement.
Balance Verification Process
ℹ️Staff responsible for processing ERAs must verify all patient balances after insurance processing is complete before generating a statement or initiating collection.
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Confirm the balance should not be billed to a secondary insurance
  • ✓
    Confirm the balance should not be adjusted due to CCM, PCM, BHI, or Cognitive Assessment services
  • ✓
    Confirm all required insurance payments, adjustments, or write-offs have been correctly applied
  • ✓
    Confirm diagnosis codes have not been changed — any coding concerns must be escalated to management, not changed during this process
Patient Statements
When to Generate
  • Generate a patient statement only after the balance has been verified per the process above
  • Do not send statements for balances that have not completed verification
How to Distribute
  • Provide the statement to Mrs. Mir's Administrative Team or the Front Desk for mailing
  • Do not mail directly without this handoff
Collection Tracking
Adding to the Tracking Sheet
  • All patients with verified balances must be added to the Collection Tracking Sheet
  • Do not initiate collection contact before the account is on the tracking sheet
Collection Specialist Review
  • The Collection Specialist reviews and re-verifies all balances on the Collection Sheet before initiating any collection efforts
  • Verification by the Collection Specialist is a separate step from the ERA verification
Collection Activities & Communication Methods

The Collection Specialist contacts patients regarding outstanding balances using approved methods only.

📞 Phone

Direct phone call reminders to patients with outstanding balances

📧 Email

Email reminders — may be sent through Everyware

💬 Text

Text message reminders — may be sent through Everyware

Daily Collection Responsibilities
Progress:
0 / 5
  • ✓
    Ensure all amounts due from patients scheduled that day are collected whenever possible
  • ✓
    Verify that collected payments are accurately posted
  • ✓
    Confirm that payments are properly reflected within Everyware
  • ✓
    Answer patient questions regarding balances, statements, and payment responsibilities
  • ✓
    Escalate unresolved billing concerns to the appropriate billing or management staff
Special Considerations
CCM / PCM / BHI / RPM / Cognitive Services

Balances for these services may require adjustment rather than transfer to patient responsibility. Indications and supporting documentation must be reviewed before any adjustment is made.

Incomplete Contact Records

If a patient demographic record contains at least one valid contact number, the account should not be considered incomplete solely because an additional phone number is missing.

Diagnosis Codes

Diagnosis codes must not be changed during the collections process. All coding concerns must be escalated to management for review.

🤝
Financial Aid Policy — Mir Neurology & Spine Center
▼
ℹ️Mir Neurology & Spine Center is committed to assisting patients who experience financial hardship and may be unable to pay certain out-of-pocket healthcare expenses.
Eligibility & Covered Amounts
✅ Who May Qualify
  • Medicare beneficiaries experiencing financial hardship
  • Patients with very low income or documented financial difficulties
✅ Covered Balance Types
  • Coinsurance amounts
  • Copayments
  • Small outstanding patient balances
⚠️Large account balances generally do not qualify for Financial Aid and require management review and approval before any adjustment is considered.
Required Documentation
Patient Must Provide
  • Proof of income or documented financial hardship
  • A completed and signed Financial Hardship Form
Documentation Handling
  • The signed Financial Hardship Form and income documentation must be scanned and maintained in the patient's chart
  • Financial Aid adjustments must not be processed until required documentation has been received and reviewed
Approval Process
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Collection Specialist or designated staff gathers required documentation (Financial Hardship Form + proof of income)
  • ✓
    Management reviews the request and determines eligibility
  • ✓
    Approved adjustments are documented in the patient account with supporting notes
  • ✓
    All Financial Aid approvals are supported by documentation retained in the patient's chart
No Documentation = No AdjustmentFinancial Aid adjustments must never be processed before the required documentation has been received, reviewed, and scanned into the patient chart. Management approval is required for all Financial Aid decisions.
🎓
Billing Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Billing Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

📝 Prior Authorization

Step-by-step PA workflows, neurology procedures that require authorization, payer-specific submission instructions, and the denial and appeal process.

PA WorkflowAvailityMRIBotoxEMGEEGCareFirstAetnaUHCMedicareDenial AppealPeer-to-Peer
Prior Authorization — Common Pitfalls

⚠ PA Don'ts — Mistakes That Affect Patient Care

These are the most common missteps in prior authorization work. Reviewing them helps everyone stay on track and avoid gaps that slow down care for our patients.

🚫 Don't Leave MRI PAs Untracked
Every MRI order needs a PA initiated, submitted, and followed to a decision. Starting the request isn't enough — it must be seen through to completion.
🚫 Don't Delay Submissions Past 24–48 Hours
PA requests should be submitted within 24–48 hours of receiving the order. Delays push back the patient's care and create backlogs that are hard to recover from.
🚫 Don't Let Orders Go Without Follow-Through
If a patient returns and their ordered imaging or medication was never completed, that is a gap in care. Every order must be tracked until the patient actually receives it.
🚫 Don't Allow Unnecessary Delays
Once a medication or imaging study is approved, move quickly. Delays between approval and the patient receiving care should be minimized at every step.
🚫 Don't Leave the Team Without an Update
When a provider or MA asks for a PA status, respond promptly. Keeping colleagues informed prevents them from having to chase information during a patient visit.
🚫 Don't Push Back on Reasonable Requests
The PA team's role is to solve problems, not create new ones. When a request comes in, the default should be to find a way to get it done.
🚫 Don't Treat Cases Without Urgency
Every item in the PA queue is a real patient waiting for care. Treat each case with the same sense of urgency you would want if it were your own family member.
The goal: Every prior authorization is initiated promptly, tracked through the Google Sheet, communicated clearly to the team, and followed through until the patient receives what was ordered. When problems arise, escalate them — don't let them sit unresolved.
Required Operational Tool

📊 Shared PA & Orders Tracking — Google Sheet

All imaging orders, medication prior authorizations, and clinically important ordered studies must be logged and tracked on the shared PA Tracking Google Sheet. This is not optional — it is the primary accountability tool for the PA team and must be kept current at all times.

Status Categories
📋 Ordered
Order placed by provider — PA process not yet started
📤 Submitted
PA request sent to payer — awaiting acknowledgment
⏳ In Process
Under payer review — decision pending
✅ Approved
PA granted — auth number and expiration recorded
❌ Denied
PA refused — denial reason documented; appeal path initiated
📞 Needs Peer-to-Peer
Provider peer-to-peer review requested or scheduled
🤙 Patient Contacted
Patient notified of approval or next steps
📅 Scheduled
Appointment or service booked — waiting to occur
✔ Completed
Service or procedure has taken place
📥 Result Received
Lab or imaging result is back — provider notified
🗂 Closed
Case fully resolved — no further action needed
Row Color Coding
Green — Approved / Completed
PA approved, service completed, or result received and reviewed. No action pending.
Yellow — In Process
Submitted or under payer review. Active tracking required — check daily.
Red — Denied / Urgent Problem
PA denied or a critical problem exists (expired auth, unresponsive payer, etc.). Requires immediate attention.
Orange — Clinically Urgent
Order is time-sensitive due to clinical need. Expedited PA processing required. Escalate to provider if needed.
Gray — Closed
Fully resolved — case closed with no outstanding tasks. Kept for records.
Required tracking fields for each entry: Patient name · Date of birth · Insurance / payer · Item ordered (study, medication, service) · Date ordered · Date submitted · Current status · Color code · Auth number (when approved) · Expiration date · Notes / next action · Staff responsible

Update frequency: Every row must be updated each time there is any change in status. Do not leave items stagnant. Any row that has not been updated in 3+ business days should be reviewed and escalated.
Patient Safety Protocol

🚨 Urgent Imaging & Safety Concerns — Escalation Pathway

Certain clinical presentations require imaging to be treated as a medical urgency — not a routine PA request. When one of these presentations is identified, the standard timeline does not apply. Escalate immediately.

Presentations That Require Urgent Imaging Escalation
🦴 Cord Compression
Possible spinal cord compression — any level. Time-sensitive risk of permanent neurological injury.
📉 Worsening or Progressive Weakness
New or rapidly worsening weakness in any limb — especially if bilateral or progressing over days.
🔱 Cauda Equina Symptoms
Bowel or bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, bilateral leg weakness or numbness — surgical emergency.
⚡ Severe Radiculopathy
Severe, debilitating nerve root pain — especially with neurological deficits (foot drop, hand weakness, sensory loss).
📈 Rapidly Progressive Symptoms
Any neurological symptom that is rapidly worsening over hours or days — regardless of cause — requires prompt evaluation.
Escalation Steps — Follow in Order: 1. Identify the urgency — recognize that the clinical scenario involves one of the presentations above when reviewing the order or PA request. 2. Flag as urgent in the tracking sheet — mark the row Orange (Clinically Urgent) and add a note with the clinical reason. 3. Contact the payer immediately — request an expedited or urgent review. Most payers are required to respond to urgent PAs within 24–72 hours (vs. the standard 3–5 business days). State the clinical urgency clearly. 4. Notify the provider the same day — do not wait until the next day to tell the provider that a PA is pending, delayed, or denied for an urgent imaging order. Bring it to their attention immediately. 5. If denied — a denied urgent imaging request must be brought to the provider's attention the same day. The provider may initiate a peer-to-peer call or authorize the patient to go directly for imaging with a self-pay option pending appeal. A delay in urgent imaging is a patient safety event. Treat it accordingly.

💻 CoverMyMeds (CMM) — Primary PA Submission Tool

CoverMyMeds is the main platform for completing prescription medication prior authorizations. All staff processing medication PAs must have an account.

🔐
CMM Setup — Account & Provider Configuration
▼
Creating Your Account
  • Go to CoverMyMeds.com
  • Click the red CREATE A FREE ACCOUNT button (top right corner)
  • Complete the series of setup questions
  • Open all tabs and enter information for all providers — this is critical so PAs route correctly
How CMM Receives PA Requests
  • When a provider sends a prescription to a pharmacy that requires PA, the pharmacy may route it directly to CMM
  • You will receive both an email notification and likely a fax that a PA needs to be completed
  • Note: Not all pharmacies send PAs to CMM — some fax your office directly instead
🔑
Workflow A — PA Received via CMM (Key + Patient Name + DOB)
▼
ℹ️CMM faxes and emails will include a KEY, patient NAME, and DOB. Use these to pull up the PA directly.
  1. Log in to CoverMyMeds.com
  2. Click the red ENTER KEY button — top left corner of the page
  3. Enter the key from the fax or email
  4. Follow the on-screen directions to complete the PA form
🏦
Workflow B — PA Received Directly from Pharmacy (BIN / PCN / RxGroup)
▼
⚠️Pharmacy faxes that come directly to your office (not routed through CMM) may include BIN, PCN, RxGroup #, and ID#. You can still use CMM to complete these.
Steps to Submit via CMM
  1. Log in to CoverMyMeds.com
  2. Click the blue NEW REQUEST button — top left corner
  3. Enter the correct medication and dosage
  4. Under Patient Insurance, enter the patient's state, BIN, PCN, and RxGroup# (you can proceed with at least 2 of these numbers)
  5. CMM will generate the correct form(s) at the bottom of the page — click to open and complete
  6. If more than one form is generated, always choose the first form unless you specifically need a different form type (e.g., quantity limit exception)
Finding BIN / PCN / RxGroup from Allscripts

If the fax doesn't include the pharmacy insurance numbers:

  1. Open the PM side of Allscripts → go to Scheduling from the left Functions menu
  2. Choose Appointment Scheduling → then Patient Scheduling from the blue tabs
  3. In the patient window, enter: first 3 letters of last name, comma, first 3 letters of first name → click the binoculars icon
  4. Double-click the correct patient from the list
  5. Click the camera icon at the top of the PVH page — the patient's insurance information will appear
  6. Double-click the patient's RX CARD from the image category (if no RX Card, use their PRIMARY INSURANCE) — one of these will have all the numbers you need
📞If all else fails, call the patient's pharmacy and ask for their BIN, PCN, RxGroup, and ID#. Then return to CMM Workflow B above.

⚙️ General Prior Authorization Workflow

The universal process for any PA request regardless of payer or service type.

📋
PA Process — Step by Step
▼
Step 1 — Verify PA Requirement
  • Check the patient's insurance card and verify the plan
  • Look up whether the ordered service or procedure requires PA for that specific plan
  • Use Availity, the payer's portal, or call the Provider Services line
  • Confirm the patient's eligibility is active on the date of service
  • If PA is not required, document this and proceed with scheduling
Step 2 — Gather Required Information
  • Patient demographics (name, DOB, member ID, address)
  • Insurance plan name, group number, and payer ID
  • Ordering provider name and NPI
  • ICD-10 diagnosis codes (primary + secondary)
  • CPT/procedure code(s) being requested
  • Most recent office note (signed, within 90 days)
  • Supporting clinical records (imaging, labs, specialist notes, prior treatment history)
Step 3 — Submit the PA Request
  • Log in to the appropriate payer portal (Availity, NaviNet, etc.) or complete the payer's PA form
  • Enter all required fields accurately — errors delay review
  • Attach clinical documentation electronically or fax per payer instructions
  • Record the submission date and authorization reference/tracking number
  • Keep the fax confirmation or portal submission receipt
Step 4 — Track & Follow Up
  • Check the portal or call payer status line 3–5 business days after submission
  • If additional information (questionnaire) is requested, respond promptly
  • Document every follow-up contact: date, rep name, reference number, status
  • Standard turnaround: 3–14 business days depending on payer and urgency
  • Urgent / expedited requests: 24–72 hours — request expedited review when clinically necessary
Step 5 — Authorization Approved
  • Record the authorization number in the patient's chart immediately
  • Note the approved service(s), approved quantity/units, and validity dates
  • Communicate to scheduling — do not schedule until PA is confirmed
  • Attach the approval letter or portal confirmation to the patient's chart
Step 6 — Denied → Appeal
  • Review the denial reason carefully
  • Determine if a first-level appeal, additional documentation, or peer-to-peer is the best path
  • See the Denial & Appeal section below for the full process
✅
PA Submission Checklist — Universal
▼
Progress:
0 / 10
  • ✓
    PA requirement confirmed for this procedure + this payer (verified via portal or phone)
  • ✓
    Patient eligibility active on the intended date of service
  • ✓
    Patient demographics, member ID, and insurance plan details verified and accurate
  • ✓
    Ordering provider NPI confirmed and correct
  • ✓
    ICD-10 diagnosis codes identified and confirmed with provider
  • ✓
    CPT / procedure codes confirmed — matches exactly what was ordered
  • ✓
    Most recent signed office note attached (within 90 days preferred)
  • ✓
    Supporting clinical documentation attached (imaging, labs, prior treatment history)
  • ✓
    Submission reference number recorded and documented in patient chart
  • ✓
    Follow-up date noted — calendar reminder set for status check in 3–5 business days

🩺 Neurology Procedures Commonly Requiring PA

PA requirements vary by payer and plan. Always verify for the specific patient. This list reflects common requirements for neurology practices.

🔬
Procedures & Services Requiring Authorization
▼
⚠️Medicare generally does not require PA for most outpatient neurology services, but Medicare Advantage plans do. Always check the specific plan, not just the payer name.
🧲 Imaging
  • MRI Brain (with/without contrast) — most commercial plans require PA
  • MRI Spine (cervical, thoracic, lumbar) — PA commonly required
  • MRI with spectroscopy — almost always requires PA
  • CT Brain/Spine — PA varies by payer; often required for advanced CT
  • PET Scan — requires PA for all major commercial payers
⚡ Neurology Procedures
  • EMG/NCS — PA required by many commercial payers; Medicare typically does not require
  • EEG (routine & ambulatory) — varies by payer
  • Video EEG Monitoring — PA almost always required
  • Nerve Block Injections — typically requires PA for commercial plans
  • Cognitive Assessment / Neuropsych Testing (96132, 96138) — see Cognitive Assessment tab for payer-specific PA workflows
💉 Medications & Infusions
  • Botox — PA required for chronic migraine (31 units+), spasticity, dystonia on virtually all plans
  • Ocrevus — PA required for all payers; see Infusions tab
  • Leqembi / Kisunla — PA required; see Infusions tab
  • Tysabri / Natalizumab — PA required; TOUCH Program enrollment also required
  • IV Immunoglobulin (IVIg) — PA required by virtually all payers
  • Specialty oral medications (e.g., fingolimod, dimethyl fumarate) — PA required for most commercial plans
🩺 Other Services
  • Sleep Study / Polysomnography — PA required by most commercial payers
  • CPAP / BiPAP Equipment — PA or certificate of medical necessity required
  • Genetic Testing — PA required by most payers
  • Outpatient Rehabilitation (PT, OT, ST) — PA/visit limits vary
  • DME — refer to the DME section; most items require prior authorization
✅ Typically No PA Required (Medicare FFS)
  • Standard E&M office visits (99202–99215)
  • EMG/NCS for traditional Medicare (not Medicare Advantage)
  • Routine EEG — most Medicare plans
  • Basic lab orders (CBC, CMP, etc.)
Always verify — Medicare Advantage plans do impose PA requirements
❌ Never Perform Without Confirmed PA
  • Infusion services for high-cost biologics
  • Botox injections when PA is required
  • Advanced imaging (MRI, PET) when PA is required
  • Neuropsychological testing for non-accepted CA plans
Performing a service without required PA = claim denial + potential patient liability

🧠 Headache & Migraine Medications — PA Procedures

CGRP medications, triptans, and preventive agents have specific step-therapy and contraindication rules. Review this section carefully before submitting any headache medication PA.

💉
CGRP Medications — Step Therapy Requirements Aimovig · Ajovy · Emgality
▼
⚠️CGRP medications are non-formulary for most insurances and will all require a PA. Insurances typically require the patient to have tried and failed an antidepressant, anticonvulsant, and/or a beta blocker. Read each question carefully and include the correct number of trials and failures requested.
Finding Prior Trial Documentation
  • Check the patient's previous and current medication list first
  • If not found there, conduct a thorough review of chart notes going back years
  • Check referring doctor notes from before the patient first saw us
  • If you still cannot demonstrate what the insurance wants, check whether the requested medications are contraindicated for this patient (other diagnoses, drug interactions, etc.)
  • Ask the provider who prescribed the medication, or look up contraindications via Google
Insurance-Specific CGRP Step Therapy Rules
InsuranceRequired Step Therapy
UNICAREAimovig AND Emgality 90-day trial before Ajovy
CareFirst BC/BSAjovy first
CareFirst BC/BS CVS CaremarkEmgality first
Federal BC/BSEmgality OR Aimovig first
MPCAimovig, Ajovy, OR Emgality
Aetna Better Health of MDAimovig AND Emgality before Ajovy
Johns Hopkins Health Plan PPOAjovy; also try at least 2 of: beta antagonists (propranolol, metoprolol), anti-seizures (valproate, topiramate), antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline, venlafaxine) — all within the last 180 days without adequate effect
ℹ️CareFirst BCBS PPO — currently not requiring PA for Emgality. CF BC/BS MD PPO does not need a PA for Emgality per pharmacy.
⚠️
Contraindications — Triptans, Beta Blockers, Anticonvulsants, Antidepressants
▼
ℹ️When an insurance requires the patient to have tried these medication classes, document why they could not — contraindications, interactions, or other diagnoses. This is your clinical justification.
Triptans — ACUTE ATTACK Medications

Almotriptan (Axert) · Eletriptan (Relpax) · Frovatriptan (Frova) · Naratriptan (Amerge) · Rizatriptan (Maxalt, Maxalt MLT) · Sumatriptan (Imitrex, Treximet) · Zolmitriptan (Zomig, Zomig ZMT)
Ubrelvy & Nurtec — not triptans; require try-and-fail of at least 2 triptans. Nurtec can also be PREVENTIVE.

Contraindications:

  • Coronary artery disease / hypertension / hyperlipidemia / peripheral vascular disease
  • Hemiplegic or basilar migraine (stroke risk)
  • Concurrent use of Ergots, SSRIs, TCAs
  • Pregnancy and lactation
  • Qulipta (gepant class — review separately)
Beta Blockers — PREVENTIVE Medications

Acebutolol · Atenolol · Betaxolol · Bisoprolol/HCTZ · Bisoprolol · Metoprolol (Lopressor, Toprol XL) · Nadolol · Propranolol (Inderal) · Sotalol · Carvedilol (Coreg CR, Coreg)

Contraindications:

  • Asthma and COPD
  • Prinzmetal angina / bradycardia / heart block
  • Acute decompensated heart failure
  • Peripheral vascular disease / Raynaud's disease
  • Diabetes mellitus / depression
Anticonvulsants — PREVENTIVE Medications

Carbamazepine · Clonazepam · Diazepam · Felbamate · Gabapentin (Neurontin) · Lacosamide (Vimpat) · Lamotrigine · Levetiracetam (Keppra) · Lorazepam · Nitrazepam · Oxcarbazepine · Phenobarbital · Phenytoin (Dilantin) · Pregabalin (Lyrica) · Primidone · Topiramate (Topamax) · Valproic Acid (Depakene, Divalproex) · Zonisamide · Losartan (Cozaar)

Antidepressants — PREVENTIVE Medications

TCAs: Amitriptyline (Elavil) · Amoxapine · Clomipramine · Desipramine · Doxepin · Imipramine · Maprotiline · Nortriptyline (Pamelor) · Protriptyline · Trimipramine
SSRIs: Citalopram · Escitalopram · Fluoxetine · Fluvoxamine · Paroxetine · Sertraline
SNRIs: Duloxetine (Cymbalta) · Venlafaxine (Effexor) · Desvenlafaxine
Others: Mirtazapine · Trazodone · Bupropion · Nefazodone

Key Contraindications:

  • Elderly · Decreased liver/kidney function · High BUN · Diabetes
  • Hereditary porphyria · Significant alcohol use
  • Thai or Han Chinese ethnicity (carbamazepine)
  • Pregnancy/breastfeeding · Bipolar disorder · Schizophrenia
  • Bleeding disorders / blood thinners · Uncontrolled epilepsy
  • Heart disease / uncontrolled hypertension / glaucoma / enlarged prostate
ℹ️Botox — every 12 weeks. Qulipta — preventative. CGRPs (Emgality, Ajovy, Aimovig) — injectable preventives, once monthly. Qulipta pill — preventative. Additional contraindications: Myasthenia Gravis and allergy to botulinum toxins for Botox; keloidal scarring. Savella — used for fibromyalgia (M79.7); confirm diagnosis with provider before submitting PA.
💡
PA Tips — Specific Medication Rules & Workarounds
▼
  • Ubrelvy / Nurtec: Insurance will want to see try-and-fail of two triptans. If the patient hasn't tried 2, document the clinical reason. When asked for duration of treatment, document undetermined.
  • Tizanidine Capsules: Call or fax the pharmacy to switch to tablets instead. Check with provider first. Also note: 6 mg will not go through insurance — write as 2 mg + 4 mg.
  • Butalbital / Tramadol (Medicare): Medicare requires a Quantity Limit Exception. Complete through CMM — choose Quantity Limit Exception when asked.
  • Propranolol for tremors: Can sometimes cause a denial. Look up FDA prescribing information for Propranolol for tremors and include with the PA submission.
  • Cymbalta / Duloxetine: Must be written for max dosing. No PA needed if written as: 30 mg 1 cap a.m. AND 60 mg 1 cap p.m. NOT as 30 mg 2 caps p.m. May also require a Quantity Limit Exception for more than 1 cap daily of 60 mg or higher.
  • Topamax: Insurance will not pay for 100 mg BID — write as 200 mg, ½ tab BID. Insurance will not pay for more than 30 pills/month.
  • MD Straight Medicaid: If a PA goes to straight MD Medicaid, you can fill out a paper PA. CMM will send it back for you to enter more information.
  • Epilepsy/Seizure PAs: Include: diagnosis, how long patient has been on medication, and note that changing therapy could cause breakthrough seizures and/or death.
  • Aptiom & Briviact: Require a specific diagnosis — partial onset seizures or localization-related seizures.
  • WV Medicaid CGRP: Requires 90 consecutive days of prophylactic medications, name and NPI of consulting provider, recent MIDAS score (within last 3 months), and explanation of contraindications.

🧬 Multiple Sclerosis (MS) Medications — PA Procedures

All MS medications require prior authorization and have specific pre-treatment requirements. Follow all 5 steps for every MS medication PA.

📋
MS Medication PA — 5-Step Process All MS Meds — Required
▼
🚨All MS medications require prior authorization — no exceptions. Complete all 5 steps before the patient starts any MS therapy.
Step 1 — All MS medications have start forms that must be completed and signed before the patient leaves the office. Labs or pre-treatment tests are required before the patient can start any medication.
Step 2 — When submitting PA to the insurance company (not the infusion center), include: office notes, labs, and diagnostic imaging reports (MRIs, CT scans). Include both older and newer notes to show disease progression. Document MS medications previously tried and failed, and why other medications are not appropriate for the patient's disease stage. Also include the infusion center name, Tax ID, and NPI when applicable.
Step 3 — Appeal the decision if denied. Do not give up on the first denial — review the denial reason and determine the best appeal path (letter, peer-to-peer, additional documentation).
Step 4 — Fax the authorization, labs, test results, and office visit notes to the infusion center. Call the infusion center to confirm receipt and ask if they need anything else.
Step 5 — Patient assistance programs: The medication must first be run through the patient's insurance and denied before the patient can apply to receive it for free through a manufacturer PAP. See the Medication Assistance Programs tab for program details.
🧪
MS Medications — Pre-Treatment Requirements by Drug
▼
⚠️Verify the most current start form for each medication with the manufacturer rep. Requirements may change. Use the form that is current at the time of initiation.
Aubagio (teriflunomide)
  • Pre-treatment labs (within 6 months before start): CBC with Platelets & Auto Diff, Liver Function Tests (LFTs), TB Test, Cell-Mediated Immune Measure
  • Repeat CBC and LFTs every 6 months for 6 months after starting
Tecfidera / Vumerity (dimethyl fumarate / diroximel fumarate)
  • Monitor for low lymphocyte count — check CBC every 6 months
  • If lymphocyte count drops, notify provider promptly
Tysabri (natalizumab) — MEDICAL BENEFITS — Not Pharmacy
  • Patient needs two separate authorizations: (1) TOUCH® prescribing program, AND (2) Insurance PA
  • TOUCH authorization is valid for 6 months — a re-authorization form is sent 1 month before it expires; TOUCH will confirm approval separately
  • JCV antibody (John Cunningham Virus) must be checked every 6 months
  • Currently infused at Schwab Cancer Center (WMHS), Cumberland or John R. Marsh Cancer Center, Hagerstown
  • After PA is obtained, send paperwork to the infusion center
Gilenya (fingolimod)
  • Pre-treatment requirements: CBC with Platelets & Auto Diff, Comprehensive Metabolic Panel, Varicella-Zoster Antibody
  • Eye appointment (ophthalmology) and dermatology appointment required before starting
  • First Dose Observation (FDO) required — includes EKG. (Labs and EKG currently done in-home.)
Mayzent (siponimod)
  • Eye appointment and EKG required before starting (currently done in-home)
  • Labs: CBC with Platelets & Auto Diff, CYP2C9 Genotype (required — determines dosing)
  • Cardiac Evaluation required
  • Start Form: Mayzent Start Form (obtain current form from Novartis rep)
  • Novartis rep — Anthony Mullins: Cell 606-748-3111
Ocrevus (ocrelizumab) — MEDICAL BENEFITS — Not Pharmacy
  • Pre-treatment labs: IgM, IgG, Hepatitis B Core Antibody (IgG/IgM), Hepatitis B Surface Antigen, Hepatitis C Antibody, CBC with Platelets & Auto Diff, Comprehensive Metabolic Panel, Varicella-Zoster Antibody
  • After PA is obtained, send paperwork to the infusion center
  • New Start Dosing: 300 mg on Day 1 → 300 mg on Day 15 → then 600 mg every 6 months. (Comes in 300 mg vials — 2 vials needed for each 600 mg dose.)
  • Ocrevus Patient Navigator: 1-800-888-2882
  • Start Form: Ocrevus Start Form (obtain current version from Genentech)
  • Premedication protocol: 30 minutes before infusion

💉 Botox — Prior Authorization & Ordering Process

Complete end-to-end workflow from new patient intake through drug delivery and appointment confirmation. Follow every step in order.

📋
Botox Codes, Requirements & Quick Reference
▼
CPT Codes by Indication
  • 64612 — Blepharospasm
  • 64615 — Chronic Migraine
  • 64616 — Cervical Dystonia
  • 64642 — Upper limb spasticity
  • 64653 — Hyperhidrosis
J-Codes
  • J0585 — Botox (onabotulinumtoxinA), per unit
  • J0586 — Dysport, per 5 units
  • J0587 — Myobloc, per 100 units
  • J0588 — Xeomin, per 1 unit
Always — Medical Benefits, NOT Pharmacy

ALWAYS initiate Botox through MEDICAL BENEFITS — never pharmacy benefits. When calling insurance, state: "I am requesting a PA for Botox [# Units], J code J0585, for CPT [code + indication], by [Provider name and NPI#], at [location]. For chronic migraine: UP TO 200 or 100 units to be given, in office, by [Provider], every 12 weeks into head, neck and facial muscles. Remainder to be discarded."

Botox Savings Program — Non-Medicaid Patients
  • Give new patients (except Medicaid) the Botox Savings Program info at checkout
  • Text SAVE to 27747, or visit BOTOXSavingsProgram.com, or call 1-800-44-BOTOX
  • When sending Rx to pharmacy, patient must call and give their BIN number from the program
  • Savings up to $1,000 — can apply to both office visit copay and medication, as long as total does not exceed $1,000
⚠️Humana Medicare: Requires auth for J code J0585 (not CPT code) — they do pay at regular Medicare rates.
🆕
New Patient — Botox Start Process
▼
Provider
  • Complete the Botox Quick Start Form: Patient Name, DOB, Units, Dx Code, Procedure Code
  • Ensure all office notes completed within 1–2 days to avoid PA delays
  • Document all previously tried and failed Prophylactic / Acute / Abortive medications (see Botox Requirements Worksheet)
Medical Assistant
  • Obtain patient consent for Botox ONE (benefit verification and PA) — patient must sign
  • Scan the completed Quick Start Form and signed consent into the chart
  • Send to Authorization Specialist (April) — this officially starts the Botox process
Front Desk
  • Give all new patients (except Medicaid) the Botox Savings Program information at checkout
  • For existing patients: send an EHR message to the Authorization Specialist with the patient's next injection date
✅
Authorization Specialist — Benefit Verification (Botox ONE)
▼
ℹ️Use Botox ONE for all Benefit Verifications and to initiate PAs. Enter the patient into the Botox Start List Spreadsheet upon receiving the Quick Start Form.
New Patient — BV Workflow
  1. Enter Demographics
  2. Enter Insurance
  3. Enter Treatment Details
  4. Set Preferences / fax number
  5. Request a Prior Auth during BV
  6. Submit
  7. Check Notifications in 1–2 days
Existing Patient — BV Workflow
  1. Confirm Patient Details / Insurance
  2. Confirm Treatment Details
  3. Update Preferences as needed
  4. Request a Prior Auth during BV
  5. Submit
  6. Check Notifications in 1–2 days
Review BV Results For:
  • Insurance policy START/END dates
  • Whether CPT/J-codes need authorization
  • Patient Copay / Coinsurance
  • BUY & BILL vs. Specialty Pharmacy
  • PA Requirements (forms, clinical docs, call)
  • Submission method (fax / website / call)
  • Additional PA instructions for J code under Medical Benefits
  • Specialty Pharmacy name and phone number
  • Plan Renewal date
🔑
Authorization Specialist — PA Submission & Approval (Botox ONE Patient Management Tab)
▼
New Patient — PA Workflow
  1. Enter Demographics
  2. Enter Insurance
  3. Click BV Details
  4. Click Initiate PA
  5. Complete PA Form OR Upload Form
  6. Upload Notes
  7. Check Notifications daily for response
Existing Patient — PA Workflow
  1. Click BV Details
  2. Click Initiate PA
  3. Complete PA Form OR Upload Form
  4. Upload Notes
  5. Check Notifications daily for response
When Approval Is Received — Chart Note Must Include:
  • Estimated out-of-pocket cost
  • START / END date of authorization
  • Authorization number
  • How many visits / units approved
  • Drug obtained by Buy & Bill or Specialty Pharmacy
ℹ️Add patient to the Botox Master List and complete all above fields. Document Order Date and Expected Delivery Date on the Master List after ordering.
🛒
Authorization Specialist — Ordering Botox
▼
Medicare / Humana — Buy & Bill (Allergan)
  1. Order through Allergan Buy & Bill (BV from Botox ONE provides information)
  2. Enter NPI (provider-specific) and Tax ID: 52-2091771
  3. Choose Location (Hagerstown or Cumberland)
  4. Choose BOTOX Therapeutic
  5. Choose # of Units
  6. Choose Bill Me
  7. Delivery typically 1–2 days
Allergan B&B Contacts:
Orders: 800-377-7790
Acct # Cumberland: 59198080
Acct # Hagerstown: 59143143
Jennifer (clinical): 443-699-3554
Emily (auth): 410-310-7109
Commercial / Medicaid — Specialty Pharmacy
  1. BV from Botox ONE will provide Specialty Pharmacy (SP) information
  2. New Patient: Call SP to find out if patient is enrolled; fill out SP enrollment form if not; SP will also call patient to enroll — patient must answer phone
  3. Fill out SP Order Form; send with Demographics, what is being ordered, and a separate Prescription
  4. Existing Patient: Call SP or order through SP website
📅
Monthly Schedule Review & Office Responsibilities
▼
Authorization Specialist — Monthly Schedule Review
  • Review schedule on the first week of each month for Botox appointments in the following month (e.g., review February in January)
  • This allows time to renew authorizations and place orders in advance
  • Every Monday: confirm all Botox has been received or ordered for that week's appointments; confirm patient insurance eligibility; document in Master List
  • After approval: call patient to schedule and go over estimated OOP costs / copays, and advise if patient assistance may apply
🖥️To pull Botox patient list from Allscripts: Scheduling Reports → Report Preferences → open piece of paper icon → OK → Select Appt Type (4 boxes with 2 checks) → click BOTOX → Enter appointment dates (from/to) → Run report.
Office Staff — Drug Receipt & Storage
  • Label Botox with patient's name and received date immediately upon delivery
  • Store in the refrigerator in the nurse's office at each location
  • Hagerstown: All accompanying paperwork → Iain's In-Box
  • Cumberland: Paperwork comes back on Tuesdays with Melissa → placed in Iain's In-Box
  • Send a Google Chat message to the Authorization Specialist confirming Botox was delivered
⚖️
Botox Appeal Language & Specialty Pharmacy Directory
▼
Appeal Note Template

"Patient has experienced a 50% reduction of frequency and duration of headaches from baseline since starting Botox treatment."

Accredo NO AMERIGROUP
  • 📞 800-803-2523 · 📠 888-302-1028
  • NPI# 1346208949 · Tax# 113358535
  • prescribers.accredo.com
Alliance Not BC/BS Medical Benefits
  • 📞 855-244-2555
  • 📞 888-347-3416 — can bill Fed BCBS medical benefits
Chartwell Rx
  • 📞 800-366-6020 · 📠 412-920-1869
CVS Caremark Specialty
  • 📞 888-877-0518
  • Does not dispense Botox but can add another SP to auth to dispense under Medical Benefits
Express Scripts
  • 📞 Urgent: 800-417-8164 · 📠 877-251-5836
  • 📞 Appeals: 800-946-3979
Federal Employee Program
  • 📞 877-727-3784 · 📠 877-378-4727
Optum / Optum Rx Not BC/BS Medical
  • 📞 855-427-4682 / 888-397-8129
  • Tax ID# 46273176
Prime Specialty Pharmacy NO BOTOX
  • 📞 888-346-3731 · Federal employees only
Value Specialty Pharmacy
  • 📞 855-265-8008 · 📠 844-812-6227
Walgreens Specialty
  • 📞 888-782-8443 · 📠 888-570-4700
JHH Home Care Group
  • 📞 410-288-8000
  • Courtney: 410-288-7096
  • MPC backup fax: 800-385-4169

🏦 Payer-Specific PA Workflows

Expand the payer below for portal, fax, and phone submission instructions.

🔵
CareFirst BlueCross BlueShield (BCBS)
▼
Submission Portal
Availity
Patient Registration → Auth/Referrals
Provider Services
800-854-4306
PA status, questions, peer-to-peer requests
Fax (if portal unavailable)
410-528-7540
Utilization Management fax line
Submission Steps (Availity)
  • Log in to Availity → Patient Registration → Auth/Referrals
  • Select CareFirst BCBS as the payer
  • Enter member ID, provider NPI, diagnosis codes, and CPT codes
  • Attach most recent office note and supporting clinical documentation
  • Submit and record the authorization reference number
Notes
  • CareFirst may route imaging PA requests through AIM Specialty Health — confirm on the portal
  • Turnaround: 3–5 business days standard; 24–72 hours urgent
  • Questionnaires may be sent electronically via Availity — respond promptly
  • If denied, call 800-854-4306 to request peer-to-peer or initiate appeal
🔴
Aetna
▼
Submission Portal
Availity
Or Aetna's NaviNet portal
Provider Services
888-632-3862
PA status and inquiries
Imaging PA (AIM)
AIM Specialty
Aetna routes imaging PAs through AIM — aimspecialtyhealth.com
Submission Steps
  • For imaging (MRI, CT, PET): submit through AIM Specialty Health at aimspecialtyhealth.com
  • For other services: submit through Availity or Aetna NaviNet
  • Enter member ID, provider NPI, diagnosis and CPT codes
  • Attach most recent office note and supporting documentation
  • Record tracking number upon submission
Notes
  • Aetna Better Health MD (Medicaid) — see Cognitive Assessment tab for CA-specific PA
  • Turnaround: 3–5 business days standard
  • For peer-to-peer: call 888-632-3862 and request Utilization Management
  • Botox for chronic migraine: requires ICD-10 G43.709 or G43.719; documentation of ≥ 15 headache days/month required
🟢
UnitedHealthcare (UHC)
▼
Submission Portal
UHC Provider Portal
uhcprovider.com → Prior Auth & Notification
Provider Services
877-842-3210
PA status and inquiries
Imaging PA (Optum)
Optum / AIM
Imaging PAs route through Optum/AIM portal
Submission Steps
  • Log in to uhcprovider.com → Prior Auth & Notification
  • For imaging: submit through Optum/AIM — portal link is within the UHC provider site
  • Enter member ID, NPI, diagnosis codes, CPT codes, and clinical summary
  • Attach office notes and supporting documentation
  • Submit and save the reference number
Notes
  • UHC has a PA Lookup Tool on their provider portal — use it first to confirm if PA is required before submitting
  • UHC Community Plan (Medicaid) has different requirements than commercial UHC — verify separately
  • Turnaround: 3–5 business days standard; 24 hours for urgent requests
  • Cognitive Assessment (CA): UHC is a non-accepted plan for full CA — MMSE only (see Cognitive Assessment tab)

Notes

Humana
▼
Submission Portal

Provider Services
800-448-6262
PA inquiries and status checks
Imaging PA (Evicore)
Evicore
Imaging PAs routed through Evicore
Submission Steps
  • For imaging: submit through Evicore at evicore.com
  • For other services: submit via Availity or the Humana provider portal
