Notes
  • Humana Medicare Advantage plans have PA requirements where traditional Medicare does not — always verify plan type
  • Turnaround: up to 14 calendar days standard; 3 business days urgent
  • Tricare (military) is administered through Humana Military — see Cognitive Assessment tab for Tricare CA PA instructions
🔷
Medicare Advantage Plans (General)
▼
⚠️Traditional Medicare (fee-for-service) does not require PA for most outpatient neurology services. However, Medicare Advantage plans (AARP/UHC, Humana Gold, Aetna Medicare, CareFirst Medicare Advantage, etc.) impose their own PA requirements. Always identify whether the plan is traditional Medicare or Medicare Advantage before assuming no PA is needed.
How to Identify Medicare Advantage
  • Check the insurance card — Medicare Advantage cards show the plan name (e.g., "Aetna Medicare," "Humana Gold Plus," "AARP MedicareComplete")
  • Verify via Availity eligibility — the plan type field will indicate Medicare Advantage
  • Traditional Medicare cards say "Medicare" and show Part A/Part B coverage only
PA Process for Medicare Advantage
  • Follow the same PA process as the underlying plan sponsor (UHC, Aetna, Humana, CareFirst)
  • Use that plan's portal (Availity, UHC provider portal, etc.) and submit as you would for their commercial plans
  • Call the number on the back of the insurance card for Provider Services if unsure
  • Turnaround: by law, up to 14 calendar days standard; 72 hours urgent
📞
Other / Unknown Payers — How to Proceed
▼
Steps for Unfamiliar Payers
  • Call the Provider Services number on the back of the patient's insurance card
  • Ask: "Is prior authorization required for [CPT code] with diagnosis [ICD-10]?"
  • If yes: ask for the submission method (portal name, fax number, or form)
  • Ask for the UM/Utilization Management fax number if submitting clinical records
  • Request a reference number for the call and document it in the chart
Documentation Required on Every Call
  • Date and time of call
  • Representative name and ID (if provided)
  • Reference number
  • Whether PA is required (Yes/No) and for which codes
  • Submission method and fax/portal information
  • Expected turnaround time

✅ After Approval — Patient Notification & Medication Follow-Up

Once a PA is approved, the work isn't done. Notify the patient, alert their MA, and follow up to confirm the medication is actually obtained.

📲
Step 1 — Notify the Patient (Call or Text)
▼
ℹ️Contact the patient by phone call or text message as soon as the authorization is confirmed. Use the approved message below for medication approvals sent to a pharmacy.
Approved Patient Notification — Text or Call Script:

"Good news! Your insurance has approved your [medication name] prescription. It has been sent to your pharmacy. Please contact your pharmacy regarding pickup. If you have any questions or have difficulty obtaining the medication, please call Mir Neurology & Spine Center."
After Notifying the Patient
  • Notify the patient's assigned Medical Assistant (MA) that the medication has been approved
  • Document the notification in the patient's chart — include date, time, method (call or text), and message sent
  • Every notification must be documented — no exceptions
What to Document
  • Date and time of patient notification
  • Method used: phone call or text message
  • Message content or script used
  • Name of MA notified
  • Authorization number and approved medication name
⏰
Step 2 — 7–14 Day Medication Pickup Follow-Up
▼
If the patient has not obtained or started the medication within 7–14 days — especially for specialty drugs — call the patient directly. Do not assume the patient picked it up.
⚠️This follow-up is required for specialty medications including: Emgality, Ajovy, Aimovig, Leqembi, Kisunla, Ocrevus, and any other high-cost or specialty-dispensed drug. Standard prescriptions should also be followed up if there is reason to believe the patient may have difficulty obtaining them.
📞 Call the Patient
  • Call the patient if they have not picked up or started the medication within 7–14 days of notification
  • Ask whether they have obtained the medication and whether they have started taking it
  • If they have: confirm and document
  • If they have not: ask why and proceed to resolve the issue
🔍 Determine the Barrier
  • Copay / cost concerns — patient cannot afford out-of-pocket cost
  • Pharmacy doesn't stock it — retail pharmacy does not carry specialty drug
  • Specialty pharmacy routing — prescription needs to go to a specialty pharmacy (e.g., Accredo, CVS Specialty, Biologics Inc.)
  • PA expired or not transmitted — confirm auth is still active and prescription was sent
  • Patient forgot or lost the information — remind and re-send details
✅ Resolve the Issue
  • Copay assistance — connect patient to manufacturer copay card programs or patient assistance programs (PAP)
  • Specialty pharmacy — transfer the prescription to the correct specialty pharmacy and provide the patient with the pharmacy's contact info
  • Pharmacy stock — assist with locating a pharmacy that carries the medication
  • Manufacturer assistance — enroll patient in manufacturer free drug or assistance program if eligible
  • Document every step taken to resolve the barrier and the final outcome
💉
Specialty Drug Prescription Notes — Emgality (Galcanezumab) Loading Dose Required
▼
Emgality requires a loading dose on the first month — this is NOT the same as Aimovig, which uses a single injection. Writing a single-injection prescription for an Emgality patient's first month is an error. Two separate prescriptions must be written.
Emgality vs. Aimovig — Do Not Confuse
  • Emgality (galcanezumab) — First dose is a loading dose of 240 mg, given as two 120 mg injections at the same visit. Subsequent doses are 120 mg monthly (single injection)
  • Aimovig (erenumab) — Single injection every month; no loading dose required
  • These are different medications with different dosing protocols — do not assume one works like the other
Two Separate Prescriptions Are Required
  • Prescription #1 — Loading Dose: Emgality 120 mg/mL, Qty: 2 syringes, for the first-month loading dose (240 mg total)
  • Prescription #2 — Monthly Maintenance: Emgality 120 mg/mL, Qty: 1 syringe per month, for all subsequent months
  • When the PA approval letter is received, ensure the authorized dose matches the prescription written — the PA must cover both the loading and maintenance doses if applicable
  • Specialty pharmacies (Accredo, Biologics Inc., etc.) may require separate authorizations for the loading dose quantity — verify this when routing the prescriptions
⚠️If a patient has already received the loading dose and is returning for monthly maintenance, do not re-prescribe the 2-syringe loading dose. Confirm the patient's history before sending the prescription. Document which dose cycle the patient is currently on.
✅
Post-Approval Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Authorization number, approved medication/service, and validity dates recorded in patient chart
  • ✓
    Patient notified by phone call or text — approved message used
  • ✓
    Notification documented in chart — date, time, method, and message content recorded
  • ✓
    Patient's assigned MA notified that the medication has been approved
  • ✓
    Follow-up reminder set for 7–14 days to confirm patient obtained the medication
  • ✓
    At 7–14 day follow-up: patient called — medication pickup status confirmed
  • ✓
    If not obtained: barrier identified and resolution steps taken (copay assistance, specialty pharmacy, manufacturer program, etc.)
  • ✓
    All follow-up actions documented in patient chart — barrier, steps taken, and final outcome

❌ PA Denial & Appeal Process

What to do when a prior authorization is denied — appeals, peer-to-peer reviews, and escalation paths.

❌
Denial Response Workflow
▼
Never accept a denial without reviewing it. Most PA denials are overturned on first-level appeal or peer-to-peer review when documentation is complete and the correct appeal path is followed.
Step 1 — Understand the Denial Reason
  • Read the denial letter or portal notice in full
  • Identify the specific denial reason code and description
  • Common reasons: not medically necessary, missing documentation, non-covered service, incorrect code, step therapy required
  • Determine the appeal deadline — usually 30–60 days from denial date
Step 2 — Choose the Appeal Path
  • Missing/insufficient documentation → Resubmit with complete records before formal appeal
  • Medical necessity denial → First-level appeal with additional clinical justification, or request peer-to-peer
  • Incorrect code → Correct and resubmit (not an appeal — this is a new submission)
  • Step therapy required → Document that step therapy was attempted or contraindicated
Step 3 — Submit the Appeal
  • Write a formal appeal letter clearly stating why the denial was incorrect
  • Reference the payer's own clinical criteria and show how the patient meets them
  • Attach all supporting documentation: office notes, imaging reports, labs, specialist letters, clinical guidelines
  • Submit via the payer's appeals process (portal, fax, or mail per denial letter)
  • Record the appeal submission date and confirmation number
📞
Peer-to-Peer Review — How to Request
▼
ℹ️A peer-to-peer is a direct call between our provider (Dr. Mir or another treating physician) and the payer's medical reviewer. It is often the fastest and most effective way to overturn a medical necessity denial. Request it immediately after a denial — most payers allow it within 5–14 days of the denial.
How to Request
  • Call the payer's Provider Services line and say: "I need to request a peer-to-peer review for a prior authorization denial."
  • Provide: authorization number, patient member ID, denial date, and CPT code
  • Request to speak with or schedule a callback from the medical reviewer (not just a rep)
  • Get the callback number and scheduled time — document it in the patient chart
  • Brief the treating provider before the call: denial reason, key clinical points, the evidence supporting medical necessity
What to Have Ready for the Call
  • Patient name, member ID, date of birth
  • Authorization number and denial reference
  • Clinical summary: diagnosis, symptoms, prior treatments tried, why this service is necessary
  • Relevant clinical guidelines or literature supporting the request
  • Most recent office note and test results
  • After the call: document the outcome, reviewer name, and any new reference number
✅
PA Appeal Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Denial letter reviewed in full — denial reason and denial code identified
  • ✓
    Appeal deadline confirmed — appeal will be submitted before the deadline
  • ✓
    Denial reason categorized: missing docs / medical necessity / incorrect code / step therapy
  • ✓
    Appeal letter written — clearly states why denial is incorrect and references payer criteria
  • ✓
    All supporting documentation attached: office notes, imaging, labs, treatment history
  • ✓
    Appeal submitted via payer's designated method — submission confirmation documented in chart
  • ✓
    If peer-to-peer requested: provider briefed and callback scheduled — outcome documented after call
  • ✓
    Patient notified of denial and appeal status — appointment not scheduled until PA is confirmed

📅 PA Expiration Tracking & Re-Authorization

Proactive management of PA expiration dates — especially for ongoing specialty and disease-modifying medications.

📅
Why PA Expiration Tracking Matters Required
▼
A prior authorization that expires is not just a paperwork problem — it means the patient cannot receive their medication or service until a new PA is approved. For MS disease-modifying therapies (Ocrevus, Tysabri, Briumvi, etc.) and other specialty drugs, a lapse can delay treatment for weeks and put the patient at clinical risk.
What to Track
  • Authorization number
  • Approval date and expiration date
  • Approved medication or service and dosage
  • Payer / insurance plan
  • Number of approved doses or units (for infusions or specialty injectables)
  • Re-authorization lead time required by payer (often 2–4 weeks or longer)
Medications Requiring Proactive Tracking
  • MS disease-modifying therapies: Ocrevus, Tysabri (natalizumab), Briumvi, Kesimpta, Gilenya, Aubagio, Tecfidera, Mayzent
  • CGRP migraine treatments: Emgality, Ajovy, Aimovig, Vyepti
  • Alzheimer's infusions: Leqembi, Kisunla
  • Any other specialty drug or high-cost injectable
🔄
Re-Authorization Workflow
▼
⚠️Do not wait until the PA has expired to start re-authorization. By that point, there may already be a treatment gap. Begin the re-authorization process well in advance of the expiration date.
Step 1 — Know When PA Expires
  • Record the expiration date when the PA is first approved
  • Set a reminder at least 4–6 weeks before expiration to begin re-authorization
  • For payers with slow turnaround (e.g., Medicare Advantage, Humana), start earlier — up to 8 weeks in advance
Step 2 — Begin Re-Authorization Early
  • Submit the re-authorization request with all required updated clinical documentation
  • Include any updated labs (e.g., CBC for MS patients), office visit notes, and treatment history
  • Confirm the payer's re-authorization form requirements — some require a new form, others allow a renewal request
Step 3 — Confirm & Update
  • Follow up with the payer if the decision is not received within 5–7 business days
  • Once approved: record new authorization number and updated expiration date in the chart immediately
  • Notify the patient and any relevant pharmacy or infusion center of the renewed authorization
  • If denied: initiate the appeal process immediately — do not wait
ℹ️For MS disease-modifying infusions like Ocrevus, there is typically a scheduled infusion date. If the PA expires before the infusion date and re-authorization has not been secured, the infusion cannot take place. Confirm PA validity before every scheduled infusion appointment.
💊
Schedule II Prescription Limits & Prior Authorization by Payer Maryland Reference
▼

Last updated: July 2026  ·  Verify against each payer's live formulary at least annually — plan edits change with the plan year.

Several Maryland payers — especially Medicare Advantage plans — will only cover a short initial fill of a Schedule II (CII) prescription (typically a 7-day supply) and require prior authorization or a coverage determination for anything beyond that. Use this section to set patient expectations and start the PA early.

⚠️
This mostly affects OPIOIDS, not stimulants. The 7-day limits below target opioid prescriptions for acute pain in opioid-naïve patients. Schedule II stimulants neurology commonly prescribes (methylphenidate, amphetamine salts, dextroamphetamine, lisdexamfetamine — for ADHD/narcolepsy) are generally not subject to the 7-day acute-pain edit, though they may carry their own quantity limits or PA on specific formularies. Always confirm on the member's active formulary.
How the Limits Stack — Three Layers (check all three)
Layer 1 — Federal (Medicare)

CMS requires every Medicare Advantage–PD and Part D plan to run the same opioid point-of-sale safety edits. This is why CareFirst, UnitedHealthcare, Aetna, and Humana MA plans all behave identically for opioids.

Layer 2 — Maryland State Law

Applies to the prescriber regardless of payer — lowest effective dose, quantity no greater than needed for the expected duration of pain.

Layer 3 — Plan / Formulary Edits

Commercial and Medicaid plans layer on their own quantity limits and PA criteria (MME thresholds, early-refill blocks, etc.).

Layer 1 — Federal: CMS Medicare Part D Opioid Safety Edits (CY 2026)

Applies to ALL Medicare Advantage (MA-PD) and standalone Part D plans in Maryland — CareFirst, UnitedHealthcare, Aetna, Humana, Wellpoint, Kaiser, and any other MA plan a patient carries.

Key Edits
  • 7-day hard edit: Initial opioid fill for acute pain in an opioid-naïve patient is limited to 7-day supply. More than 7 days rejects at the pharmacy — requires coverage determination (PA)
  • 90 MME/day care-coordination edit: Soft edit prompting pharmacist review when cumulative daily morphine milligram equivalents reach 90
  • 200 MME/day hard edit: Some plans block fills at/above 200 MME/day pending PA
Exemptions (edit does NOT apply)
  • Hospice care
  • Cancer-related pain
  • Palliative / end-of-life care
  • Long-term-care residents (per plan)
📝Document the exemption in the chart — it removes the edit at the pharmacy.
Layer 2 — Maryland State Law (All Payers)
Md. Health Occupations §1-223 (HB 1432, 2017)

When treating pain, the prescriber must order the lowest effective dose of an opioid and a quantity no greater than needed for the expected duration of the pain.

Exemptions from the prescribing limit: substance-use-disorder treatment, cancer pain, hospice/palliative/end-of-life care, and chronic pain. Violations are grounds for Board discipline.

Layer 3 — Payer-Specific Rules: Medicare Advantage Plans

All MA plans follow the federal 7-day opioid-naïve edit above. Plan-specific details below.

PlanSchedule II LimitPrior Authorization
CareFirst BCBS Medicare AdvantageOpioid-naïve fill >7-day supply rejects; pharmacist may dispense 7 daysPA/coverage determination for >7 days; also for >90 MME or >14 days. Post-limit approvals up to 200 MME/day and 30-day supply
UnitedHealthcare Medicare Advantage (2026)Opioid limited to 7-day supply if no recent opioid history; cumulative MME limit appliesPrescriber may request coverage of additional quantity
Aetna / Humana / Other MA-PD PlansSame CMS 7-day opioid-naïve edit (federal requirement)Coverage determination for quantities beyond the edit
Layer 3 — Payer-Specific Rules: Commercial Plans
PlanSchedule II LimitPrior Authorization
CareFirst BCBS (Commercial)Opioid-naïve fill >7-day supply rejects (7-day allowed)PA for additional quantity; also for >90 MME or >14 days. Post-limit up to 200 MME/day, 30-day supply

Commercial plans vary by employer group and formulary — verify each member's benefit. CII stimulant quantity limits, when present, are formulary-specific.

Layer 3 — Maryland Medicaid HealthChoice MCOs
Statewide Medicaid Rule (FFS and All MCOs)
  • PA required for opioid-naïve patient receiving >7 days or >50 MME/day
  • PA required for any opioid regimen exceeding 90 MME/day
  • Immediate-release opioids need PA beyond 7 days (acute pain) or 30 days (chronic pain)
  • Early refills of controlled medications are generally not authorized
MCOSchedule II LimitPrior Authorization / Notes
Aetna Better Health of MarylandState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
CareFirst Community Health Plan MDIR opioid >7 days (acute pain) rejectsPA approves up to 1-month supply
Jai Medical SystemsState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Kaiser PermanenteState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Maryland Physicians CareState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
MedStar Family ChoiceState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editNo early refills of controlled meds (since 1/1/2019); exceptions: cancer, sickle cell, hospice/palliative
Priority PartnersState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
UnitedHealthcare Community PlanState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Wellpoint MarylandState opioid-naïve 7-day / 50 MME editPA beyond limits; follows MD Medicaid PDL
Clinical & Front-Desk Workflow
  • At scheduling or check-in, identify the patient's plan type (Medicare Advantage, commercial, or Medicaid MCO)
  • For any opioid-naïve patient starting a CII opioid for acute pain, expect a 7-day fill — start the PA/coverage determination at the same visit if a longer supply is clinically needed
  • For CII stimulants (ADHD/narcolepsy), the 7-day acute-pain edit generally does not apply — but check the member's formulary for quantity limits or step/PA requirements
  • Document the exemption (cancer, hospice, palliative, chronic pain, SUD) when one applies — it removes the edit at the pharmacy
  • Keep this list current — verify each payer's rule against its live formulary at least annually; plan edits change with the plan year
Sources
  • Federal / CMS: CY 2026 Opioid Safety Edit Submission Instructions; AMCP summary
  • Maryland law: Md. Health Occupations §1-223; HB 1432 (2017)
  • CareFirst: Opioids IR MME Limit & Post-Limit PA criteria; CareFirst Medicare drug-management programs; CareFirst CHPMD pharmaceutical management
  • UnitedHealthcare: 2026 Complete Drug List (Formulary), Medicare Advantage
  • Maryland Medicaid: Pharmacy Program Quantity Limits; MedStar Family Choice opioid PA requirements; HealthChoice MCO list
⚠️
PA Operational Concerns & Quality Standards Quality Standard
▼
Issues IdentifiedDelays in obtaining prior authorizations for anticonvulsant medications resulted in a patient going without essential medication for an extended period. Formulary coverage was not identified promptly, leading to repeated patient inquiries and delayed treatment. Manufacturer sample programs are underutilized, and plan-specific coverage restrictions are not consistently tracked.
1 · Anticonvulsant & Seizure Medications — Treat as Urgent
🚫Delays in anticonvulsant PA put patients at risk of breakthrough seizures. These are not routine PAs — they must be treated as urgent from the moment the prescription is written.
  • Prior authorization requests for anticonvulsant and other high-priority neurological medications must be submitted immediately — the same day the order is placed
  • Mark the request as urgent whenever the payer's process allows it; document the reason (active seizure disorder, risk of breakthrough seizure)
  • Follow up daily with the payer until a determination is received — do not wait for the standard 5–7 business day follow-up cycle
  • Notify the provider immediately if a determination is delayed beyond 24–48 hours so clinical alternatives (samples, bridge supply, peer-to-peer) can be initiated
  • Document every follow-up contact with date, time, representative name or reference number, and outcome
2 · Formulary Verification & Patient Communication
⚠️Discovering a formulary exclusion after a prescription is written wastes time and delays treatment. Verify formulary status before or at the time of prescribing — not after a pharmacy rejection.
  • Verify the patient's formulary status for any new or high-cost medication promptly — use the Formulary & PA Tool (🔍 tab) and the plan's live formulary
  • If a medication is not covered or is subject to restrictions, notify the provider immediately so alternatives, step therapy, or an appeal can be initiated without delay
  • Keep patients informed at every stage — acknowledge their inquiry, provide a status update, and give an expected resolution timeframe
  • Do not leave patients without a response while a PA or formulary issue is being resolved — proactive communication prevents repeat calls and patient frustration
  • Document all patient communications related to PA and formulary status in the chart
3 · Manufacturer Sample Programs — Bridge While PA Pending
  • Pharmaceutical manufacturer sample programs must be fully utilized to prevent therapy interruptions while a prior authorization or appeal is pending
  • As soon as a PA is submitted and approval is not immediate, check whether the manufacturer offers samples, a bridge program, or a patient assistance enrollment
  • Patients should not go without medication while waiting for insurance approval when a bridge option is available
Designated Sample Manager — Required Role
  • A designated staff member must be responsible for the sample program — ordering, inventory, expiration tracking, provider distribution, and patient dispensing logs
  • A backup must be trained so sample management never depends on a single person
  • All samples dispensed to patients must be documented in the patient's chart
4 · Plan-Specific Restrictions — Maintain a Reference List
ℹ️Some insurance plans — particularly certain Medicare Advantage plans — impose restrictions not found in standard Part D rules. For example, plans may allow only a limited initial supply of a medication before requiring PA for any additional quantity. These restrictions vary by plan and change annually.
  • Maintain an up-to-date reference list of known plan-specific restrictions for the payers most commonly seen in the practice
  • Review the list at least annually, or whenever a plan's coverage policy changes
  • When a patient's plan has a known restriction, anticipate the PA requirement and initiate it proactively — before the pharmacy rejects the fill
  • Use the Formulary & PA Tool (🔍 tab) and the Schedule II Prescription Limits reference above as starting points; supplement with plan-specific formulary lookups for other medication classes
  • When an unexpected restriction is encountered, document it in the reference list so the entire team benefits from the discovery
🎓
Prior Authorization Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Prior Authorization Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

🫀 Care Management

Chronic Care Management (CCM), Principal Care Management (PCM), Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM), and Behavioral Health Integration (BHI) — eligibility, enrollment, billing codes, documentation requirements, and monthly workflows.

CCMPCMRPMBHI99490994249945799484ConsentCare PlanTime TrackingMonthly Billing
Why These Programs Matter

Improving Outcomes for Our Most Vulnerable Patients

Care management programs — CCM, PCM, RPM, and BHI — exist to provide structured, ongoing support to patients who have complex or chronic health conditions. Rather than waiting for a crisis, these programs allow our team to actively check in, monitor health trends, coordinate care, and adjust treatment plans between office visits.

🏥
Fewer Hospital Visits
Early identification of worsening symptoms reduces ER trips and hospitalizations
💊
Better Medication Adherence
Regular contact helps patients stay on track with medications and care plans
🤝
Coordinated Care
Our team communicates across specialists so nothing falls through the cracks
📡
Real-Time Monitoring (RPM)
Devices at home catch dangerous trends in blood pressure, glucose, and more before they escalate
🧠
Mental Health Support (BHI)
Behavioral health conditions are treated alongside physical health — whole-person care
📋
Personalized Care Plans
Each patient gets a structured plan with measurable goals tailored to their conditions

These programs are especially impactful for elderly and chronically ill patients who may struggle to navigate the healthcare system on their own. Enrolling eligible patients is not just a billing function — it is a meaningful extension of the care we provide.

💚 No Out-of-Pocket Cost to the Patient

Mir Neurology & Spine Center will not charge patients for any patient responsibility that arises from care management services. We accept whatever the patient's insurance covers as payment in full.

When enrolling patients in CCM, PCM, RPM, or BHI, staff should communicate this clearly: "There is no cost to you — we accept what your insurance pays and will not bill you for any remaining balance for this program." This removes a common barrier to enrollment and ensures patients are not deterred by concerns about cost-sharing.

📋 Chronic Care Management (CCM)

For patients with two or more chronic conditions expected to last at least 12 months or until death.

✅
CCM Eligibility & Enrollment
▼
Eligibility Requirements
  • Medicare Part B beneficiary
  • Two or more chronic conditions expected to last ≥ 12 months or until death
  • Conditions place the patient at significant risk of death, acute exacerbation, or functional decline
  • Examples: diabetes, hypertension, COPD, heart failure, Alzheimer's, depression, epilepsy, chronic pain
Enrollment Requirements
  • Written or verbal consent required — must be documented in the chart before the first billable month
  • Consent must explain the program, and the right to revoke at any time — inform the patient that no out-of-pocket cost will be charged; the practice accepts whatever insurance covers
  • Only one provider may bill CCM per patient per month — confirm no other practice is already billing CCM
  • Initiating visit: face-to-face visit is required for new patients or patients not seen in the past year before CCM can begin
⚠️CCM cannot be billed the same month as Transitional Care Management (TCM — 99495/99496) or the same month the initiating visit code is billed under some circumstances. Confirm with billing before submitting.
💵
CCM Billing Codes & Time Rules
▼
All CCM codes are billed per calendar month. Time must be met within the calendar month — it does not roll over.
99490 — CCM Base (20 min)
  • First 20 minutes of clinical staff time per calendar month
  • Billed once per month per patient
  • Requires: structured care plan, 24/7 access, care coordination
99439 — CCM Add-On (each add'l 20 min)
  • Each additional 20-minute increment beyond the first 20
  • Can be billed up to 2 units per month (i.e., 40+20+20 = 60 min total with base)
  • Append to 99490; requires the base code to be billed first
Complex CCM — 99487 & 99489
  • 99487 — Complex CCM, first 60 min (physician or clinical staff)
  • 99489 — Complex CCM add-on, each additional 30 min
  • Requires moderate or high complexity medical decision-making
  • Cannot bill 99490 and 99487 the same month
What Counts as CCM Time
✅ Billable Activities
  • Phone calls with patient or caregiver about their chronic conditions
  • Care coordination with other providers (referrals, labs, specialist follow-up)
  • Medication management and reconciliation
  • Care plan review and updates
  • Patient education related to chronic conditions
  • Review of test results and updating care plan accordingly
❌ Not Billable as CCM Time
  • Scheduling appointments (not directly related to care management)
  • Administrative tasks (insurance verification, check-in)
  • Time spent during a face-to-face E&M visit (billed separately)
  • Time already counted toward another care management code (PCM, BHI, TCM) in the same month
📄
CCM Documentation SOP
▼
Every CCM encounter must be documented before the claim is submitted. Undocumented time cannot be billed.
Required Documentation Elements
  • Date and duration of each CCM encounter (to the minute)
  • Name of the staff member who performed the activity
  • Nature of the activity (phone call, care coordination, care plan update, etc.)
  • Patient response / outcome of the encounter
  • Total accumulated time for the month — must meet the minimum threshold before billing
  • Signed and dated care plan on file (reviewed or updated at least annually)
Care Plan Requirements
  • List of all chronic conditions being managed
  • Measurable treatment goals for each condition
  • Medications list (current, with dosages)
  • Planned interventions and community resources
  • Coordination of care with other providers
  • Patient/caregiver education plan
  • Copy provided to patient — document that it was given
✅
CCM Monthly Billing Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Patient has signed (or verbally consented, documented) CCM enrollment — consent on file
  • ✓
    Structured care plan exists, is current, and has been provided to the patient
  • ✓
    Minimum 20 minutes of clinical staff time documented for the calendar month (for 99490)
  • ✓
    All CCM encounters logged with date, time, duration, staff name, and activity description
  • ✓
    Add-on units (99439) documented with additional 20-min increments if billing beyond base 20 min
  • ✓
    Verified no other provider is billing CCM for this patient this month
  • ✓
    Confirmed CCM is not being billed same month as TCM (99495/99496)
  • ✓
    Claim submitted with correct code(s), diagnosis codes, and rendering provider NPI
📵
When You Cannot Reach the Patient — Alternative Contact Protocol Important
▼
Repeated unanswered phone calls are not sufficient documentation of effort. If CCM or PCM staff cannot reach a patient by phone after multiple attempts, escalate to alternative contact methods before the month ends. Abandoning outreach without exhausting all options may result in lost billing and — more importantly — a patient falling through the cracks.
⚠️Document every attempt — date, time, method, and outcome. If a patient is consistently unreachable, this pattern must be flagged and escalated. Do not silently drop a patient from CCM/PCM without a documented resolution.
📞 Step 1 — Phone Attempts
  • Call the patient's primary phone number at different times of day (morning and afternoon)
  • Leave a voicemail if no answer — keep the message brief and professional
  • Try at least 2–3 separate call attempts on different days before escalating
  • Document each call: date, time, outcome (no answer / voicemail left / busy / disconnected)
📬 Step 2 — Send a Postcard
  • If phone attempts are not successful, send the patient a postcard to their address on file
  • The postcard should ask the patient to call the office to reconnect with their care team — keep it non-clinical and non-identifying to comply with HIPAA
  • Document that a postcard was sent, including the date and mailing address used
  • If the address is returned as undeliverable, update the record and flag for provider review
🤝 Step 3 — Contact PCP and/or Emergency Contact
  • Contact the patient's PCP — ask whether the patient is following up with them and if they have current contact information or can relay a message
  • Contact the patient's emergency contact (on file in the chart) — ask them to let the patient know the care team is trying to reach them; do not share clinical details without authorization
  • Document all contacts made — who was contacted, when, and what the outcome was
  • If the patient is found to be deceased, hospitalized, or otherwise unable to continue: document and update enrollment status accordingly

🎯 Principal Care Management (PCM)

For patients with a single high-risk chronic condition requiring substantial care management.

✅
PCM Eligibility & Enrollment
▼
Eligibility Requirements
  • Medicare Part B beneficiary
  • One complex chronic condition expected to last ≥ 3 months
  • Condition is of sufficient complexity to require development, monitoring, or revision of a disease-specific care plan
  • Requires frequent adjustments of the treatment plan, drug regimen, or referrals to specialists
  • Examples: advanced Parkinson's, treatment-resistant epilepsy, MS, ALS, uncontrolled diabetes with complications
Enrollment & Consent
  • Patient consent required — verbal or written, documented in the chart
  • Inform the patient that no out-of-pocket cost will be charged — the practice accepts whatever insurance pays as payment in full
  • Only one provider can bill PCM per patient per month for the same condition
  • PCM and CCM cannot be billed together in the same calendar month
  • Disease-specific care plan must be documented before billing begins
💵
PCM Billing Codes & Time Rules
▼
PCM codes are billed per calendar month. Time is cumulative across all qualifying encounters within the month.
99424 — PCM Base (30 min, Physician/QHP)
  • First 30 minutes per calendar month
  • Must be performed by a physician or other qualified health professional (QHP)
  • Billed once per month per patient
99425 — PCM Add-On (each add'l 30 min, Physician/QHP)
  • Each additional 30-minute increment beyond the first 30
  • Must also be performed by a physician or QHP
  • Append to 99424; no stated maximum add-on units per CMS guidance
99426 — PCM Base (30 min, Clinical Staff)
  • First 30 minutes of clinical staff time per calendar month
  • Performed by clinical staff under physician supervision
  • Billed when clinical staff (not physician) performs the care management activities
99427 — PCM Add-On (each add'l 30 min, Clinical Staff)
  • Each additional 30-minute increment of clinical staff time
  • Append to 99426
⚠️Do not mix physician and clinical staff codes in the same month. Use either the 99424/99425 set (physician) or 99426/99427 set (clinical staff), not both.
📄
PCM Documentation SOP
▼
Required Documentation
  • Disease-specific care plan on file — focused on the single qualifying condition
  • Date, duration, and staff/provider name for each PCM encounter
  • Description of the activity performed
  • Total monthly time documented before claim submission
  • Evidence of care coordination with other providers when applicable
  • Medication management notes specific to the condition
Disease-Specific Care Plan Elements
  • Identification of the single qualifying chronic condition
  • Condition-specific measurable goals
  • Expected outcomes and treatment milestones
  • Medication list relevant to the condition (with dosages)
  • Planned interventions and specialist coordination
  • Patient/caregiver education related to the condition
  • Copy provided to patient — documented in chart
✅
PCM Monthly Billing Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Patient consent documented — verbal or written, on file in chart
  • ✓
    Disease-specific care plan on file and current for the qualifying condition
  • ✓
    Minimum 30 minutes of qualifying time documented for the calendar month
  • ✓
    All PCM encounters logged with date, duration, provider/staff name, and activity type
  • ✓
    Correct code set used — either 99424/99425 (physician) or 99426/99427 (clinical staff), not mixed
  • ✓
    Confirmed PCM and CCM are not both billed this calendar month for this patient
  • ✓
    Claim submitted with correct diagnosis codes and rendering provider NPI

📡 Remote Patient Monitoring (RPM)

Continuous physiologic data collection from devices in the patient's home — blood pressure, glucose, weight, pulse oximetry.

✅
RPM Eligibility & Enrollment
▼
Eligibility Requirements
  • Medicare Part B beneficiary with an established relationship with the billing provider
  • Has an acute or chronic condition that warrants monitoring
  • Able to use a monitoring device (or caregiver can assist)
  • Common qualifying conditions: hypertension, CHF, diabetes, COPD, obesity
Enrollment & Device Setup
  • Patient consent required — written preferred, documented in chart
  • Billing provider must supply, or arrange supply of, the monitoring device
  • Device must be a medical-grade, FDA-cleared device — consumer-grade wearables do not qualify
  • Patient education on device use must be provided and documented (covered under 99453)
  • Device must transmit data automatically or via patient upload — at least 16 days of data required per 30-day period to bill monthly monitoring codes
ℹ️RPM can be billed alongside CCM or PCM in the same month, as long as time is not double-counted. Confirm with billing when stacking programs.
💵
RPM Billing Codes & Thresholds
▼
99453 — Device Setup
  • Initial device setup and patient education on use
  • Billed once per episode of care (not monthly)
  • Must document education provided and device successfully set up
99454 — Device Supply (Monthly)
  • Supply of the monitoring device per 30-day period
  • Requires ≥ 16 days of data transmission in the 30-day period
  • Billed once per 30 days — not per calendar month
99457 — RPM Treatment Management (20 min)
  • First 20 minutes of interactive communication with patient/caregiver per calendar month
  • Must include real-time interactive communication (phone call or video) — not just passive data review
  • Requires ≥ 16 days of data in the month
99458 — RPM Add-On (each add'l 20 min)
  • Each additional 20-minute increment beyond the first 20 minutes
  • Append to 99457; can be billed multiple units
  • All time must include qualified interactive communication
16-Day Data Threshold — Key Rule
⚠ Critical: If the patient transmits fewer than 16 days of data in the 30-day period, 99454, 99457, and 99458 cannot be billed for that period. Only 99453 (setup, one-time) is unaffected by this threshold.
📄
RPM Documentation SOP
▼
Required Documentation
  • Patient consent for RPM on file
  • Device type and FDA clearance documentation
  • Date of device setup and patient education (99453)
  • Record of data transmission days per 30-day period — confirm ≥ 16 days before billing 99454/99457
  • Date, duration, and nature of each interactive communication encounter
  • Clinical response to monitored data — any care plan changes made
  • Name of staff/provider who reviewed data and communicated with patient
Monthly Data Review Notes
  • Document specific readings reviewed (e.g., "BP readings ranged 128/80 – 155/92 over 18 days")
  • Note any out-of-range values and the clinical action taken
  • Document whether the patient was contacted due to abnormal readings
  • Medication adjustments or referrals triggered by RPM data must be noted
✅
RPM Monthly Billing Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 7
  • ✓
    Patient consent for RPM documented and on file
  • ✓
    Medical-grade FDA-cleared device confirmed — device type documented in chart
  • ✓
    99453 billed (one-time) — device setup and education completed and documented
  • ✓
    ≥ 16 days of data transmission confirmed for this 30-day period before billing 99454/99457
  • ✓
    99457: Minimum 20 minutes of interactive communication documented for the calendar month
  • ✓
    All encounters logged: date, duration, staff/provider, activity, and clinical response to data
  • ✓
    Claim submitted with correct codes, diagnosis codes, and rendering provider NPI

🧠 Behavioral Health Integration (BHI)

Integration of behavioral health services into the primary care or specialty setting for patients with mental health or substance use conditions.

✅
BHI Eligibility & Enrollment
▼
Eligibility Requirements
  • Medicare Part B beneficiary
  • Diagnosed with a behavioral health condition — depression, anxiety, PTSD, bipolar disorder, substance use disorder, OCD, and others
  • Condition is being managed in conjunction with a primary care or specialty provider at this practice
  • Patient must be willing to engage with care management and behavioral health support
Enrollment & Consent
  • Patient consent required — document in chart before first billable month
  • Explain what BHI entails (regular check-ins, care coordination with behavioral health)
  • Behavioral health care plan must be established before billing begins
  • BHI can be billed alongside CCM or RPM in the same month (time must not be double-counted)
  • Only one provider may bill BHI per patient per month
💵
BHI Billing Codes & Time Rules
▼
All BHI codes are billed per calendar month. There are two tracks: General BHI (99484) and Collaborative Care Model (CoCM — 99492/99493/99494).
General BHI — 99484
99484 — General BHI (20 min clinical staff)
  • 20 minutes or more of clinical staff time per calendar month
  • Under direction of a physician or other QHP
  • Activities: assessment, care plan development, patient communication, care coordination
  • Does not require a psychiatric consultant — can be billed by PCP or specialty practice
  • Billed once per month per patient
Collaborative Care Model (CoCM) — Higher Complexity BHI
99492 — CoCM Initial Month (70 min)
  • First calendar month of CoCM services
  • First 70 minutes of billing provider and/or clinical staff time
  • Requires: care manager, treating provider, and psychiatric consultant
99493 — CoCM Subsequent Month (60 min)
  • Subsequent calendar months of CoCM
  • First 60 minutes of team time per month
  • Same team structure required (care manager + billing provider + psychiatrist)
99494 — CoCM Add-On (each add'l 30 min)
  • Each additional 30-minute increment beyond 70 (initial) or 60 (subsequent) minutes
  • Append to 99492 or 99493
⚠️CoCM (99492/99493) requires a dedicated psychiatric consultant arrangement. Do not bill CoCM codes without confirming the practice has this arrangement in place. General BHI (99484) does not have this requirement.
📄
BHI Documentation SOP
▼
Required Documentation
  • Patient consent for BHI enrollment — dated and on file
  • Behavioral health care plan — diagnosis, goals, planned interventions
  • Date, duration, and staff/provider name for each BHI encounter
  • Description of the activity (assessment, check-in call, care coordination, care plan update)
  • Patient response and progress toward behavioral health goals
  • Total monthly time documented before claim submission
Behavioral Health Care Plan Elements
  • Behavioral health diagnosis (ICD-10 code)
  • Measurable treatment goals (e.g., "PHQ-9 score improvement by 4 points within 3 months")
  • Psychiatric/behavioral health interventions planned
  • Referrals to behavioral health specialists if applicable
  • Medication management notes related to behavioral health conditions
  • Coordination with psychiatrist (required for CoCM) — document consultations
  • Copy of care plan provided to patient — documented
✅
BHI Monthly Billing Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 8
  • ✓
    Patient consent for BHI documented and on file
  • ✓
    Behavioral health care plan established, current, and accessible in the chart
  • ✓
    Minimum time threshold met for chosen code: 20 min (99484) or 70/60 min (CoCM 99492/99493)
  • ✓
    All BHI encounters logged with date, duration, staff/provider, and activity description
  • ✓
    If billing CoCM (99492/99493): psychiatric consultant arrangement confirmed and consultation documented
  • ✓
    Time not double-counted with CCM, PCM, or RPM if multiple programs billed this month
  • ✓
    Only one provider billing BHI for this patient this month — confirmed
  • ✓
    Claim submitted with correct BHI code(s), behavioral health diagnosis codes, and rendering provider NPI

📊 Program Comparison Quick Reference

Side-by-side summary of all four care management programs.

📊
CCM / PCM / RPM / BHI — At a Glance
▼
Program Qualifying Condition Minimum Time / Month Base Code Add-On Code Can Stack With
CCM 2+ chronic conditions 20 min (clinical staff) 99490 99439 (×2 max) RPM, BHI
PCM 1 complex chronic condition 30 min (physician or staff) 99424 or 99426 99425 or 99427 RPM, BHI (not CCM)
RPM Acute or chronic condition needing monitoring 20 min interactive + ≥16 data days 99457 99458 CCM, PCM, BHI
BHI Behavioral health diagnosis 20 min (99484) or 70 min (99492) 99484 or 99492/99493 99494 CCM, RPM (time not double-counted)
ℹ️Time stacking rule: When billing multiple programs in the same month (e.g., CCM + BHI), each minute of staff time can only count toward one program. Do not count the same interaction toward both CCM and BHI time in the same month.

🤝 Care Coordination Standards

Policies, responsibilities, and quality standards for coordinating care across the patient's full healthcare team.

Key Principle

Chronic Care Management is about coordinating the patient's entire healthcare journey — not just today's visit. Every provider involved in the patient's care should have the information they need, and every outstanding issue should be tracked until resolved. The goal is seamless communication, continuity of care, and preventing patients from falling through the cracks.

📌
Care Coordination Policies Required
▼
  • Every CCM patient must have a complete and up-to-date Care Team. The following should be listed when applicable: Primary Care Provider (PCP), Cardiology, Nephrology, Pain Management, Rheumatology, Endocrinology, Psychiatry/Psychology, and all other regularly treating specialists.
  • Ask at check-in whether the patient has started seeing any new providers since their last visit. If yes, update the Care Team in the chart immediately.
  • Send office notes and relevant lab/imaging results to the PCP and consulting specialists when: (a) the provider requests it, or (b) there has been a significant change in the patient's condition or treatment plan.
  • New patients: Send the consultation note to both the referring provider and the PCP. Follow-up patients: Notify the PCP when there is a significant change in diagnosis or management.
  • Track all referrals, specialist consultations, lab results, imaging studies, and medication changes until they are completed and documented. Do not assume an ordered item was acted on without verification.
  • Review charts of patients who cancel or no-show to ensure there are no unresolved abnormal results or outstanding clinical issues that need to be addressed before the next contact.
  • Infusion medications (e.g., Ocrevus, Tysabri, Briumvi) must be documented in the patient's active medication list — not just in visit notes.
  • Medication reconciliation must be completed at every visit. A complete, accurate medication list is the foundation of safe care coordination.
✅
Care Team Requirements — What Must Be on File
▼

The Care Team is a living document — it must reflect who is actively involved in the patient's care at any given time. Incomplete Care Teams are a common source of communication failures and treatment gaps.

Primary Care Provider (PCP)
Required for all CCM patients
Cardiology
If patient is under cardiology care
Nephrology
If patient has kidney disease or CKD
Pain Management
If patient is being managed for chronic pain
Rheumatology
If patient has autoimmune or connective tissue disease
Endocrinology
If patient has diabetes, thyroid disease, or other endocrine conditions
Psychiatry / Psychology
If patient is under mental health care
Other Specialists
Any other provider regularly involved in the patient's care
✅
Care Coordination Checklist
▼
Progress:
0 / 10
  • ✓
    Care Team verified — all active specialists listed and up to date
  • ✓
    Recent hospitalizations or ER visits reviewed — follow-up initiated if needed
  • ✓
    Specialist consultations reviewed — pending consults tracked
  • ✓
    Outstanding referrals reviewed — status confirmed or follow-up initiated
  • ✓
    Laboratory results reviewed — abnormal results flagged and escalated
  • ✓
    Imaging results reviewed — reports in chart and provider notified of any findings
  • ✓
    Medications reconciled — active list accurate, infusion medications documented
  • ✓
    Patient education documented — care plan reviewed with patient
  • ✓
    Care plan updated with current status, goals, and any changes
  • ✓
    Significant findings communicated to PCP or relevant specialists as appropriate
🚫
Care Coordination — Common Errors to Avoid
▼
  • Missing specialists in the Care Team — an incomplete Care Team means providers who need information don't get it
  • Failing to send notes to PCPs or consulting physicians — especially after new diagnoses, treatment changes, or significant clinical events
  • Not documenting infusion medications in the active medication list — Ocrevus, Tysabri, and similar drugs must be in the chart, not just referenced in notes
  • Poor communication between providers — providers ordering conflicting treatments because they don't know what other specialists have done
  • Missing follow-up on abnormal lab or imaging results — a result that is received but not acted on is as dangerous as one that was never ordered
  • Incomplete medication reconciliation — an inaccurate medication list is a patient safety risk
  • Patients falling through the cracks after missed appointments — cancelled and no-show visits must be reviewed for outstanding clinical issues, not simply rescheduled
📊
Care Coordination Quality Indicators (KPIs)
▼
Care Team Completion Rate
% of CCM patients with a complete, up-to-date Care Team on file
Medication Reconciliation Accuracy
% of visits with complete, verified medication reconciliation documented
PCP Communication Rate
% of applicable visits where PCP received updated notes or clinical summaries
Specialist Communication Rate
% of significant clinical changes communicated to relevant specialists
Referral Completion Rate
% of ordered referrals confirmed completed and results received
Hospital Follow-Up Completion Rate
% of patients seen after a hospitalization or ER visit within the recommended timeframe
Abnormal Result Follow-Up Rate
% of abnormal lab or imaging results with documented provider notification and follow-through
Patient Satisfaction — Care Coordination
Patient-reported experience of communication, follow-through, and feeling supported between visits
🎓
Care Management Knowledge Quiz Required — All Staff
▼
📋

Care Management Knowledge Check

10 questions  ·  80% required to pass  ·  Certificate awarded on successful completion

🧠 Cognitive Assessment

Virtual Cognitive Assessment (CA) workflow, training guide, insurance coverage rules, eligible conditions, and prior authorization processes for all payers.

Brain CheckVirtual CAInsurance EligibilityAge CriteriaMMSEPrior AuthorizationMPCAvailityPA Extension
📋 Provider Recommendation Required Before Scheduling
  • Cognitive Assessment must only be offered to a patient after the provider has reviewed the case and recommended it
  • Staff should not schedule a CA or present it to the patient as required, expected, or routine without prior provider awareness and direction
  • If a patient asks about cognitive assessment and there is no provider recommendation on file, let the patient know it will be discussed with the provider and follow up — do not proceed independently
📤 Abnormal Results Must Be Routed to the Provider
  • Any abnormal cognitive assessment result must be routed to the provider promptly — the same day if possible
  • Abnormal results should not be filed without provider review
  • The provider must acknowledge the result and determine whether it should be addressed in the patient's care plan, trigger a follow-up visit, or prompt a referral
  • Document that the result was routed to the provider and when it was reviewed
🚫
Insurance Coverage Guidelines — Accepted vs. Non-Accepted Plans
▼
⚠ CRITICAL: For patients with non-accepted insurance plans, do NOT perform a full Cognitive Assessment. Administer the MMSE (paper test) only. All other insurance plans are accepted for full CA.
❌ Non-Accepted Insurance Plans for Full CA
  • Maryland Physicians Care (MPC)
  • WellPoint / Unicare
  • Johns Hopkins (all plans — EHP, Priority Partners, USFHP)
  • UnitedHealthcare (UHC)
  • Aetna Better Health MD
  • American Correctional
  • Railroad Medicare
  • Workers' Compensation (WC) — unless prior authorization for CA is obtained
  • Motor Vehicle Accident (MVA)
Action: Administer MMSE (paper test) only for these plans
✅ Accepted Plans — Full CA Permitted

All insurance plans not listed as non-accepted are eligible for full Cognitive Assessment, including:

  • Medicare (standard)
  • Most commercial/private insurance plans
  • Medicaid MD (standard managed care plans)
  • Tricare — confirm PA requirements first
  • Any plan not listed in the non-accepted column
When in doubt: Verify with billing before proceeding with full CA
🩺
Conditions Eligible for Cognitive Assessment
▼
CA is indicated if the patient presents with any one of the following conditions or complaints:
🧠 Memory-Related Issues
  • Memory changes
  • Forgetfulness / frequently misplacing items
  • Amnesia
⚡ Neurological Conditions
  • Alzheimer's disease
  • Parkinson's disease (if patient can use screen or mouse)
  • Stroke
  • Tremors (if patient can use screen or mouse)
  • Seizures / Convulsions
  • Syncope (fainting)
  • Concussion / Head injury
  • Falls
👂 Sensory Changes
  • Dizziness
  • Vertigo
  • Tinnitus (ringing in ears)
  • Hearing loss
💭 Mental Health Conditions
  • Anxiety / GAD
  • Depression / MDD
  • ADHD / ADD
  • Autism
  • Panic attacks
  • PTSD
  • OCD
  • Bipolar disorder
  • Hallucinations
  • Insomnia
  • Alcohol abuse
📋
Age-Based Criteria & Repeat Testing Guidelines
▼
Age 55 and Older

CA is recommended for all patients aged 55+, regardless of presenting conditions, unless covered by a non-accepted insurance plan.

If non-accepted insurance → MMSE only (paper)
Under Age 55

CA is performed only if the patient has one or more eligible conditions from the conditions list above. Age alone is not sufficient to qualify under 55.

Repeat Testing Schedule
✅ Previous CA Results — Normal

Repeat CA after 6–12 months

⚠ Previous CA Results — Abnormal or Possible Cognitive Impairment

Repeat CA after 3–6 months

💻
Virtual Workspace Requirements
▼
To maintain high-quality virtual assessments, all of the following must be in place before starting a CA session:
Environment
  • Proper lighting — face and workspace well illuminated
  • Neutral, professional background
  • Quiet environment with minimal distractions
Equipment
  • High-quality camera positioned at eye level
  • Noise-cancelling headphones
  • Microphone and speakers providing clear audio
  • Fast and stable internet connection
Software Access
  • Access to Brain Check website for CA administration
  • Access to patient encounter software (TalkEHR) for documentation
🗣
Patient Interaction Workflow
▼
Step 1 — Greeting & Explanation
Greet the patient and explain the purpose of the assessment using the following script:
Script:
"We will be assessing your attention, concentration, short-term memory, and long-term memory. Your current conditions and diagnoses may affect memory, and this test helps us understand how your brain is functioning."
HIPAA: Make the patient comfortable and obtain their agreement before proceeding, in accordance with HIPAA compliance requirements.
Step 2 — Brain Check Setup
  • Log in to the Brain Check website
  • Enter the patient's information accurately
  • Confirm correct patient record before starting
  • Begin the test and guide the patient through each task
🧩
Cognitive Assessment Tasks — Brain Check (Tasks 1–5)
▼
Task 1 — Immediate Recognition / Short-Term Memory
How it Works
  • Present 10 words to the patient to memorize
  • Later in the test, show words on screen one by one
  • Patient identifies which words were previously presented
What it Assesses
  • Short-term memory and immediate recall ability
Task 2 — Trail Making Test (TMT)
Part 1
  • Patient connects numbers in sequential order (1, 2, 3…)
  • Assesses attention and processing speed
Part 2
  • Patient alternates between numbers and letters (1-A, 2-B, 3-C…)
  • Assesses mental flexibility and executive function
Task 3 — Stroop Color and Word Test
How it Works
  • A word is displayed in a conflicting ink color (e.g., word "RED" printed in blue)
  • Patient matches the word itself, ignoring the ink color
  • Example: word "RED" in blue ink → patient selects "RED"
What it Assesses
  • Attention
  • Cognitive inhibition
  • Processing speed
Task 4 — Digit Symbol Substitution Test (DSST)
How it Works
  • A symbol appears at the top of the screen
  • Patient matches numbers with corresponding symbols
  • One-minute timed test
What it Assesses
  • Processing speed
  • Attention
  • Concentration
Task 5 — Delayed Recognition / Long-Term Memory
How it Works
  • Patient is asked to recall the 10 words presented at the very beginning of the test
  • Words are shown again and patient confirms which were originally presented
What it Assesses
  • Long-term memory and delayed recall
📋
Creating a Cognitive Assessment Encounter in TalkEHR
▼
⚠ Provider Selection: Always select Dr. Mir Sarim as the provider for all Cognitive Assessment encounters.
Step-by-Step Encounter Creation
Progress:
0 / 6
  • ✓
    Step 1: Click "Add Encounter" in TalkEHR
  • ✓
    Step 2: Select Encounter Type: Cognitive Assessment
  • ✓
    Step 3: Select Provider: Mir Sarim (always)
  • ✓
    Step 4: Select Location: Hagerstown or Cumberland — based on the patient's office
  • ✓
    Step 5: Complete all required fields (patient demographics, insurance, diagnosis and CPT codes, plan of care)
  • ✓
    Step 6: After test completion, receive and attach the report from Brain Check to the encounter
Employee Training Responsibilities
Trainers Must Cover
  • How to create a Cognitive Assessment encounter in TalkEHR
  • Selecting the correct CPT and ICD codes
  • Creating a Plan of Care (POC)
  • Proper patient interaction and HIPAA-compliant communication
Key CPT Codes for CA
  • 96132 — Neuropsychological testing evaluation, first hour
  • 96138 — Neuropsychological testing evaluation, each additional 30 min (technician)
Ensure both codes are billed when applicable and prior authorization is obtained for payers that require it

📝 Prior Authorization — CA Payer-Specific Workflows

Expand a payer below for step-by-step prior authorization instructions, contact numbers, and fax submission details.

🏥
Maryland Physicians Care (MPC) — Prior Authorization Process
▼
📠 Submit PA via Fax

800-953-8856

MPC Prior Authorization fax line

📞 PA Status Inquiries

800-953-8854

MPC Provider Services — for status checks

📞 Peer-to-Peer Request

410-424-4480

Use when a peer-to-peer review is needed after denial

Step-by-Step PA Process — Interactive Checklist
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Step 1 — Extraction: Review and extract all required patient information — demographics, insurance details, ICD-10 diagnosis codes, requested procedure, and supporting clinical documentation (recent office notes, imaging reports, treatment history)
  • ✓
    Step 2 — Initiation: Complete the MPC Prior Authorization Request Form and submit it with the patient's most recent clinical records to MPC via fax: 800-953-8856
  • ✓
    Step 3 — Questionnaire: If MPC requests additional information, complete the questionnaire received from the insurance company, attach requested clinical documentation, and fax it back for review
  • ✓
    Step 4 — Review & Determination: MPC reviews submitted records against medical necessity criteria. Decision (approval, denial, or request for more info) received within up to 14 business days. For status: call Provider Services at 800-953-8854
Step 1 — Extraction: What to Gather
Patient & Insurance Information
  • Full patient demographics (name, DOB, address, member ID)
  • Insurance details and payer information
  • ICD-10 diagnosis codes relevant to the requested service
  • Requested procedure / CPT codes
Supporting Clinical Documentation
  • Most recent office visit note (signed)
  • Imaging reports (MRI, CT, X-ray)
  • Lab results if applicable
  • Treatment history relevant to the requested service
  • Any prior PA numbers if reauthorizing
Step 2 — PA Form & Submission
MPC PA Request Form

Download, complete, and fax with supporting documents.

⬇️ Download MPC PA Form
Fax Submission Checklist
  • Completed MPC PA Request Form
  • Most recent signed office visit note
  • Relevant imaging or lab reports
  • Insurance/demographics information
  • Fax to: 800-953-8856
  • Keep fax confirmation as proof of submission
Step 3 — Questionnaire Response
⚠️If MPC sends back a questionnaire requesting additional clinical information, respond promptly. Delays in returning the questionnaire extend the determination timeline. Attach all requested documentation and fax to 800-953-8856.
Step 4 — Determination & Follow-Up
✅ Approved
  • Record the authorization number in the patient's chart
  • Note the authorization effective dates and approved codes
  • Schedule or proceed with the service
❌ Denied
  • Review the denial reason
  • Determine if appeal or peer-to-peer is appropriate
  • For peer-to-peer: call 410-424-4480
  • Document all denial and appeal activity in the chart
⏳ Pending / More Info Needed
  • Provide requested additional documentation promptly
  • For status updates call: 800-953-8854
  • Timeline: up to 14 business days from submission
🏛
Medicaid MD — Prior Authorization Process
▼
Initiation Call
800-888-1965
Call to create authorization request
Questionnaire Fax
888-586-1343
Fax completed questionnaire + clinical records
Step-by-Step PA Process — Interactive Checklist
Progress:
0 / 4
  • ✓
    Step 1 — Extraction: Review chart and extract all required information — patient demographics, insurance details, ICD-10 diagnosis codes, requested procedure/service, and supporting clinical documentation (recent office notes, imaging reports, treatment history)
  • ✓
    Step 2 — Initiation: Call Medicaid MD at 800-888-1965. Provide patient demographics, requested service, diagnosis codes, and any other information required to create the authorization request in the system
  • ✓
    Step 3 — Questionnaire & Clinical Submission: Complete the questionnaire received from Medicaid MD. Attach all requested clinical records (office notes, imaging, labs, treatment history) and fax the complete packet to 888-586-1343
  • ✓
    Step 4 — Follow-Up & Determination: Monitor authorization status and respond promptly to any additional information requests. Final decision will be approval, denial, or request for additional information. Document all activity in the patient chart
Step 1 — Extraction: What to Gather
Patient & Insurance Information
  • Full patient demographics (name, DOB, address, member ID)
  • Medicaid MD insurance ID and group number
  • Primary care physician name and NPI
  • Referring provider information
Clinical Documentation
  • ICD-10 diagnosis codes (primary + secondary)
  • CPT/procedure code being requested
  • Most recent office note (within 90 days)
  • Supporting imaging, labs, or specialist reports
  • Prior treatment history demonstrating medical necessity
Step 2 — Initiation: What to Have Ready Before Calling
📞 Have all of the following available when calling 800-888-1965:
Patient name, date of birth, Medicaid ID number, ICD-10 codes, CPT code(s) being requested, treating provider NPI, and a brief clinical summary of medical necessity. The representative will create the authorization request and may assign a reference/tracking number — record this number immediately.
Step 3 — Questionnaire & Fax Submission
Completing the Questionnaire
  • Answer all clinical questions thoroughly and accurately
  • Reference specific chart entries when describing treatment history
  • Include dates of prior treatments and their outcomes
  • Document why alternative treatments were insufficient
Fax Packet Checklist
  • Completed questionnaire (all sections filled)
  • Most recent office/progress note
  • Relevant imaging reports or lab results
  • Any specialist consultation notes
  • Cover sheet with patient name, Medicaid ID, and reference number
  • Fax to: 888-586-1343
Step 4 — Follow-Up & Determination
⏱ Turnaround Time: Standard turnaround is 14 business days from the date of submission. If reinitiation is required after expiration, the full 14-business-day timeline restarts. Always document the submission date and expected decision date in the patient chart.
✅ Approved
  • Document approval and authorization number in chart
  • Note approved service(s), quantity, and validity dates
  • Communicate approval to scheduling and clinical team
❌ Denied
  • Review denial reason carefully
  • Determine if appeal or additional clinical information is appropriate
  • Document all denial and appeal activity in the chart
  • Escalate to provider if peer-to-peer review is needed
⏳ Pending / More Info
  • Provide requested additional documentation promptly
  • Re-fax to 888-586-1343 with new cover sheet
  • Follow up by phone at 800-888-1965
  • Track all submissions and response dates
🎖
Tricare — Prior Authorization Process
▼
🖥 Portal-Based Submission: All Tricare prior authorization requests are submitted through the Humana Military provider portal. Do not call or fax unless the portal is unavailable or the rep instructs you to.
Process Overview
How to Submit
  • Log in to the Humana Military provider portal
  • Search for the patient using member ID or name + DOB
  • Initiate a new PA request and enter diagnosis (ICD-10) and procedure (CPT) codes
  • Attach supporting clinical documentation (office notes, imaging, treatment history)
  • Submit and record the reference/tracking number
What to Have Ready
  • Patient demographics and Tricare member ID
  • DEERS enrollment confirmation (patient must be DEERS-eligible)
  • Ordering provider NPI and practice information
  • ICD-10 primary and secondary diagnosis codes
  • CPT code(s) being requested
  • Most recent clinical notes within 90 days
Questionnaire & Follow-Up

If Humana Military requests additional information, complete the questionnaire by reviewing the patient's most recent office visit notes and extracting the relevant clinical information. Re-upload or fax the completed questionnaire with supporting records per the portal's instructions. Monitor the portal for the final determination and document the outcome in the patient's chart.

⚕
Wellpoint / Unicare — Prior Authorization Process
▼
Submission Platform
Availity ICR
Most Wellpoint plans use Availity for PA submission
Status Check
800-444-5445
Call to check authorization status
📝 Note: Some Medicaid MD plans may be managed through Wellpoint/Unicare. If the patient's Medicaid plan routes through Wellpoint or Unicare, use the Availity ICR portal for submission and call 800-444-5445 for status inquiries.
Submission Process
Steps to Submit via Availity
  • Log in to Availity and navigate to Patient Registration → Auth/Referrals
  • Search patient by member ID or name + DOB
  • Select the appropriate payer (Wellpoint/Unicare/Anthem)
  • Enter requesting provider, facility, diagnosis codes, and CPT codes
  • Attach clinical documentation and submit
  • Record the authorization reference number
Questionnaire Process
  • If a questionnaire is received, review the patient's most recent office visit notes
  • Extract the relevant clinical information required by the insurance company
  • Complete all questionnaire fields thoroughly
  • Attach supporting medical records
  • Submit the completed packet per the instructions on the questionnaire (portal re-upload or fax)
🏥
Johns Hopkins EHP & Priority Partners — Prior Authorization Process
▼
Submission Platform
Availity
Used for PA submission for EHP and Priority Partners
Peer-to-Peer / Callback
410-424-4480
Status inquiries and peer-to-peer requests
Process Overview
PA Submission via Availity
  • Log in to Availity and navigate to Patient Registration → Auth/Referrals
  • Select Johns Hopkins EHP or Priority Partners as the payer
  • Enter diagnosis codes, CPT codes, ordering provider, and clinical details
  • Attach the most recent office notes and supporting documentation
  • Submit and record the tracking number
Questionnaire & Follow-Up
  • Johns Hopkins EHP and Priority Partners do send questionnaires and additional information requests
  • Review patient's most recent office visit and extract relevant clinical information
  • Complete questionnaire and attach supporting records
  • Submit per the questionnaire instructions
  • For status or peer-to-peer: call 410-424-4480
🎖
Johns Hopkins USFHP — Prior Authorization Process
▼
Callback Number
410-424-2603
Status inquiries and follow-up calls
Submission Method
Paper Form + Fax
Complete USFHP form and fax with clinical records
📋 Submission Method: USFHP does not use Availity for PA submission. Complete the Johns Hopkins USFHP Prior Authorization Request Form and fax it along with the patient's clinical records to the number indicated on the form.
Process Overview
Steps to Submit
  • Download and complete the USFHP PA Request Form (link below)
  • Fill in all patient demographics, diagnosis codes, and CPT codes
  • Attach the most recent office note and supporting clinical documentation
  • Fax the complete packet to the number indicated on the form
  • Record the fax confirmation and document the submission date in the chart
Questionnaire Process
  • Questionnaire process is the same as other payors
  • Review patient's most recent visit notes and extract relevant clinical information
  • Complete all fields on the questionnaire thoroughly
  • Attach supporting medical records and fax back per instructions
  • For status inquiries: call 410-424-2603
